First question on this one, do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Umm.
Oh, this is a yes and no, isn't it?

Interviewer - This is a better than usual, no worse than usual. Every single question is using that set of answers. So for the last month, compared to your baseline, do you have problems with tiredness? Better than usual, no worse than usual, worse or much worse.

Participant - Oh.
OK. All right.
Right. Sorry about the ambulance.

Interviewer - No problem.

Participant - I'm sorry I'm so stupid. Could you just repeat the question?

Interviewer - Yeah, it's. I, I-I have this a lot with this one. This is the least friendly to brain flog-brain fog.

Participant - Frog?

Interviewer - Frog. Do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Erm...worse than usual, I think.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, but not much worse, to clarify.

Participant - Yeah, it's a bit worse than it is usually.

Interviewer - OK.
Do you need to rest more?

Participant - Um.
Yeah, much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse.
Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Worse than usual, I think.

Interviewer - Worse?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse.
Do you lack energy?

Participant - Yeah, much, much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Probably no worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse.
Typing left-handed with a cup of tea in my hand is difficult.
Sorry about that.
Do you feel weak?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse.
Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse.
Do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - Oh yeah, much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse, almost like flogging. Do you find it difficult-Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - Yeah, definitely worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - How is your memory?

Participant - Yeah, also worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - I forgot what disabilities I had at the start.

Interviewer - Hmm.
Yeah, not-not uncommon I'll be honest. Right. OK, so that is that done? That's all of your weird wordy surveys done. Which is, to be blunt, I find the most difficult bit. So that's... lovely and lovely.
Demographics, fill in the thing, right, main body of the interview. I am going to ask before the main interview. Have you anything like a an activity diary you'd like to submit it all? It's not-not mandatory, purely optional.

Participant - Umm.
I do not. Also again ambulances.

Interviewer - OK, yeah, no problem. I-I only hear...I didn't hear the first one. I heard a very small tail end of that one, I genuinely thought it was a bird.

Participant - Oh, OK to me...it's so loud because the main road is there. Like, like if I point that direction, I'm pointing at the [landmark].

Interviewer - Yeah, yeah.
Yeah. No, it's.

Participant - In that in that vague direction...[landmark], yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah, that that road does get a bit busy, right? Where's...?
There it is, right. OK. So for these ones, there's no particular format to the answer. You can not answer, if you want, you can ramble on for half an hour for a single question if you want, no pressure with time or format.
And by all means, feel free to circle back to something there, because there are some questions I will only ask if you mentioned something earlier. So we might jump around a bit if something comes up.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - OK. Question number one. What chronic mobility condition or conditions do you have?

Participant - So I have joint hypermobility. I have a unnamed congenital calf condition. It's not necessarily mobility, but it can affect it sometimes. My irritable bowel syndrome will cause...cramps that are quite painful that can cause a lot of muscle tightness at the top of my thighs as well, so that can also be...you know, it impacts and then the-the amount of energy going into these conditions also, you know, knocks me out a bit.

Interviewer - Sorry, that congenital condition. Can you be a little more specific if you can?

Participant - Yeah, it doesn't help the doctors are always vague.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So I'll have I'll just speed run...I'll speedrun it. Oh, yeah, I also hav-.

Interviewer - Just to note I will, I will ask about symptoms in a second.

Participant - Oh.

Interviewer - So if it's list of symptoms, save it for that. But more in one word it is because it's a congenital. Was it a calf condition?

Participant - Calf, yeah.

Interviewer - For you so legs.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Is that blood flow or muscles or...?

Participant - The.
The muscles are too short for my body and that causes a lot of tightness and pain.

Interviewer - OK.
OK, right. Thank you very much. And you went, sorry you went to mention something and I cut you off there, sorry.

Participant - No, it's OK. It was that that I was going to mention, so don't worry about it.

Interviewer - OK, perfect.
Um, can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - So with the calf condition, if I overexert myself or sometimes randomly, my calf muscle will become very tight, very tense. This will cause a lot of pain.
So it means walking anywhere will be...erm...difficult. And then also really quite exerting which will cause me to feel quite, you know, fatigued, low energy. The joint hypermobility as well, because my joints are more flexible, having to exert a lot of energy into keeping them relatively straight. If-if that makes sense. So if I'm wearing shoes that don't have ankle support, I'm having to put a lot of energy into making sure my ankles aren't like doing this. (wobbling motion)
Then with the irritable bowel syndrome, if I'm having a flare up that will cause a lot of abdominal pain and that abdominal-abdominal pain can travel into the tops of the legs.
And then that can cause similar sort of issues of walking, mobility and again, the amount of energy the body is having to exert trying to correct that, that if that makes sense.
It-it, it just causes a lot of energy usage I suppose.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you from being able to do. Not just stuff that you used to do, but stuff that you want to do but...and-and have never done but can't.

Participant - So in terms of like exercise, so like walking, I'm able to walk for an amount. You know, I'm able to walk around a city, but I will be in...a lot of pain. So the ability to travel distances on foot that can be affected. If I'm wanting to do exercise like, you know, like on purpose rather than just walking somewhere as in.
A concerted effort to do exercise cycling I can manage as long as I'm on the flat. The minute I have to cycle anywhere that has hills, I'm out, I can't do it. If I'm.
I can't use running machines at a gym.
The uh...actually I-I can use them for like let's say 5 minutes 5-10 minutes. But because of my calf condition it forces my feet into tiptoe position and so when I run, I run on my tip toes and then what that then does is puts a huge strain, which makes me just sort of crash from the fatigue. It's not something that you can keep up, and because I'm not physically able to run at any different way that would save energy, it kind of...
You know, it cut short the amount of time I'd be able to do, you know, running on like a running machine. Other stuff that it prevents me from doing.
Sorry, brains having a bit of a minute.

Interviewer - No problem. Take your time.

Participant - Yeah, I mean it, it just also stopped me from, you know.
Like other-other people my age who are not, you know, who don't have disabilities, if they-they would think nothing of, you know, several miles long walk to get somewhere and...yeah. Even though I feel like the stuff that I can technically do, but I'm able to do it only if I'm in a lot of pain and just kind of ignore it. I don't know how to phrase it. Sorry.

Interviewer - Well.
Yep, that's no problem. Is there anything else you'd like to add?

Participant - I'm sure there's some kind of activity that I'm forgetting. Again, it's the cake of forgetting every activity I've ever done.

Interviewer - At-at risk of leading you, were you not looking into archery?

Participant - Good point, yeah.

Interviewer - Because that is, that is also a walk away from you as well.

Participant - Yes, that's a good point.

Interviewer - There's at least two different ways that I can personally think of. But if you can think of any other ways to expand on it, then by all means do.

Participant - Yeah. So basically any activity that involves walking up to the Uni or you know regularly walking into town will be difficult for me.
Yeah, especially with anything that happens at the Uni. So any society that would include archery, that would include any music based societies that meet up there, etcetera.
I'm kind of like prohibited from going to places regularly unless there were alternative like venues. Like if there was in the town, sometimes wouldn't be as bad. But if there's activities that exclusively happen on campus, I kind of can't go to them.

Interviewer - Yeah, because I'm... to provide information without needing it to be redacted. That particular university is up a hill, is it not. Quite a, quite a steep hill that takes a good 15...10-15 minutes for a normal person to walk up.

Participant - Yeah, and...The steep hill to get to university from where I am...erm...that steep hill is at minimum a 30 minute walk, and if I'm having to walk up it, I have to take regular breaks and will always be in pain. There is no, there is no way to walk up the hill without being in pain.

Interviewer - Yeah. So I've just sent you a message relevant to the archery thing with a clarification if you want to look at later that because that name's a specific location. So on-on the hill. Yeah, the-the even worse context.
Most of the town has the hill, but it's-it's still steep, but quite a lot shallower gradient than where you are. So you-you would be a much steeper hill isn't-isn't that correct?

Participant - Yeah, that's-there's more or less two hills, as it were in the town. And I live near the worst one, yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. Or-or more like two sides of the same hill. And one side is a lot, lot steeper. It is, it is nicknamed Cardiac Hill for a reason.

Participant - Yes.
It is.
Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Is there anything else specific?

Participant - My GP practice is also located on the very steep hill, so I also have accessibility issues with being able to actually access healthcare because I do not own a car, I have to walk there. And I, you know, have fatigue when walking it kind of..

Interviewer - Yeah. And-and again for-for the sake of context of the recording, there is no direct bus. You would have to either walk into town on the flat and then get a bus up the hill or get a bus from where you are to town and then change buses and go up the hill, correct?

Participant - Yeah. Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Anything else you'd like to add?

Participant - Think that's about that for that question.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - So...

Interviewer - Obviously with a congenital condition, by all means clarify that modifier, because that's not a normal modifier that we can...we considered when writing.

Participant - So...
I suppose it was always there, but when I first started noticing slightly different things.
So when I...can't even remember how old I was, let me just think.
I must have been about four, maybe four-ish when...I didn't. Yeah, when other children were walking, I was not walking. And the reason why was because of the calf condition.
Also dyspraxia, but that's like, you know the-the...sorry, the dyspraxia does not cause the fatigue, but it does. It doesn't help.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - But for both the congenital calf condition and the dyspraxia, I had to have physical therapy from when I was very young. So before primary school age, about four years old. And so that's when I became aware, because I had to go to the doctor, see specialists about it and...I would have to be. I have to wear special boots to school. When I started primary school.
This...yeah, this would, you know, cause pain because it just it would force my feet to be in a more normal position. And so that would also exacerbate the existing fatigue caused.

Interviewer - Mmmm.

Participant - Yeah, the you know, there's a there's a baseline that the condition caused and then there's the sort of cosmetic modifiers to make me walk more normally that added a bit on top.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - If that's useful to have or know.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
OK, so this isn't meant to be a question, I'm only going to bring up with mentioned, but I think this is relevant, reading between the lines. Extended exhaustion AKA post exertional malaise, kind of thing, relevant to you or not.

Participant - Yes, I think so.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity?

Participant - So...
For example, one recent one happened on the 31st of July this year when I was visiting a large city. I'm pretty sure you know the city and...What happened was I had to travel to several different places within this city and...the exertion really sort of caught up to me, especially because I needed a phone charger this...led to me having to go on a quest to go and find a phone charger. Buy one.
What ended up happening is when I finally arrived home after that exertion, I more or less was unable to leave the House and do most tasks after that. So after exertion I will be...more or less bed bound, only getting up if I have to eat or use the bathroom.
This this has also happened...For example, where I live here, having to walk quite a long distance over several hills. Upon returning home, I am immediately crashing into bed. I am not getting up for several hours.

Interviewer - Cool.

Participant - I hope I'm describing things in a way that's helpful, and I know that I can be vague.

Interviewer - Even the way that you were describing it is useful. Also, there's-there's no expectation of describing things in a specific way, because even if you say that there is a problem but you're struggling to describe it, that's a symptom.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Like exhibiting symptoms in the interview is more than OK and won't...
I'm exhibiting a symptom I can't think of the right word, obstruct? Not quite what I mean, but I think you know what I mean. The-the study. So like, not being able to describe things in a purely academic way won't...make what you're providing less valuable.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, because like doctors hate me because I am kind of vague. They'll be like, how does this affect you? And I'll describe it. But what they're looking for something specific. So I'm glad that's not the case here.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yeah, they're, they're-they're looking for a specific phrase from their textbook and you're unable to think of that specific word and also unable to think of putting it that specific way and they're trying to draw it out of you and it's just not happening.

Participant - And it won't, and it won't click for them. If I don't say the code word basically.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yeah.
That-that is not the case with this. So yeah, feel free to describe things in the way that is most comfortable for you.
OK question 6, can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting activities?

Participant - So.
I mean, even just whilst talking, you know, I-I also have the thing where I'll either, you know, pause for a really long time or I'll be trying to think of the right, the right word, that just won't come to me. It'll impact any academic work. So if I'm trying to describe a concept or a bit of...research...
What will end up happening is I'll get stuck. You know, the stumbling point will be my inability to think of the either the content I want to write about or the, or a phrasing that I want to use.
Even if it's a topic that I know that I know really, really well, like my dissertation topic.
Even though I should know this upside down and backwards, my brain can sometimes just short out and it's-it's as if I don't know anything, even though I do.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
Umm...
OK. So again like question five, I'm reading between the lines here...sleep schedule. Is it affected and if it is, does it exacerbate your symptoms or do your symptoms cause sleep schedule disruptions?
That's sleep schedule disruptions.

Participant - Yes, I'd say it does impact sleep.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - So if I've had like a exhaustion crash like I've described, if I've been out walking for a long time, unable to sit down and rest, come home, bed immediately.
There's two ways this will affect me. The 1st way is if I end up crashing into bed and I'm not normally a person who takes naps, but sometimes my body will forcibly make me take a nap.
If this happens, I won't be able to then sleep at night.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - The 2nd way is if I'm in too much physical pain to sleep.
So I will be-I could feel really tired. I'll be so fatigued mentally I am, will just be just...Unable to do anything more taxing than 1 + 1 = 2. However...I might be prevented from sleeping because of physical pain. I also used to have restless leg syndrome, which still have on and off. But as a teenager it was really bad, which was a symptom of the...disabilities as well. So if I'm in pain, I might move my legs a little bit to ease the pain, which then in turn prevents me from sleep because I'm moving, if that makes sense.

Interviewer - I've not heard of that symptom before. OK, that's really interesting.

Participant - Oh.
I can type it if you want just.

Interviewer - No, it will be transcribed and-and put in written form, but by all means don't tax yourself extra, needlessly.

Participant - But yeah-

Interviewer - That's the-the last thing I want with participants of a study like this is for people to go to extra work.

Participant - Yes. Yeah.
But yeah, restless leg syndrome. It's relatively common as a condition that affects sleep, but there's lots of different things that can cause it, and it can go into remission and then just come back out of nowhere. So yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. OK. So question 7, can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse. If you don't, can't describe it more than you already have then, by all means, just say that.

Participant - So if I'm like unable to sleep, obviously that causes more tension in my body because you know, that's what sleep does to a sleep deprived person. That tension...If I'm like, holding myself in a way that might cause cramps.
That can then, you know, as a knock on of the sleep that can then sort of trigger my disabilities to start, you know, flaring up.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - So if I'm really tired and I'm like sort of holding your attention in my legs that can then spark my calves to start going off and doing their thing. And if I'm then trying to go on with my day, I am just going to be...You know, dredge day I think is the best way to put it. I'm just going to know that whatever I do that day because of the sleep deprivation, my disabilities are just going to be having a free for all.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right on to more device focused questions now. What device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - So I use on my phone a separate app that I downloaded which is called Activity Tracker.
And I used it well, I've been using it for just about a month, I think.

Interviewer - OK. Have you used any previous tools of any kind before? So this includes diet trackers, sleep trackers, not just fitness trackers.
Oh, and also the devices that remind you to drink water and track water consumption.

Participant - Oh yeah, I used to use one of these around about October of last year.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Probably a couple of months then, but I stopped using that one.

Interviewer - OK, if you want to chip in with anything about that in addition to your current device, feel free.

Participant - Yeah. So that that specific app was had a lot of different self-care reminders. Your drinking water was included in, for example, reminders to rest, that sort of thing.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - And...I used that just...For a brief period, though, so it's not something I've continued. I'm not sure if it's useful.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. I'm just gonna put this on the table over there.
Right. Hello again. OK, question 9, how do you use this device or application?

Participant - So because it's a pedometer, it's obviously it's tracking the amount of steps that I do and I mainly use it to try and estimate...one how far it is in terms of steps between places that I need to go regularly.
And two to figure out if I'm having fatigue and you know a lot of symptoms are happening, finding out what distance or how many steps has caused that. Since it also measures distance as well. So it'll say you've done this many kilometres.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. When do you use the device or application?

Participant - So it's on like all the time, so it counts my steps more or less all the time, but I tend to use it if I've been out all day and I've gotten home. I'll check how many steps that was to see...Um, if I'm feeling fatigued...Figure out what kind of ballpark figure it is that I get fatigued at, so that I kind of know for future.

Interviewer - OK. And this might be a little bit of a repeat. How frequently do you use the device or application?

Participant - So if I'm checking it, yeah, on purpose, it'll just be at the end of some kind of exertion, whether it's like walking or if I've done exercise on purpose.

Interviewer - OK, so I think it's fair to say that this is outside of the manufacturer scope because I'm guessing this is an application that wants you to walk more to become fitter?

Participant - Yeah, it has a has a calorie tracker which I don't use at all...like I think it has like it has the data there, but I just don't look at it. It's not relevant to what I'm using it for.

Interviewer - OK, is, is, are there any other ways that you would say you were using the device outside of manufacturer scope?

Participant - Umm...Let me just think a minute.
So yeah, the-the main function of the app is to track like you have a goal that you set so you have people setting they want to do so many steps in a day. I've got it set to 7-7000 at the minute.
But it's not that that I'm particularly sort of focused on, I suppose, like it's nice if I met that target, but I'm not using the app to meet a target, I suppose.

Interviewer - OK.
Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application?

Participant - So just sort of walking to different locations in town. Seeing how many steps it takes me to get from my house to, you know, places that I go regularly.
What else?
Oh yeah, and also just to see if the actual effects of physical exercise.
Like, I'm not sure how to phrase it.

Interviewer - Take your time.

Participant - Yeah, just sort of seeing how...if I am doing exercise on purpose a bit more seeing that, how that then affects me, I suppose is the main-main kind of draw as well.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - So that I don't overdo it, I guess.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the device or application alerted you to over exertion?

Participant - So...
As I'm sort of figuring out where-where my baseline is, what my limits are, if I've walked a lot in a day and I come home and sort of check the app and I find that I've done maybe double or triple the amount of steps, that's my limit.
You know, I can then kind of have a clear picture in my head of, I have really overdone it. I, that then sort of tells me I need to go and do some self-care because, you know, I-I-I should have...I should have done some self-care...You know, maybe half this amount of steps ago.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device or application?

Participant - So...I mean, I've started cycling more because I have acquired a bike so...
Because I've-I've now got a bike that helps me get into town a bit faster, it's now using the app to see as well what kind of effects the different kind of physical activity has, rather than just walking, which is my predominant...physical activity before.

Interviewer - OK. Quick question on that. Are you using a cycling mode in the application at all?

Participant - The-I don't think the app actually has one, so I suppose it's maybe not as useful if I'm just winging it as it were.

Interviewer - So you you're hoping that it's recording distance travelled or interpreting your cycling as steps in a way that's comparable then I suppose.

Participant - Yeah, because the-the term step as well, it's kind of, because it's a phone, it's maybe not as accurate as a-a purpose built device. So if your phone is in your bag and it's jostling around, that might count steps by accident. So it's not...as precise, but it does also record distance, so there is that there is that usefulness for distance.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Just a quick question on the particulars of that app. Do you know if it records distance using GPS or something or if it's using distance by or if it's estimating distance by exten-extruding out from the number of steps.

Participant - I think it's from the...amount of steps I think. Because...because when I actually downloaded the app, one of the reasons I downloaded it is because it has minimal data gathering and if I remember correctly it sort of asks you at the start how big your typical stride is.

Interviewer - Yes.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device or application?

Participant - I've not really stopped doing anything other than walking too far. I-I don't know if that counts.

Interviewer - Umm...OK. So would you say...Hmm.
OK. So can you give me examples of things that you have put off or changes in your prioritisation as a result of using the device or application?

Participant - Actually prioritisation is a good way to put it, because if I know the distance that I need to travel and I know that going that far on foot will be too exerting for me and then much more likely than I used to be since having the app, to consider if I could try and find a different way. So if there's a bus that I can take or if I can do part of the route...if there's different terrain that I can kind of...You know, find a different way rather than defaulting to my usual...You know path, I suppose. And the app would help with that because it's, it does give me a way to sort of actually think in terms of numbers rather than just guesstimating.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device or application?

Participant - I don't think...
There's anything that I was...
Although...
Might-might just need a minute just to think.

Interviewer - No problem. Take your time.

Participant - Hmm.
I don't think I was thinking of starting at anything that would be significant enough if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Is, well, is there anything that would fall into that category of not significant enough that you were thinking of starting that you haven't?

Participant - Like I, I've been thinking of like...outside of like the university as well-being being up the hill, you know, sort of activities that I could do there. Outside of that, ummm...You know there's other sorts of like...Sports that I've been thinking about that I've...not done, that I've been, you know, too...There's too much exertion that...You know, I-I used to like about two years ago. I used to live in a location in the town near one of the two bowling clubs. And I, you know, I attended that Bowling Club for a while and I live near a different one now and I was thinking of going to that but...I have to walk about 15 minutes to get there and then the exertion for sort of the bending down and the...
Activity involved, I think the...Amount of energy that I would need to do that regularly, that specific activity, I've kind of gone "Nah". not to like...That's the best way I can put it. The thought process was "Nah".

Interviewer - OK.
To specify that's lawn bowls, garden bowls, correct.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Sort of. The English version of Petanque.

Participant - Basically, yeah.

Interviewer - OK.
So...that involves a lot of standing, doesn't it? If I'm correct?

Participant - Yeah, there's not generally an ability to sit very much even though the demographics for bowling skews older because of the...because of the way that things are set up, you kind of need to be stood up to be able to gauge the distances.
Compared to a few other sports where you might be able to sit down like 10 pin bowling for example, you could absolutely sit down.

Interviewer - Yeah. Yeah. OK. Thanks very much. Almost at the end. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device or application?

Participant - Events?

Interviewer - Mhmm.
Things that vindicate your choice to put energy into using it.

Participant - I'm not exactly sure how you mean. Could you like rephrase it a little bit?

Interviewer - So an occurrence where something has happened and you've-you've decided this has gone better because I'm using the device than if I wasn't using the device.

Participant - Alright, OK.

Interviewer - So a circumstance where you feel justified in using the device because it's better for you as a result that you have been using it.

Participant - I suppose um...Sort of going on like my usual sort of walk. And if I've stops to check my phone and have a look at the app and I've noticed that I've stayed well within my limits and not overexerted myself, I've kind of then gone "This is useful to have".
Because if I didn't have this, if I wasn't...If I wasn't able to check mid exercise or mid activity, I would likely you know, against my better judgement carry on and then end up overdoing it. So I suppose that would be it.

Interviewer - So it prevents that feeling of "No, I've got to push through and get to the end of this".

Participant - Yeah, I've got a lot more...I've got the I've got more ability to kind of go.
"Yes, I want to continue" or "No, I don't want to continue" depending on...
You know where I'm at when I check the app.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Yeah. Sorry. I know that's quite a wordy and perhaps incuse question.
Right. Three final questions. These are more speculative. What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - So...
Like an ideal sort of device or application.
Possibly something that's a little bit more accurate than a sort of generic phone app, so we'll probably be talking a wearable device of some description. I would also like...Yeah, I would also say things like things like heart rate would be useful to monitor. Steps, distance travelled, options for alternative modes of transport that are physical, so...riding a bike, walking, that kind of thing.
Or skateboarding if-if somebody skateboarded I guess. What else? Perhaps a specific piece of coding that if you sort of input, if I go beyond this amount of steps or this amount of distance, I will get fatigued, some kind of alert that goes..."You're nearing your limit. Do you want to continue, or do you want to go back home?" That's that sort of thing, that sort of automates the that process, because it's easy to get wrapped up in something and then forget.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Yeah.
I think that's about.
Everything that I'd put.

Interviewer - OK, you have unintentionally answered the next two questions. What functions would you add and what functions would you remove?

Participant - Oh.
Oops.

Interviewer - No, that's perfectly fine. I will specify the current question more.
Perhaps it should be reworded. What physical form would your ideal device or application take,so you-you mentioned a wearable? Is there a particular form of wearable or more than one or something that isn't on the market yet that you've-you've had an idea about?

Participant - I mean, I suppose.
The default is like a wrist wearable, though I don't know if like there's...Depending on if somebody wanted more...You know in-depth data. I know there's, like, certain disabilities where you might need to monitor your heart, for example, or even things like insulin. If there was, like, a device that could also have...Ways to...Shake hands of other people's disability devices, I suppose, would also be like a nice feature. I know I'm doing it again. I f*** sorry I'm swearing.

Interviewer - No, no, it's perfectly fine. I'm-I'm going to be redacting this all anyway.

Participant - Please, please redact the swearing. I did not mean it's just very hot today.

Interviewer - No, it's no problem, it's no problem.
Yeah. OK. So...
Can you elaborate on that handshake feature a little bit? So is this is this multiple devices from different people interacting with each other?

Participant - No, I mean more like if your fatigue or disabilities, you know, if you have a certain condition that requires you to monitor something else, for example, blood pressure, insulin, heart rate. I'm thinking about my friend [name], who had a device that [they] wore...That was constantly monitoring [their] insulin. If the device had a...You know, had a sort of function or feature that allowed that to connect to that device.
So like for example with a...Like a, an insulin monitor...If the, if the device was, let's say, worn on the wrist...If that was then able to get the data from the insulin monitor and then also display that for sort of ease of use, I suppose on the specific device, although I could be getting ahead of myself here.

Interviewer - So you were talking about a system of devices like, almost like mobile watches interacting with the mobile phone as-as a pair.

Participant - Yeah, a sort of linking of.

Interviewer - OK, OK. But allall for a single user, yeah.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much.
I think there was a little clashing of terminology there, so thank you for clarifying. Yeah, thank you very much. I think that is everything on my end, unless there's something you want to add or circle back to...because the start was focused on condition description-
Condition descriptions and symptoms. The middle was about the device.
The tail was the combination of the two and how we're using one affects the other. And then you had that little speculative bit at the end. So if there's anything you'd like to-to add to any of these, go ahead.

Participant - I think I've been quite comprehensive, sorry if I got a bit sci-fi at the end of that.

Interviewer - No problem, no problem at all. Right. I'm going to finish the recording then.

title: P04 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - There we go. Right first survey. Do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Do you need to rest more?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - Paradoxical, paradoxically, better than usual.

Interviewer - We love a terrible baseline. Do you lack energy?

Participant - Umm.
About the same as usual.

Interviewer - So no worse?

Participant - No worse.

Interviewer - Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse. Do you feel weak?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - Oh.
About the same as usual, no worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse. Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse.
And how is your memory?

Participant - Worse than usual? Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.
OK, right. So that's survey number 1.
Two more.
Neither of the next two use words. They use number scales.

Participant - Yeah.
Love a number.

Interviewer - So...
This is in the last week I think.

Participant - Last week.
Last week.

Interviewer - Yes, it is. It's in big bold letters. I wish I could read, OK.

Participant - Me too.

Interviewer - So the scale for the first one is from numbers 1 to 7. Number 1 is a strong disagreement. 7 is a strong agreement. 4 is neutral.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So.
If, if you were to be asked if you were fully able, it would probably be a 1.

Participant -  So one is full agreement, no full disagreement.

Interviewer - As like a full disagreement.

Participant - Yeah. OK.

Interviewer - So it's like how much you-you agree or like the thing?

Participant - With 4 being neutral...

Interviewer - So yes, so...
During the past week I have found that my motivation is lower when I am fatigued.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7.
Exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - I don't think I've exercised in the last week so 4.

Interviewer - 4.
I am easily fatigued.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6.
Fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Right question 5. Fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7. Fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7. Fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7. And-

Participant - Oh, hang on. Yeah. No, we're sticking to seven.

Interviewer - Yep, happy?

Participant - I just remembered the disabilities, but I've considered them now. Still 7.

Interviewer - OK, good good. Fatigue interferes with my work family or social life.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Thank you very much.
OK, so this next one is it's called the Epworth Sleepiness Scale, which is a lovely name.

Participant - That is.

Interviewer - Each item is rated from 0 to 3 with 0, meaning you would never doze or fall asl-doze or fall asleep in the given situation.
1 meaning that you have a slight chance of dozing, 2 meaning you have a moderate chance and 3 meaning there is a very high chance that you would dose or fall asleep.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So how likely are you to doze or fall asleep in contrast to just feeling tired?
Umm.

Interviewer - Even if you haven't done any of these activities recently, think about how they would have affected you.
This is quite a relatively old survey. Some of these activities are quite amusing and especially the way that they're put, so please keep in mind I did not write this.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So yes, this is like a general thing. It's not over a week or a month. It's a general vibe check, I guess.

Participant - Gotcha.

Interviewer - Right situation #1, sitting and reading from 0 to 3.

Participant - 3, yeah.

Interviewer - 3, watching TV.

Interviewer - Also, a 3. Sitting inactive in a public place, for example a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - A very solid 0 from me.

Interviewer - Very solid 0.
As a passenger in a car for an hour without a brake.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - 1.
Lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3.
I've accidentally hit the enter key at the same time. Oops. Sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - I guess it's situational, but 1.

Interviewer - 1.
Sitting quietly after lunch brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - 2, and in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - I assume not as the drive- I can't drive, so that's a moo-.

Interviewer - I-I would assume not as the driver correct if the driver was falling asleep, that would be bad.

Participant - Yeah, 2.

Interviewer - 2.
OK, lovely. Lovely. That is all of the surveys done.
Wordiness is not over. I'll apologise for that in advance, but that is the weird scale over. Do you want a pacing break or anything?

Participant - I am OK, but I'm equally happy to take one if you need one.

Interviewer - OK, I'm good. I'll just have a nap after this.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - And I hope it doesn't turn into a 10 hour sleep because that would ruin me.

Participant - Yeah!

Interviewer - OK.
Where is it gone? It's here somewhere.
Interview materials, interview guide. It wasn't here somewhere I forgot to open that. Lovely.
Come on, Microsoft Word.
You're so much worse than LibreOffice, but you can do it eventually.
There we go.
Right, so this is 20 questions. It's kind of split into subcategories. So questions will tend to lead on from each other. Some of them, I'll only ask if I...if you have explicitly mentioned something in a previous question or if I read between the lines and reckon it's applicable.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - If I read between the lines I will check I will ask and say...hi, does this sound about right? If so this question.

Participant - Gotcha.

Interviewer - Again, take as much time as you need. Circle back stuff if you want. This is entirely wordy. Feel free to say stories and talk as much as you want in whatever way that you want. It's not just the answer, but how it's given is valuable as well.
So don't like I had one, one participant who was kind of trying to. He started off trying to phrase it like he was talking to a doctor. And I'm like, no, that's the opposite of what I want. Please.

Participant - I hear you.

Interviewer - So yeah, if you're ready?

Participant - I'm ready.

Interviewer - What chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - Fibromyalgia.

Interviewer - Does-does that have fatigue as a as a symptom and comorbidity and the like?

Participant - So fibromyalgia is characterised by the chronic pain. Fatigue is a very common secondary symptom as well as so minor pain, fatigue, nausea and cognitive fog. But that kind of comes from the pain in the fatigue, you know so.

Interviewer - Uh-huh.

Participant - And dizziness. But there are like 400 associated comorbidities with fibromyalgia because it's one of these diagnosis of, well, you've got something wrong with you disorder.

Interviewer - Kind of long COVID, everyone's just slightly different.

Participant - Yeah.
Yeah, and no one really knows what causes it, and that's a weird kind of pain because it's... no one truly understands, you know, I guess neurological pains. So, yeah, if I would I...
My experience of fatigue is such that for a while I wondered if I also had chronic fatigue syndrome because I was experiencing fatigue a lot more than other people. I was like talking to who had fibro, but I don't think I experienced the like post exertional...

Interviewer - Malaise?

Participant - That's the one. Yeah. I mean, obviously, after I exert myself, I'm I, my recovery is going to be a lot rougher than it would be if I wasn't disabled, but it's not quite the same. I don't think. I think it's just that I'm running from a much smaller tank and I'm in pain all the time. And that's exhausting as well.

Interviewer - OK, you've answered question 2 in doing that, I just kind of needed you to say you experienced fatigue because that is what the study is about. But in the process you've answered, can you describe to me the symptoms of this condition that you experience? So question 3, can you give me examples of activities this condition has prevented you being able to do?

Participant - Yeah, I used to hike like most days and now sometimes I can't get out of bed. I will never hike. I can walk, and I can fortunately live. I know you know this, but for the sake of getting a quote, I fortunately live in rural Wales right next to the sea. So.
I still have some access to the natural world, but I can't...shortly before....
I got as unwell as I am now, I went on a 12 mile hike. That's like never gonna happen again. But then wait...
Working is quite a salient one. I-I haven't figured out a way to work with the fatigue that I now experience yet, it's... I may not complete the masters that I started depending on if they ever get back to me about my resit because...
The fatigue messed me up and the pain, but the fatigue messed me up for three weeks, which is really it turns out, and not being able to do anything for three weeks. Not good for dissertation. Who'd have thought?
I would say...
It's prevented me fully from doing a lot of things, but because I've-I've experienced the fatigue for seven years, I've experienced the fatigue in the way that I experienced it now for only a year and a half there was a I had a very stressful period a year and a half ago in that...
Turned it from fibre that I could ignore and pretend I wasn't chronically. I'll it just meant I had to sleep for 12 hours at least once a week to oh, everything's different now. And so I'm still in the process of learning what I now can't do, but I'm also still in the process of learning...What can I do to adapt to make things that I used to love possible for me again?

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much.
When is the earliest incidents you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - In 2018 to 2000-early-19, which is when it did initially start, there were what I now know was flare ups during a flare up of fibromyalgia. It will often be flu like symptoms. So I mean I get fatigue all the time. But like the fatigue you get when you have the flu and it's combined, it's a different fatigue. It's a heavier fatigue somehow, and I would experience that...
Once every month, once every two months, and I remember that was really difficult because I I just thought I was getting the flu all the time, to be honest. I was like, wow, this is improbable! But that, like, I think it was mostly social events and uni stuff. Not that I was doing a lot of uni back then, but that's for other reasons.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK.
OK, so you said explicitly, no PE-PEM, but you also said recoveries are rough, would you describe that as extended exhaustion?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity please?

Participant - What day are we on, Wednesday? On Monday, I went to the beach with two friends for a religious observation. We spent the whole time like sat down, you know, sang some songs it wasn't...
A massively physically exerting thing and then yesterday I left my bed once because...
I had muscle fatigue, I guess so I feel like this fatigue, my experience of fatigue at least, and I don't know the science and I don't know the medicine, medical understanding, but my experience, my own fatigue is that there's the sort of generalised fatigue which is kind of like sleepiness, but it's more than sleepiness...
Which I experienced to some degree all the time, but then muscle fatigue, which is kind of like a pain but it's not, but it is. Eloquent, and I'm more likely to experience that in addition to a higher levels of generalised fatigue...
After exerting and that's what I would consider a period of extended exhaustion. It wasn't that extended because I'm not in bed today like I'm talking now, but it certainly did last, like probably 30 hours.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting activities?

Participant - Yeah.....

Interviewer - Yeah?

Participant - Right now, but more usefully for you, possibly... Oh my God... Ha ha!
Often when I...
Try and do anything that requires following a trade of thought for more than 30 seconds whilst also standing up, especially so that would be like most things, but it's particularly I-I literally can't think of an example because I'm experiencing it now.

Interviewer - Uh-huh.

Participant - Sorry.

Interviewer - No, that's fine. Everyone has struggled to answer that question, which I think is just proof of pudding.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So yeah, that's-that's not unusual at all and not a problem because funnily enough, giving people a question where they need to think and remember when they have memory issues and cognitive issues never tends to go well.

Participant - No, no. I imagine it wouldn't cursed by your own work there, I suspect.

Interviewer - The joys of having to ask things in a particular way because of academia. Question 7, can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse? I ask this because I know you as a person.

Participant - So interestingly, I haven't been an insomniac for about two years and it-

Interviewer - Oh, lovely!

Participant - ...yeah! I still will have an occasional night of insomnia and that will make my fatigue much worse. But it's so rare.
Umm.
They're a combination of medication and acquiring a routine for a while, so that's actually not.

Interviewer - OK.
I'm-I'm very happy to hear that that question is actually not applicable at all.

Participant - Thank you.

Interviewer - Apologies for the assumption, but you know.

Participant - We nah, we lived together. You... That's a fair assumption.

Interviewer - What app or device do you currently use for this conditions and when did you start using them?

Participant - Guava, like the fruit and which isn't an app. I don't think it's called guava like the fruit. I think it's just called guava. I just wasn't sure if I'd pronounced that right. It's...it can be a link to-to all of your smart devices and stuff. I don't have any of those because I'm broke. So it was...
I have an iPhone and that will automatically count your steps and some other stuff, and I use the data from that and then I also manually I would tell you exactly which symptoms, but I've actually lost my phone right now so-so I can't.
But I would manually put in a few times a day.
Let's score out from 0 to 10 for fatigue.
Pain and other symptoms.

Interviewer - OK. That is that along, as a result of or in tandem with the NHS advice of an activity diary?

Participant - I'm inspired by but not result of or in tandem with it's... I've been going about. I've gone through. I'm going through the whole process of having been diagnosed now. Please will you give me some...

Interviewer - Something.

Participant - Anything. Yeah. So I was keeping it, so I hadn't been asked for that, but I knew I might be. And also I am comforted by having as much information as I can about a situation. So when there's something as nebulous as, oh, chronic illness that I don't understand and neither do the doctors taking my own data on it was as well as having like, oh, it's literally helpful because of all the reasons that having the data is helpful. It was also psychologically helpful because it made me feel like I had a little bit more, not necessarily control, but at least knowledge of what was happening.

Interviewer - OK. Just as an aside, would you be willing to volunteer 10 screenshots, pictures, whatever of said to diary at the end of the study?

Participant - If I can find my phone, which for both of our sakes I hope so then yes.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yep. OK, that's. I mean you can you can submit it as late afterwards as you want as long as it's not like 3 months, which I hope you don't lose your phone for that long.

Participant - Me too.

Interviewer - OK. Have you used any previous tools before guava?

Participant - Umm.
No, I think I kept intending to, but I never got around to it.

Interviewer - OK.
How do you use this device or application? If you feel you've already answered this, then just say that.

Participant - I think I answered it. The main in terms of use because so many of the numbers that I was putting in were self-described rather than, as opposed to something being measured like my heart rate or my blood pressure or something like that.
I think it's well, well known amongst chronically ill people that if someone says describe your fatigue from 1 to 10 or describe your pain or describe your anything from 1 to 10, especially if you're neurodivergent, which I am, that's difficult because it's not an objective scale and the worst pain you can ever imagine is going to be different on different days. It's like the oh...say breaking your leg is the worst pain you've ever experienced. Well, I could break both my legs and that would be even worse. So is that a 10 or is that a what, a 5? I digressed, sorry, so I...A lot of the actual use of the app and putting the numbers in was sort of the reckoning of trying to make it as objective as possible to make the data useful.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - To what degree I've managed that I don't know. I change my mind on it every day as to how I consider my own self reported data.

Interviewer - OK.
So it's used more as a self recording platform than a hard data recording platform.

Participant - For now, yeah. Although I'd love to get, like a... smart measuring device for things like blood pressure and heart rate, because again, I'm aware this is very common among chronically ill people. But because Fibromyalgia is a diagnosis of exclusion...

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - ...it's not impossible that something else is amiss.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So it's and the NH- I'm grateful to have an NHS, but it is underfunded and it they-they do not have a lot of time. So anything that puts the not the power of diagnosis, I don't have that, nor do I want it, but the power of figuring out what the hell is going on.
Anything puts that a little bit more in my hands. Is good for me in my opinion.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When do you use the device or application?

Participant -  I try and use it every morning and every evening at least realistically, that is not always the case, because sometimes I forget about it for days at a time, which makes it less useful. But.
That is what it is.

Interviewer - OK, just because I don't know much about this particular application is that outside of its intent or is that part of what it was designed for?

Participant - As in my use or when I use it.

Interviewer - Both.

Participant - I think I broadly use it within its intent. It has options for, I mean it's also got a premium level that I haven't looked at because I'm not giving them my money. But within the free features, it's got other features like the ability to link up other devices. It-one thing that-It is-I think it is intended for....
It's not just intended for inputting the data, it's intended for the data being used by the patient themselves. There are exports for to show doctors and stuff, but they are also it has a really well equipped interface for visual data visualisation for the data that you're putting in and for example, you can compare two factors like pain score and fatigue score for example, and it will do things like figure out the P value for you and that kind of thing. And I realise that, I mean I-I'm a statistics nerd, so...I will export the data and play with it myself. The majority of the world do not care about a P value, so it's clearly that it's being designed. It's clear to me that it's designed to allow people to look at their own data and make reasonable...decisions or observations from it. But if whether I think it is supposed to be used every day with on a degree of regularity, and it has a function for setting a reminder that I ignore mostly.

Interviewer - OK.
OK, so I think you've answered this question. If you've got anything to add to it, just say how frequently do you use the application?

Participant - Yeah, I feel like I've answered that.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, good. Good. Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application?

Participant - So if...
I experience chronic fatigue syndrome as opposed to just chronic fatigue with the...
Post...

Interviewer - Exertional malaise?

Participant - Thank you. With the post exertional malaise, then that means that I should not exercise as much or possibly at all, because exercise can trigger that and exercise is not helpful. If it is just fibromyalgia with a strong fatigue component, exercise will improve my...condition.
That... wanting to know whether or not I should be exercising and getting an idea of how much I should exercise...
I guess it was not an activity that triggered the use of the thing, but it was an urge for an activity.

Interviewer - So..Is there an example of an activity before you started using it? That kind of embodied that decision making, or was it more a general...

Participant - Umm.

Interviewer - ...generalised decision?

Participant - I think it was probably...
I mean the activity that I do the most that...
Is the most likely to have caused it is just going for a walk, a little walk. It was almost certainly that because that is also when I do my thinking, but I imagine that is the case of it being a combination of a lot of things rather than one specific activity. But if it was an activity, it would have almost certainly been walking.

Interviewer - OK. And that was a daily walk, right? If I'm remember?

Participant - Yeah, well, not daily, but as daily as I can get.

Interviewer - Yeah, daily in the context of someone who's not fully able.

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Perfect. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times that device or application alerted you to over exertion?

Participant - No, because I've not got that set up, but I would like to have that set up.

Interviewer - I will clarify, this isn't just a live alert, but for example you get to the end of the day, look back on-on your data, and go oh wow, I did way too much. That will count.

Participant - Oh, right. Well, yeah. So the step count is obviously...step count isn't a massively useful signify for everything, but if I...
I look at my step count and I've walked 8000 steps. I will get out the Epsom salts, you know, and make sure that I don't have any plans the following day or the following three days.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Umm.
I can't think of a specific time it's happened, but I know it's happened. It just has happened quite a few times, so it's all sort of blended into one.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, perfect. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device or application?

Participant - Yes, I can. There's a weekly in [town], which is a town like 5 to 10 miles away from where I live. Ish.
How do I put this?
It's a weekly sober rave. I guess it's like an ecstatic dance thing that's relevant because part of the practise of ecstatic dancing is about mind, body connection and feeling your body's limits as well as what your body can do.
I think for a lot of the majority of the people that do this are like hippies in their 40s. So for a lot of them, I think it's about reminding them what their body can do. For me, it's more about reminding myself of my boundaries in quite a healthy way. Before I started really tracking my symptoms...and...trying to become more familiar with them, I-I was becoming very afraid to do any. I mean, I experienced agoraphobia anyway for unrelated reasons, but it was being made much worse by the fear of what if I go for a walk and then the fatigue is too much and I collapse and I can't.
If you're Googling that, that is fear. It's a fear, like anxiety about leaving the house.

Interviewer - OK.
Right. Yes, thank you.

Participant - Sorry, I would have...

Interviewer - No, no, no, it's fine. It's fine.

Participant - And before I was tracking my symptoms and felt that I had a better idea of what was going on and where my fatigue was coming from, what was causing it, I was becoming just terrified to do absolutely anything because what if that triggers a flare up?
Umm.
And so I wasn't doing...
I wasn't moving the body very much and then I think because of using the app and tracking my symptoms and even like I still don't really know what's going on, but at least I've learnt to be more aware of it, it empowers me to do things like even if I go to both and I'm like well actually it's time to sit down and watch these hippies spin around...
Umm...
It's something that wouldn't have done before.

Interviewer - Hmm.
Yeah. OK, perfect. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device or application? Don't know how relevant this is given fibro if-if it is just fibro, but...

Participant - Umm...I...
I think it stopped me from oh, wait, now that's starting. I at one point I was using it in combination with, I wasn't like counting calories or anything, But like a general tracking of nutrition...
to...
but...
I'm just not very good at that anyway. But I was like trying to cut out excess caffeine and all the rest of it, but I don't know if that's to do with the app that I failed to do that or to do with the fact that cutting out caffeine is...
Yeah...
Well, it's not something I've ever truly succeeded at.
Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, out of interest, because diet trackers, we are counting them as health devices. Did you use that particular tracking method in relation to the condition at all?

Participant - No, it I. It was literally. Did I eat a meal today?

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - It wasn't more specific than that.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much.
Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device or application?

Participant - I don't think so.
I'm not. I might have started if I wasn't using it, no.

Interviewer -Yes.
Things, things you would have tried had you not had whatever relationship you have with your application.

Participant - Writing it down in a notebook, no writing it down in a spreadsheet.

Interviewer - So...

Participant - I'd like I what I would have done if I had not got this app is I would have built myself a spreadsheet, probably to score a very system, similar system to the app. I would have also acquired exports of the native Apple health, so that's again foot count and. Foot count? I don't think that's what it is. I have two of those. Step count.
Pretty much step count.
And combined it and tried to calculate those P values myself. So I probably would have done exactly the same thing but with way more effort.

Interviewer - OK so.
To uh...
OK, so this question is meant to be...
Things you would have started if you didn't have access to the data, but I suppose this doesn't apply to you because you would have had the access to the data somehow just in a more exert- in a way that causes more exertion.

Participant -And is that which is very relevant to chronic illness 'cause it just, I wouldn't have been able to keep up with because of that, so.

Interviewer - Yeah.
So compared to other people, this isn't a data collecting advantage. This is a time saver.

Participant - Yeah, but I may not be. I'm aware that perhaps as a former data scientist and a current nerd, I'm possibly not necessarily representative of the general population for that one.

Interviewer - OK.
No, that's fine.
OK. Thank you very much.
The last of the proper questions, there's a couple of speculative ones after this, but the last of the proper ones. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?
And I suppose I'll add on all data collection methods, seeing as you would have been doing the method anyway.

Participant - Yeah. Umm...Can you read the first part of the question again> Sorry.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision?

Participant - Yeah. OK. So the sorry, I've just seen a bead on the floor and I want to pick that before it gets lost forever. I...
How do I explain this?
My friend runs a day long event once a year, for which I am often a volunteer at the door and also a performer.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Whether or not I can physically do that depends very, very much on my ability to pace, like pace myself through the day, and also like, I mean there are other factors, but pacing is the biggest and most relevant factor.
Umm.
Without.
So I the last [event] was before I started using the app.
While I certainly made it to the end of the day and...
It really messed me up quite badly, but I don't regret it 'cause it was a really...
Great event, but it did make me think like were there ways that I can handle this better? Because I did pace myself as best I could, but I didn't really have the knowledge of the best way to do that. I didn't know what it is that exerts me and I also for me a lot of the value of the practise of data collection, especially the self reporting that I do is the fact that it forces me...to...
Look at my body. It's like, not physically, but you know, oh, how fatigued am I? How much pain am I in? And then that does sort of naturally lead to...
And therefore, what can I do or what do I need to do to mitigate things and that kind of thing? So the last [event] that I went to really messed me up, I experienced a period of extended exhaustion, but not for 30 hours for like 3 weeks.
Umm.
And it was kind of a case of like, well, I want to be able to do things like that, especially performing, because that's really personally important to me. It's just not something...
That I can comfortably do in the way I used to be able to, so the use of that that is an event, an event, that sort of triggered... How can I learn to get better at this, which eventually turned into well, collecting data is something that will get me to know more. I didn't expect it to be as beneficial in the act of collecting data I thought it would just be the data that was useful.
To me, but the act of collecting it has also been very useful. I don't know if that actually answered your question. I got a little bit...

Interviewer - OK. No, that's fine. So you almost don't have examples of events after you're starting to use it, because the events that are relevant have reduced because you are using it.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Would it be fair to say?

Participant - Yeah. Yeah, definitely, yeah.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much.
Before we get on to the speculative thingies, is there anything that you have remembered or anything you would like to circle back to. To give a quick recap the first section, 7 questions was about your condition and kind of a, a recap or a, a demonstration of that.

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - Questions 8 to 11 were exactly the same, but about your device and how you use it, and then questions seven questions 12 to 17 were combining those two and giving examples of how these two things interact. So if there's anything you'd like to circle back to or mention?

Participant - Yeah.
The only thing I've thought of is that so some of the symptoms I'm aware that some of the symptoms and the reporting of the symptoms, the symptoms cause the reporting of the symptoms to be biased. And what I mean by that, as an example, is if I am experiencing a lot of cognitive fog...Like a lot of cognitive fog, I am very unlikely to bring out my phone and open up my app and put oh, I'm experiencing a 10 out of 10 of cognitive fog. Now, I might remember to do that like after the fact, probably won't though.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - And that means that the numbers the data I have for my cognitive fog is incomplete because when my cognitive fog is at its worst, I'm not putting that in, and that's not a huge problem for me because I'm aware of it and I like to think of the statistics, but it is something that I'm very aware of and I don't know if that's entirely relevant, but it might have been. So I said it anyway.

Interviewer - So to come up with an improvised question that is at risk of being too direct.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - As my supervisor says, get them to answer the question without asking it. That's quite difficult.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Would you...say that given this device isn't designed for the people with fatigue, the design of it does not...aid...those with, with cognitive fog. So the incentive to use the device to record cognitive fog is lessened because of the design of the device or the application and the UI and all of that.

Participant - I think that...
Yes, but with the caveat that although the incentive, the incentive to use it is less, I don't think...That makes it-It does make it somewhat less valuable because I just said it's an incomplete data set, an incomplete data set will always be less valuable than a complete data set. Obviously, but I don't think that it completely shrinks the value down. I do think there is still a lot of value, especially if you're aware of it, but equally I don't know of any sensors that will objectively measure my fatigue, and if I did, and I could afford it, I would wear one because I would much prefer to be measuring it in an objective way. Oh, cognitive, we're talking about cognitive fog, but those two things often come hand in hand for me anyway, with why I'm conflating them a lot.

Interviewer - OK.
Yeah.
I will say that the, the three surveys beforehand, one was meant to measure both mental and physical fatigue, and one was split into two parts, one was mental and one was physical. So when we're, we're talking about fatigue in this study, it's not just physical fatigue, but also cognitive fog, and having that battery, that fuel tank that you have used up by doing something like this, which for me isn't physically taxing, but I will need a rest after this because of how mentally...fatiguing it is.

Participant - Yeah, I mean, it'd do the same thing to me. I find it really hard to tell the difference between mental and physical fatigue. I don't know if I just happen to feel them at the same time. A lot of the time, or if it's what's the word, not proprioception. That's a different thing. The autistic trait of not knowing what the hell you're feeling.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - And you're experiencing inside your body. I think probably does alter my own perception of my own fatigue.

Interviewer - Yeah. Yeah. OK.
Right after a question that turned into four questions, the last three questions, what form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Ideally it would be a wearable devi- would be a wearable device that can measure as much as it can measure. I don't know heart rate, blood pressure might be nice. In a magical world, it's going to measure my fatigue like I just said, but I'm aware that physics...And current medical understanding makes that a bit difficult. That would connect...to, I mean, I do like the app as an interface with self reporting, but it's also personally really important to me...for it to be data that is held on your device, not in the cloud. Or at least you have the option to do that. Data that you have access to data that you can export that is exportable in a format like CSV that isn't a format private to the app, that kind of thing. The data itself is a very big concern to me because.
I care about data.

Interviewer - Yeah, you're a data scientist.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah, OK question 19, what functions would you remove from your current or... current device or devices you have used or know about?

Participant - I don't think any. I think as many it's nice to have the options for the functions that they have. I wish there weren't functions hidden behind a paywall, but that's not a problem with the app. That's a capitalism thing.

Interviewer - OK. And the last question, what functions would you add? You've touched on this a little bit more.

Participant - To be honest, I'm very happy with the app I use in its data export functions and all the rest of it. But in terms of import, yeah, I would like, in an ideal world, there to be ways to either make self reporting more objective. I've no idea if that's possible or...
There to be ways of sensing with a mechanical sensor. I suppose it would be an electric sensor, not a mechanical sensor. A mechanism that measures it.

Interviewer - Hmm.
OK. Yeah. So it's...Having additional options that aren't geared towards fitness.

Participant - Yeah. Yeah, I think that's the core of it. It is very frustrating as a chronically ill person to hear from your doctors and your physiotherapists. Not that that's oddly specific and everyone that it's all about fitness. When I think for a lot of us it's about survival.
And any tool or technology that we use, I think it's nice if it reflects that not in a OK maybe it reflecting survival might be a bit drastic but like...
It's nice for our tools to be reflective of our lives rather than reflective of the ideal that we're all supposed to be trying to live to the if we're using these apps, we probably can't.

Interviewer - OK, wonderful. Thank you very much. If-if there's anything else you'd like to add?

Participant - Don't think so.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. I'll just stop the recording.

Participant - Cool. Cool. OK.

Participant - Yep.

Participant - Cool.

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK. Question number one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant -  Undiagnosed, but likely hypermobility.
Undiagnosed, but likely complex PTSD, autism and ADHD.

Interviewer - OK. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experienced in the context of mobility or fatigue?

Participant -  Uh.
Hypermobility makes it so that any excessive amount of exercise causes my knees in particular to be incredibly painful. As I walk to the point of needing accessibility aids, if it gets... past a certain point, or just being unable to do things, the autism makes it harder to...
The brain fog makes it harder to describe things.
The autism makes it harder to do things because I'm constantly sort of having to battle myself about things being done a certain way or it makes socialisation a lot harder as well because I'm constantly sort of checking things off if that makes sense. So it sort of makes... a normal conversation, slightly harder, because I-I feel like I'm checking tone and like how I sound how they sound, how I'm looking, how they're looking, etcetera.
The complex PTSD that just sort of saps away my energy because of, I will always have like a background radiation level of sort of poor mental health, no matter what kind of day it is.
And I will always be disassociated to some extent, so I will always be sort of dealing with this like glass wall between whatever I'm doing and myself. So it's sort of having to try and overcome that.
It can also flare up and cause... like flashback episodes or just emotional episodes as well, and recovering from them can be a bit tricky.
The ADHD doesn't directly affect fatigue, but it does make it harder to sleep, so it kind of adds on to it in a roundabout way, if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - And also constantly jumping from one thing to another and sort of constantly trying to be doing a lot of different things can make it quite hard to focus.
Yeah, I think that covers everything. Unless I've missed something. One of them causes brain fog. I'm not exactly sure which one, and that can make it quite hard to think of words and... you know, sort of do regular things if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.
OK, I will say this isn't to say that these aren't important or anything...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...but with the context of interviewing someone who has fatigue issues...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...I don't want you to be using up your energy well discussing things that I won't be able to go into depth on.

Participant - Cool, yeah.

Interviewer - So say that it affects fatigue, but when so the next question we'll ask you to give examples.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - PTSD attacks and autism meltdowns, to give examples...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...wouldn't be....
Gods, what's the right word? I wouldn't be able to draw much from them...

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - ...to be able to put in the conclusion, if that makes sense.

Participant - Yeah, OK, cool.

Interviewer - But like for example the, the autism thing like, "ah, hyper mobility makes me... exhausted more often because it's more effort to do things and the way I do things is not necessarily the most efficient because of the symptoms of ASD"

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - And that is more than fine.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - Like acknowledge that it plays in, but don't-don't necessarily give it a focus if that makes sense.

Participant - Yeah, I got you.

Interviewer - And, yeah. Sorry, sorry that this is a thing, but...

Participant - No, it's fine.

Interviewer - ...Context of study and limited time and energy for us both.
So question three, can you give me examples of activities this, these conditions have prevented you being able to do?

Participant - So me and my partner went on a holiday to [place] last week and I didn't... I forgot to account for the hyper mobility and it ended up with me doing...
About 27K more steps than usual, and because of that it meant that I was struggling to walk around, I was struggling to get upstairs, sort of general exercise, and walking was more painful than normal, so it was a lot slower, so it was taking more time to get places and therefore ex-ex-expending more energy.
To the point where we went to the museum and I had to borrow a wheelchair from them because I just wasn't... it would have been either that or not go to the museum at all.
Sorry, you'll have to give me a second.
The...
The brain fog can make things a lot harder to sort of...
When I'm having important conversations that we usually spike up, especially if I've not, you know, if I'm more struggling than nor-normal, it just... kind of adds an extra layer of having to use up energy in order to do things which are easier for neurotypical people.
The ASD sensory issues can exhaust me quite a bit as well. Just being in a room with bright lights or whatever. If I don't try and nullify it, it will just sort of sap my energy and make me unable to do normal things until... it just kind of disables me basically if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Mhmm.

Participant - Uh, that's all I can think of right now, that's useful.

Interviewer - I'm going to go ahead and say that brain fog inhibited your ability to answer that?

Participant - Yeah, yeah, that would be a good conclusion to make.
