Participant - I would say around 2021-2022 is when it got a lot worse, but I've always sort of had... It would take, always take me a lot longer to sort of warm up to like walks or anything. I would always have more pain than normal.
But when I came to university, I was sort of dealing with mental health issues, so I wasn't exercising as much. So it then sort of made it so that I guess I wasn't strengthening the muscles enough to keep... my knee joints in place so, I think either late 2021 or early 2022 I started using a knee brace on my right knee quite a lot to sort of try and keep it being out of pain.
Actually no, sorry, I remember when I was 16, my right knee started hurting a lot more than the left one.
I didn't have to compensate for as much as I do now, but I remember it being sort of abnormal, but nothing was done about it.
Yeah, I think I think that's...

Interviewer - OK.
I'm going to go ahead and say that you haven't mentioned anything to do with extended exhaustion or post exertional malaise.

Participant - Yeah.
Oh.

Interviewer - Is that correct?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - I...
I...

It will just be sort of, you know, either sat on the sofa or sat in bed just kind of either watching things or just listening to music or whatever.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - It, it will be, you know, very limited social contact as well. Maybe talking to friends online, but that will be about it.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting activities?

Participant - I'm struggling with this interview, so there's, there's an example. It can also sort of inhibit like split second decision making as well if that makes sense.
So for example, if I'm playing video games with my friends, I would rather be sort of told what I'm meant to be doing rather than trying to figure it out because it means that I can access what people are doing a lot easier for, it's just like, "oh, go do this thing and then come back here."
Umm.
It, it can also sort of make me struggle with um...
When I was last in university, which was about 2023, so I'm not sure if this is too far of an example or not, but...
Being involved in classrooms, sort of like putting my hands up and getting involved with discussion would be quite hard because I would be trying to catch up with what was said and be trying to process it, if that makes sense.
Note taking is also quite difficult for me and the-the brain fog would play a part in that I'd say.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Umm.
So...
If I'm waking up past a certain time, say like midday, I will try my best to sort of shuffle it back and wake up earlier, but if I don't get about, I think it's about 10 hours of sleep a night, I just kind of write the day off because I won't be able to interact with people. I won't really, you know, be able to do anything outside or whatever. It will mostly be a day inside, but um...
Even, even sometimes, like if I've had 10 hours and just waking up to alarm, an alarm will sometimes be enough to sort of knock me out for the rest of the day. Even if I would have had enough sleep otherwise.
And especially if I've overexerted myself otherwise.
Sleepi-, I will sometimes sleep too much and that will still somehow make me feel, you know, fatigued anyway, if that makes sense.
Yeah.

Interviewer - What device or applications do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using them?

Participant - So my phone automatically comes with the Apple Health app, but I use an app called Pikmin Bloom which is, it sort of gamifies going outside and getting steps and stuff.
I can tell you the exact day I started doing that because it logs...
It logs like your emotions for that particular day.
Was the 2nd of July 2024 is when I started using it.
And I've been using it on and off throughout the year, but mostly on.

Interviewer - OK. Did you use anything before these two?

Participant - I have an Apple Watch, but it's like a really old generation one and I've had it since about 2019, but I got really frustrated with data not transferring over due to security reasons, which is fair, but it kind of knocked out my motivation to keep on using it, I will use it if I'm going to the gym or whatever so I can accurately see what, like how many calories I've burned, how many minutes of exercise I've done. Whatever. Whatever.

Interviewer - OK, how do you use these devices or applications?

Participant - Uh.
So with, I'm going to mostly focus on Pikmin because I don't really check that the health app. I just can look at it and see overall and stuff it basically. So it records your steps and it also records like how far you've walked as well using GPS. So it's not just like if you've walked about in your house for example, it will record everything.
And I use it to try and get myself to hit certain goals, because every month there will be an event on which is basically the same every month, there will be certain rounds and it's sort of gets progressively harder. So for example, the 1st, the 1st Round of stages, it will always have 4, but it could be like walk 1000 steps and then... specific game thing, specific game thing, walk 2000 steps and also plant X amount of flowers. Planting flowers records outdoor walks. You have to turn it on and it will it will plant a specific type of flower, whichever one you've put on, but afterwards you can see exactly where you've been and so... Gamifying exercise helps me quite a bit because it also gives me the sort of... Immediate gratification of, "oh, I've done this thing and I've made the Pikmin happy, so now I will do more exercise in order to have more of the..." There's also like different decoration types, so they'll be wearing silly costumes.
And I quite like completing collections, so it'll be like, "oh, if I walk X amount of steps, I will be able to grow a pikmin and then I, if I walk X amount more steps I will then be able to see what decoration that pikmin has."

Interviewer - OK.
When do you use this device or application?

Participant - Umm.
Every day I, because it's just an app on my phone I can I'll just occasionally open it up and check and see how things are going, but I will deliberately use it when I'm going on walks or just even going outside to the shop or whatever.

Interviewer - OK.
How frequently do you use the device or application?

Participant - 5+ times a day just to see how it's going, but deliberate like walk usage, I try and go on at least one walk a day, so I want to say four to seven times a week.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application?

Participant - Errr...
And...could you rephrase that, sorry.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of things that made you think, "oh, I need to start using some, a device or I need to start using an app of this nature?"

Participant - Oh.
Umm.
I didn't really. Well, I've been wanting to get more active and lose weight because I, I've, from various like sort of things I've seen online and from various friends of mine who have hypoermobility in one way or another, I saw, I've always seen that, you know, strengthening the muscles around the joints that are more loose can help with the-the pain and the fatigue and everything. But I just came across it on a YouTube advert one day and I was like, "oh, that, that looks like it could, you know, help out."
And it-it has so yay for advertising, I guess.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the application has alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - Umm...
It doesn't automatically have that as a feature, but with for example, with going to [place] last week because I could, you open it up and the first thing you see is "you've taken X amount of steps today" and... at the end of the week, it will say, "oh, you've taken X amount of steps over the week. That's more or less than last week", whatever, but being able to open up and see two days in a row of over 10K steps sort of, you know, put it in words of "I have done too much exercise."

Interviewer - OK.
Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the application?

Participant - I have deliberately gone on more walks I would say, in order to reach the goals set by the game.
I...
Because you, you can get, I think on a on a regular day if I don't go outside, I get about 150 steps or less. But in order to actually make any progression, there are some things that you can do sat inside because you can send your pikmin out to battle different things, and then they'll come back and they'll get the next thing you need for planting flowers, but you will always get to steps where you need to plant the flowers or do X amount of steps in order to progress.

Interviewer - OK.
Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the application?

Participant - Umm.
I don't think so?


Interviewer - So, so this is things that as a result of your, what the application was showing you, you decided to stop?

Participant - Ummm. I don't think...

Interviewer - If there isn't any, that's fine.

Participant - Yeah, I don't think that applies to me.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to use these applications?

Participant - Umm.
I would say overall I've had a positive trend in steps per day.
Is that answering the question correctly?

Interviewer - So, examples of times that have vindicated your decision to start using the app that have told you it was worth the time and energy to use the application.

Participant - Oh.
Sort of, because-because I've been motivated to go outside more, I think I've had more days of doing exercise I would otherwise wouldn't have. Granted, it's not as high as I would like it yet, but...
Because of my mental health, I do struggle with justifying going outside sometimes. But sort of... you get, you get a special colour in the background if you cross 10K steps per day and if I haven't done too many of those days in a row, it's nice to see, "oh, I've actually gone above and beyond today and you know, strengthened myself a bit."

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Your final three questions, they're more...
What's the word speculative?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Uh.
I think like a wearable, basically an Apple Watch, but without all of the sort of excessive apps or whatever. And without notifications pinging up on your wrist constantly as the default.
I think it would just be something that isn't automatically connected to the Internet, but still records all of the data that it needs to and also has like a, a way to store music so that I don't necessarily need to have my phone on me all the time to listen to music.

Interviewer - What functions would you remove?

Participant - From an Apple Watch?

Interviewer -  From whatever, whatever exists to create your ideal device.

Participant - Oh, I would...

Interviewer - What, what things are common or that you have experience of that you don't want?

Participant - Monetization of like fitness apps or anything. Otherwise you have a tonne of adverts. I would remove the need for either my phone to always be on to record things, or just need, I would remove the need for my devices to always be connected to the Internet so that when I go out, I don't have the distractions of whatever's in my phone, if that makes sense. I can just sort of... enjoy the natural beauty of the outside world without getting distracted by, you know, social media or whatever.
I guess just something that is... solely for tracking exercise and solely for, you know, recording things without the, the bloatware and the subscriptions and the need to always be online.

Interviewer - What functions would you add?

Participant - I would add maybe like say, "oh, you've done X amounts of steps yesterday, maybe you should average about this amount of steps" so that it's sort of it, maybe it has like a trial period of a month of seeing your energy, your activity and then sort of an algorithm that works out the rough neighbourhood of steps that you need to do a day in order to exercise, but also not like fatigue yourself too much if that makes sense. So even if it was just like you know, one day a week you walk 5K steps but the rest of the week you're walking under 2K in order to
minmax everything.

Interviewer - So it would be a feature that works out a derived limit.

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - And then a derived recommendation from it.

Participant - Yeah, but that limit would also be open to say, for example, if it was, you'd had a knee injury and then it would start really low at the beginning, but sort of taper upwards as you got stronger, I guess.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Is there anything you'd like to add? Anything you remembered or anything you otherwise feel is relevant?

Participant - Umm.
Looking for an app that does everything that you wanted to do without an-an unnecessary subscription is more of a hassle than it should be.
Because I've noticed that for certain apps if you want to access, say you know how many steps you've been doing per week, overall, it will sometimes say, "oh, you need to either have a trial or register for a subscription." I guess is my one big gripe.

Interviewer - OK. Anything else?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK, we'll stop recording then.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - OK. What? Oh, sorry. Go on.

Participant - I'd act-
Now I was just going to say like the whole approach of the app is very fitness and it would be nice if it was more health. I would prefer health angled thing than a... and actually do you know, I think that would probably do most people in the world as much good for it to be health rather than constantly...fitness seems to have got a bit mad.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Question 19, second to last one, what functions would you remove?

Participant - I would remove...All the gym bunny stuff.

Interviewer - If you want to say stuff that you would improve in this as well, then then by all means go for stuff you'd improve, because obviously I think with the step the way the steps works for you, you'd remove it. If it works as it does because it doesn't.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, it's totally counterproductive. My concern is as well because you've got Wes Streeting in the news saying that his big plan for getting people back to work is to give them wearables so they can build up activity. But if he looked at my...Fitbit he'd go well, you shouldn't be getting your pit because you're doing 2000 steps a day. And I was like, I am absolutely not doing 2000 steps a day. That's just nonsense. So if they're planning to judge people based on the input, they're going to have to improve the system. If it's going to be accurate. I just think that whole thing is a...madness. But God yeah, sorry. You're going to have to repeat the question.

Interviewer - Yeah, no problem. What functions would you remove from your device?

Participant - Remove. Remove. Yeah. No, I mean, if you can't fix it...

Interviewer - Or improve.

Participant - Yeah, I would improve the steps or get rid of it, as it is, it's completely useless to me. I would remove...
I would remove a lot of the stuff that's angled towards different type of exercise that aren't helpful. They just make you, I just look at them and go "God. How depressing. I can't do any of those things."
And I'm trying to think of all the, there's loads of settings that I've just turned loads of stuff off. I-I spent the first week that I had it every time something buzzed, thinking "Right, how do I turn it off" because I don't want it telling me to move. I don't want it telling me...
I don't want it telling me anything, I'd like it to take in all the information so that I can look at it when I want to rather than being poked by it. I guess if you get, there are some people have chronic fatigue syndrome or various chronic fatigue things who... just don't try to do anything at all, and I do, do my like my little bit of stretches every day because I think you need to have some movement, but... it's that... maybe I, maybe it's more about my psychology of guilt and shame that I just don't want it guilting or shaming.
Yeah, I mean.

Interviewer - No, that's no problem at all. That's a perfectly valid point of view, to be honest. What functions would you add?

Participant - I mean, I'd love a daily readiness that really looked like how I actually felt.

Interviewer - Or would it be fair, one that explained why it came to the conclusion that it did?

Participant - Yeah, yeah, yeah. That would be more useful. Yeah, because I think that...

Interviewer - 'Cause just sorry, just to clarify the understanding it just gives you a number.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - It doesn't tell you anything about how it came to that.

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - No. It gives you a number and-and it either says moderate or poor or something next to it. It's a one word thing and you think, well, I don't know how you think I'm more poor than I was yesterday because I felt worse yesterday. So it's obviously, I think it's like the baselines are off, I think probably the whole,
baseline is determined by what a healthy person would be, it-that's the thing that would be useful actually was be for you to be able to tell it... if you're like having a heart issue or something to begin with, because judging what is actually your heart malfunctioning and having tachycardia as "Oh, great. You've been to the gym" is really counterproductive.
Yeah.

Interviewer -  Yeah. OK. Is there anything you'd like to add from those three? So I-what form your ideal of device would take, what would you remove and what would you add?

Participant - It feels like adding... I can't think how you do it. There's something more about pacing somehow that was more...
More positive in the same way that it goes "Oh, great. You've done a heavy cardio load." If it went "Oh good for you. You've paced well today." Do you know what I mean? It was that more sort of positive feedback from-from a pacing rather than "Oh, it looks like you haven't done anything today. Let's do more tomorrow", which is very much how it's tilted at the moment. If it was for an ill person, it's far more useful for them to be pacing and for resting to be a positive thing rather than a negative thing.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Yeah, I think I can't think of anything else at the moment.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Is there anything from in general from any of the interview or anything else that you'd like to add?

Participant - Can't think of anything. Have I said enough? I don't know that I've been particularly useful to you [Interviewer].

Interviewer - Yeah, don't worry about again, like I'm... I'm not a doctor. Don't. Don't worry about. "Oh, how useful is this?" "How it does this fit into the the academic thing?" Like all faff, ignore it. Just say what you think.

Participant - Yeah.
OK, as long as you're happy, that's the main thing.
Right. If that's everything, I'm going to stop the recording then if you're happy.

Participant - OK.

title: P07 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - This is what happens when you get someone with the condition to research the condition.

Participant - Yeah, I can relate a little bit too much to that.

Interviewer - OK, so from the top, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - So I've got diagnosed joint hypermobility syndrome and, there is- I do think there is a potential I could have hypermobile ehlers danlos syndrome as well because I hit a lot of the criteria for that. So it means that I've got a connective tissue disorder and... basically all my joints have a really abnormal range of motion, which leads to a lot of different symptoms.

Interviewer - OK. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Yeah. So my joints are a lot more bendy than most people, so my range of motion is really strange. Means things bend backwards, whereas they shouldn't too far. So I'm quite injury prone, prone to partial dislocations and complete dislocations, sprains.
A lot of muscle stiffness as well. Pain after exercising or while exercising. Joint fatigue. Struggle with certain movement patterns, poor proprioception and balance. So there's, yeah, there's a lot of things from it.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. And now for the new things, again terribly sorry.

Participant - OK, that's alright.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities, these conditions have prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - Yeah. So one thing I really did want to start doing was running, but I know that's like traditionally something that people with, it's, people struggle with the knees who don't have joint conditions. So there's been a couple of times I've tried it and it just my knees just don't, don't. They're not having any of it.
My knees are probably one of the worst points I have, though that one of the things I struggle with the most so yeah, so anything to do with running anything that involves a lot of flexion in the shoulders as well, because I've had a full shoulder dislocation before, never regained full strength in that.
So at the gym there's a lot of specific exercises like I can't bench press, I can't really shoulder press, pass a certain amount of time because it's start, they try and sublux quite a lot. Yeah. So just quite a few different physical activities, really just general exercise. And there's ones that like I can do. But I have to make modifications to and a lot of gym exercises.
Yeah. So those are the main ones, I'd say.

Interviewer - OK. And when is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - God, I mean, I've had it since I was, like, forever for my whole life. Like, what I used to treat it like a party trick when I was a kid, which probably didn't really help because I used to be like, "Oh my God, look what my joints can do." I'd say, yeah, to be fair at school because there were some activities at Sports Day that I just find really painful, like anything to do with running. I just wouldn't...really, wouldn't really do, and when we used to do gymnastics classes at school, there were some things I just couldn't do because my arms are to try and pop out.

Interviewer - OK. So it's all based in, in childhood things because obviously this is a lifelong thing.

Participant - Yeah. So the earliest ones were in childhood, definitely, but there's been just random things throughout my life.

Interviewer - OK.
I'm saying this because of the mistake I made and I said brain fog and you, you related to it.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - So I'm going to ask can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - Like physical activities or just any kind of activities?

Interviewer - Any activities.

Participant - In general.

Interviewer - Fatigue in, in this case is both mental and physical.

Participant - Yeah, I'd say some of the main ones I've had is that sometimes I just I forget things and I-I just there's been times where I've completely forgotten I've had a meeting or training on a day or I've got a day mixed up and just not known what I'm supposed to be doing that day and had a call like after. Like, why didn't you turn up to a meeting? And I've realised that I've missed, so it can be quite like severe things like that like luckily, people have been really understanding but and I think that's definitely something that's been worse since doing PhD, just because like, there's so much more to remember and so much more to juggle. But there's definitely been times that I have, like, missed meetings, things like that. I can't remember anything super early on because it wasn't something that really affected me when I was younger. I always actually had a really, really good memory and I was very sharp and I still do to some extent, but I've noticed the older I've got, the more that things like, like meetings and commitments have sort of slipped my mind at times. I've just found myself forgetting things.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Oh, I sleep absolutely horrendously honestly. Like I'd say, I probably on average I'd probably get about 5 or so hours a night, and that's usually fine for me to a point. Like if I'm doing stuff in the day, but I do find that if I haven't got, necessarily got something to occupy my brain or... if it's just the right situation, I will sleep in the day sometimes and it's never usually for hours. It's normally just enough to throw me off a little bit, like an like an hour here or there or whatever, or I do find it hard sometimes to stay awake in situations like meetings. Not all the time.
But enough that it's like something that I do worry about. So yeah, my sleep at night, I'd say does make things worse because, like, I just really don't sleep very well and I'm not entirely sure why. But that does mean sometimes it's harder to get up in the morning. It's harder to motivate myself to do things. I do find that... my body doesn't feel as good when I don't sleep as well, especially with me exercising quite a lot. I don't feel like I fully get that recovery time.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. So we're now on to the section, the next section which is going to focus... on the device that you use and how you use it.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - So what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it?

Participant - So I've got a Fitbit Inspire 3. I only started using it in December, but it was something, because I used to just track like macros and exercise on like an app. But I just started using this and it's been yeah, so much easier.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Have you used any tools previously?

Participant - No, just like online apps sort of like things like my fitness power. I've like input things like my water intake and food, and like my exercise, but that isn't very accurate. So I thought this was a better bet.

Interviewer - So previous tools are more like manual reporting diaries...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...and now you've changed to a Fitbit...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...which is automatically recording.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK, how do you use this device?

Participant - So I use it for quite a few different things, so I got it originally just because I was sort of focusing a bit more on my exercise like I've been going to the gym for about nine years. But it's only in the past couple of years I'd say I've really properly got into like a serious routine.
But I'm also very aware that it is harder for me than a lot of people to, to progress in a lot of ways, so I got it originally just to check things like my steps, keep on top of my sleep because I knew that was quite a big inhibiting factor in my recovery. Things like my heart rate and calorie burn as well because like I do, I have to manage my weight quite well as well 'cos I notice if I carry extra weight, my knees just don't like it. So I've just been using it, yeah, in general, just keep an eye on my activity and just make sure I'm doing enough, but not doing too much and sort of working out which days are better for me to do certain exercises. Like if I've had a heavy day the day before, I might ease back a bit because it does... it kind of gives you advice on how much cardio and stuff to do based on what your load has been recently, so it's got a tool built in to tell you if you've you might have overtrained recently, if you might have overexerted. So I use it for that. So if it's if there's a day where it suggests that I might have overtrained a little bit recently, I'll ease off that kind of thing.

Interviewer - OK, when do you use this device or app or application? This isn't necessarily when is it collecting data, but when do you look at it? When do you think about it? When do you change actions?

Participant - So ummm, I'll-I usually check it, I check it in the mornings just to check what my sleep quality was like as well, because that's one thing, like I can sleep for a certain amount of time, but my sleep quality can often be quite low, so I use it to check that and it sort of gives you like a daily readiness score as well to say like how you know how, how well you've slept. How much you might have exercised recently just to tell you, like, how physically ready you might be for a day. So I usually have a look at that and just see, like, oh, what kind of day could it be? Like, I know I don't take it as gospel, but like, it's a good indicator. I'll usually use it maybe towards the end of the day after I've like, come home and finish what I'm doing just to check.
How many steps I've done and just make sure I've, you know, been getting enough steps in and I use it anytime I do a workout as well, so I just switch that on depending on what workout I'm doing just to keep an eye on like my metrics there and my heart rate, things like that.

Interviewer - OK, how frequently do you use or check the, the device?

Participant - I'd say like maybe, like properly check and read the metrics maybe like three times a day, but like in passing sometimes like if I notice like "Oh my heart rate feels a bit high", I'll have a look and things like that. So I'd say like 3... a proper check and a proper look at my stats on the app that's on my phone. But yeah, a couple of other times throughout the day. Just out of curiosity usually.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right, we're on to the main section. This is the last big section. This is about your device and your conditions interacting together. So can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application or previous devices and applications?

Participant - Yeah. So I've been into weightlifting like, not, not like serious lifting, but like, I just I lift weights like four to five times a week. And I've been doing it for quite a long time. But I think the main reason it spurred me on is because, like, I'm into my 30s now. So...
I've sort of noticed as well as like just having the general mobility condition, age as having a factor in it as well. I don't heal up as quick as I used to. I don't recover as quick as I used to. Some things are a bit harder like even though my joints are bendier, they're also stiffer, which I don't know if that makes sense, but like so things that used to be a lot easier for me physically and things I used to have more energy to do, I just find that I don't anymore. So I just thought it would just be a useful way just to sort of manage, because I used to just go to the gym five times a week, feel really guilty if I didn't go and smash out like an hour and a half workout multiple times a week, and I wasn't really listening to my body and I just noticed that... the further on I got, the more it was setting me back, so like I'd have a week where I'd do loads of really heavy workouts and I'd feel great about that. And then the next week I'd barely be able to move. I'd feel absolutely awful. I wouldn't be recovering because my sleep was bad, so I just thought it was, I'd rather... find something manageable and sustainable for me so I can still stay active without aggravating the condition and without making myself feel worse. So it just seemed like a good starting point to, to start managing it a little bit better and a bit more sustainably. Now I'm 30 and I'll probably, well, 33 and I'll probably end up with arthritis like my mum so...
Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thanks very much. I am going to quickly loop back and just ask you a question because you didn't mention anything that tipped my hand on this before, but you mentioned a heavy workout week and then not being able to do anything the following week.

Participant - Oh yeah.

Interviewer - Extended exhaustion or post exertional malaise or something similar, is that something you experienced?

Participant - It can be if I push myself too hard, I feel like I've learned my limits quite a lot. But there were times when I mean, and I do think this this isn't like just a mobility thing. This is also a mental thing because I used to have, like, really quite a bad body dysmorphia as well. So I had a lot of disordered eating, a lot of obsessions with exercise, so, a lot of what I would do would be get really obsessed with something, push myself and push myself and push myself to try and see results. And then the week after I'd just be like absolutely written off, so that didn't help. Like that was one thing that like it didn't help because that was a bit of like my other sort of issues coming into play, I suppose.
But yeah, I would. It was, it used to get quite bad at times. If I was pushing myself every week, I'd do like a really big workout, then I'd be like "Aw, I need to lift even heavier tomorrow", and I'd just be working the same muscle groups over and over and it would just absolutely like, write me off. So sometimes I'd have to take like a couple of weeks off the gym and just, like, do gentle walking instead.
Just to try and stay active.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right, back to where we were before. Can you give me examples of the times that the device has alerted you to overexertion? This doesn't necessarily have to be it pinging on your wrist and telling you you're doing too much.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - At the end of the day, if you look at it and go "oh wow, I've done too much" that counts.

Participant - I've literally got one from yesterday, so this I mean this one isn't entirely just linked to my joint condition. I've basically I gave blood. Was it yesterday or the day before, day before and I've done it loads of times before like it's fine. But they said after 24 hours you know you should be recovered. So I went to the gym last night and I noticed my heart rate was, I was like, oh, I don't, don't feel 100% yet. And I did check on my heart rate was way higher than it normally would be for that kind of exercise. And that is another thing I do have to keep an eye on my heart rate because with some connective tissue disorders, it can affect your heart valves. So I have to be really careful. And also I have to, I get screened every two years for a hereditary heart condition that my dad had, and the only way to find it is through an MRI. So I have to be really careful with my heart because that's the sort of thing that could come on at any time anyway. So that's like, it's all kind of linked, but also kind of not. But yeah, when I was mid exercise yesterday, it did flag up that my heart rate was quite elevated compared to normal and the variability was quite high, so that, at that point I was like, I probably need to stop because I noticed my body was feeling a little bit fatigued anyway and I thought that's probably a good indication I might be over exerting myself. And yeah, there's been times where because a lot, I do log all my workouts.
Just so I can like get an accurate picture of when I need to rest and there will be times where it said, "oh you've, you know, you've pushed yourself recently, try and ease off on, on things a little bit" and that's when I might take like an unscheduled rest day. If it's like picked up on a few things because it can pick up on things like you know like your variability in your heart rate, and how much you've done. And usually I try and listen to that and I think because I don't really always know my own, I don't know myself if I've done that much.

Interviewer - Oh, there we go.

Participant - Oh, it's back.

Interviewer - Sorry about that.

Participant - That's all good.

Interviewer - The joys of working from home.

Participant - Yeah, I can relate.

Interviewer - Right, so it can keep track of variability, unscheduled rest days...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...and I think that's about where you dropped out.

Participant - Yeah, I think that's the main thing for me really. It's just that I'm not always aware myself of when I've pushed a little too hard. So it's kind of good to have that reminder because it kind of gets ahead of it a little bit because I'm... because I do really, I do really enjoy exercising. It's just sometimes I don't realise how hard I'm pushing myself, so it's nice to have something that sort of picks up on... you know, metrics within my body or activity patterns because then like I know for most people it might just be like, "oh, I'm going to be a bit tired", but for me, there's a difference between just being a bit tired and just feeling absolutely horrendous. So even though I know it's not intended to be like, "oh, your hypermobility is going to act up" for me, that's a pretty good like... indicator that I do need to back off like it's not just that I'm going to be tired. It's like I'm going to feel awful.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. All right, so that was the over exertion question, yes?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yeah, OK, perfect. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Yeah. So I've actually started, since doing it, because I realised that I wasn't doing enough... that was like... like, not cardio, but things that were just a little bit more, because it's I think, it's still, it's still good to do things like that... I realised that you know I wasn't... I don't really care about necessarily about calorie burn as such, but like I say, it's more, it's important I do manage my weight as well because like when I've carried extra weight in the past and I don't, I don't care about what it looks like. It's just the fact it absolutely wrecks my knees. So it was things like I was just realising that I wasn't really... actually getting enough movement in in general like I was lifting weights, but I wasn't actually moving enough, so I started doing Pilates, since I had it and it's kind of helped with that because I can sort of manage like my activity there and it's a bit more gentle as well than weightlifting. And to be fair, I went last time I went to a doctor who told me Pilates would be really good for joint conditions because it basically builds all the stabilising muscles that you don't hit with other workouts and keeps your joints a bit more in place. So that's something I've started doing because I realised that I did have a bit more room for something that was not just lifting weights all the time and something, you know, it'd be good to do something that sort of regulate my breathing and my heart rate.
So started doing that.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device or previous applications?

Participant - Yes, I mean, I've not stopped as such, but I've changed the way I do my workouts. I used to really, really push myself on super heavyweights and I used to just want to lift heavier and heavier and heavier. And I've sort of changed the way I exercise now, so I do it a lot more intuitively.
And based a lot more on listening to my body rather than just being like "oh, I need to lift weights, I have to go to the gym." So I've just been rather than doing it out of habit, I've sort of been doing it based on what my body needs and it's just been really helpful in supporting with that. So like I say it's... I don't feel guilty about taking a rest day anymore because having the tracker kind of like made me realise sometimes I have pushed myself a little bit and it's like the kindest thing to do for my body, or it might have made me realise that, like I need to actually go to bed a bit earlier instead of dragging myself to the gym. So it's more the fact I've sort of like... I'm still doing the same things just differently like my... I've restructured my workout so I don't focus on the same things anymore. I don't just focus on lifting heavy weights, I just focus on building like functional strength in my joints and doing exercises like I say, like Pilates that are a bit more stabilising and not quite as strenuous.
So that's, I'd say probably more have changed it than stopped anything.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activity you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - I probably would have wanted to start running, but instead like I just walk more now because I think I've realised that sometimes my heart rate variability and like and how much it I am prone to over exertion, I just thought it was... it was probably not the best idea because it was something last year, all my friends do park runs and stuff. They all absolutely love it and everyone was like, "oh, you should come for a run." And I just realised that it's just probably not for me because like, if just normal, a normal, a normal day for like a normal person can be over exerting for me, adding extra strain onto my body and my joints is probably not a good idea.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. And last one for this section. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - Ummm...
Oh, I'm not sure I understand the question fully.

Interviewer - So the, the joys of academic wording of questions.

Participant - Ha ha.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of when you have felt... vindicated in choosing to use the device in the way that you do.

Participant - Oh, OK, yeah, definitely. I mean, yeah, yesterday was a big one, to be fair, because I know that a while ago, I would have just thought, "oh, it's totally fine to feel lightheaded and dizzy and like, I'm going to pass out in the middle of the workout. That's totally normal." And I just would have, like, I wouldn't even... sort of realised how much of an effect it was having on my heart because I knew I felt off, but I know I probably would have pushed through that before I would have just been like, "oh, it's just normal", which isn't really. I just had a really unhealthy relationship with exercise. But when I saw, when I looked at my heart rate because I'm so used to knowing what it is just from my Fitbit and from checking my work out and... knowing like what my peak is, seeing it that much over I was like, "Oh yeah, this is a stupid idea. Like I need to go home." So that was like, that was a big one for me. And also just in terms of little things like, say, like giving myself rest days and knowing that it's OK to do that was a big thing because I was like, I used to struggle with that so bad. And now it's like if-if I've had, if I look back over the past few days and I have overexerted, I'm like it is more important that I let myself rest and recover than it is for me to just smash in another workout for no reason. So yeah, just in general, it's kind of helped.
Me re-evaluate my relationship with exercise and my body and just manage everything a lot better.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. So three final questions. These are more speculative.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - They don't necessarily have to be grounded in reality. You can come up with something sci-fi if you want.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So what physical form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Like as in like a watch kind of thing or...

Interviewer - Yeah. So is it.. is it a wristband, an application, an anklet, something attached to the ear? Is it not something physically attached to the body at all? Go wild.

Participant - OK. I mean I, I mean I-I quite like this except my problem is because my I move around a lot it does slip off quite a bit. So I mean if we're going to go sci-fi, surely an implant would be like absolutely genius just to track everything. But as it is, I mean, I know there's options for a ring. I think the ring is like probably quite cool, but I'm also fine because of my how my hands are. I have a lot of sensory issues with wearing jewellery and stuff. I don't tend to wear jewellery as much as I can because like, I get so many sensory issues just from things being on my skin so I don't know. For me, yeah, if someone could just like stick some kind of implant in that tracks everything and feeds it to my phone. That would be genius. I don't have to think about again, but, but as it is, I mean, I think the band is pretty functional in as much as I can just take it off. I can see it a lot easier. So yeah, I mean, I say those kind of things be what I think of like I do... I do enjoy just being able to look at everything at a glance and the app on the phone is really useful as well because I mean, everyone's got the phones on them these days, haven't they? So it's, it's pretty easy.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove?

Participant - I mean, I don't think there's any that I'd necessarily remove. I think there's some that I don't really understand or pay attention to like the, the oxygen saturation levels and stuff. I don't really, haven't really got my head around that. So because I think mine is probably quite basic compared to some of them.
With it being my first one, I thought if I like it I'll, I'll maybe upgrade to something. I'm just going to open the app now and see which ones I don't use so yeah...

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - ...so the I mean I love the-the workout tracking function. I think that's really good. Yeah. This one like calculates, you know, how many minutes in a day you should be active based on like your readiness level, your recent activity levels and that's, I like that.
What don't I like? Yeah, skin temperature variation. I don't really use that. I don't really know anything about that.
I say the only thing that I don't necessarily like is sometimes it does buzz and tell me I need to move every now and again at times, and it's like that's a little thing that I'm like, you know, every day is different. I know it's just set to, like hourly. Remind me to get up and move. But some days, if I'm like, really busy or I'm really tired, I'm like, it feels like it's guilt tripping me. That's maybe one thing that I would probably get rid of.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - I'm sure there's a way to switch that off.

Interviewer - OK and what functions would you add?

Participant - Is this? Is this another one where I can do like something kind of sci-fi and like in an ideal world kind of thing.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So one thing I'm always...

Interviewer - You can do you... You can do things but grounded in reality all the way up to just oh, it would be wonderful if you could track my fatigue and tell me when I'm fatigued. Magically somehow.

Participant - Incredible. Yes. I mean that would be great. That would be amazing because like I do, I do think like for me so far, the readiness levels have been pretty accurate to a point. I mean, I know you can't always like take that as gospel because some days that'll say my daily readiness is 0 because I've barely slept. But I'll feel great. And that's just because I don't know, like I, some days I won't sleep at all and I'll somehow be super functional and alert. But one thing I would like and I know they can do this in like, I think the specific machines you can do it and I don't know how a fitness tracker would do it. But one thing that would be really useful for me is... body composition, scanning, seeing your muscle mass, things like that. Because I know that there's some areas that it's more important for me to build muscle than others. But I also know I do struggle with that. So things like my shoulders, where I really need them to, to stay in place or around my knees and my legs where you know, it takes the, the blood, the most of the strain, they're the ones where I'd really find it useful to be able to track my progress. Because I can't do that through strength because I'm never going to be able to lift above a certain amount, so I can't be like, oh, you know, it's getting stronger because I'm lifting more. I'd need to see, like, have I built more muscle around that area.
You know, is it able to support my joints in the way that I need to, to stop me being at risk of like, more of a dislocation? So that for me would be useful, just have a look at my body composition just to make sure I'm like maintaining muscle mass because that's one thing that is really important to me is because just because my joints aren't strong enough to...to stay where they're supposed to by themselves. So I've got to build those muscles and I've got to keep everything, everything strong. Just so I don't fall apart, basically.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So that would be really useful to sort of get a picture of my muscle mass and body composition. If that was a thing that could be like... But yeah, the fatigue thing, like, I mean, I don't, I think I'm not sure whether mine does or not like, the kind of functionality where it can detect if you have like any sort of little arrhythmias, things like that. Like I don't know if any of them are sophisticated enough, but I just know that... you know, if there were any abnormalities and heart rhythm rather than rate, that could be quite useful as well because I know that like, say, a lot of connective tissue disorders are linked to, like, problems with heart valves as well. And especially like, I think that's the kind of thing that'd be useful to anyone with those kind of disorders. But especially like, as someone who has to be investigated for a valve disorder from a completely separate thing. It would just be really useful to know that kind of thing, because then you don't have to wait two years for an MRI. You could just, you know, contact your GP and be like, oh, hey, I've had a bit of a an arrhythmia. So that would be quite interesting.
I'm trying to think of anything else.
Oh, I don't really know. I suppose if you had an implant you could check things like hormone levels as well. Because I mean, I don't really know anything about my hormones, to be honest. I probably should at some point. But I know that that would be quite an interesting thing to sort of track as well because like, I'm on the contraceptive pill as well. And like, I don't necessarily know if it impacts anything to do with hypermobility, but I know that has like a tonne of other effects as well. So yeah, things like that. But I think yeah, one of the main ones for me would be like body composition and muscle mass if there was ever a way to do that because that's just so important for me. And just like keeping my body functional. But the fatigue thing as well because, yeah, I think, I'm new-, I'm not so bad when I've got things to do. But sometimes, like when I'm just in the middle of the day and I just cannot do anything and I just, you know, if I could sort of pre-empt those sort of days...

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - And-and know when not just physically, but like mentally to back off a bit because it does tell me when not to over exert myself, but you don't always know when fatigue is going to hit you or brain fog is going to hit you...

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - ...or when you're going to be sat at your desk and thinking, "Oh my God, like I literally can't do anything and it's stressing me out" so... yeah, something like that.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much. Right. I'm just going to do a quick skim over everything, just in case you've got anything to add or if it reminds you of anything.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So the first section was about your conditions. So your conditions, your symptoms and relating to activities and onset. The second section was about your device when you started using it. How frequently, what for. The third section was these two things interacting so around activities that you... started or stopped because of it, or activities that made you decide to start using the application and then the last three of those speculative futuristic device ones.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So is there anything you'd like to add at all?

Participant - I can't think of anything. Is it something I can get in touch with you? Like further down the line? If I think of anything.

Interviewer - Absolutely.

Participant - Brilliant. OK.

Interviewer - If I haven't like, got 3/4s of the way through the paper and someone emails me 3 weeks later and goes "Oh my God I've just remembered this" then yeah, I'll try...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...I'll absolutely do my best to work that work that in and all.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - I'm never going to say no to something like that.

Participant - OK.
Brilliant.

Interviewer - Because obviously again, brain fog memory issues.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - These are frequent things for people like that.

Participant - Yeah, it's so frustrating. 'cause sometimes, like, I'll think of something absolutely brilliant, like ages after. I'm like, "oh, no."

Interviewer - Yeah, it's, it's that, it's that old joke of "I have come up with a fantastic comeback three years after the argument happened." But with everything all of the time, every day.

Participant - Yeah.
Honestly, my notes app has just become my best friend now for everything like...

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - ...I've got so many notes that just don't make sense to anyone else because I just had a thought and I'm like, I need to put that down now because I'm not going to remember that in about an hour's time.

Interviewer - Yeah. Yeah. OK. Right. If...if there's nothing you'd like to add, I'll stop the recording, if that's alright?

Participant - OK, Yep, no problem.

title: P08 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - Today is dread day apparently. Right. OK. And then the, the last one we're recording now.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - The last one has a pleasant, I suppose name the sleepiness scale. So this is.

Participant - Sleepiness!

Interviewer - I'm spelling those wrong again. This is how likely you are to fall asleep or doze in everyday situations.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And it's a scale of zero to three. I'm just going to write down there. They are a description of what each number means, and this is just in general, so this is not along any sort of time scale.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - This is if you were in this situation today or on your average day...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...what is your judgement of about how sleepy you probably would be?

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So...
The first situation is sitting and reading, zero to three, as in the chat.

Participant - 3. I was asleep and reading a book.

Interviewer - 3, watching TV.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - 2.
Sitting inactive in a public place, for example, a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero, as a passenger in a car for an hour without a brake.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero, lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3. Sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - Uhhh, 0.

Interviewer - 0. Sitting quietly after lunch brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, and in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero.
OK. And that is all of your surveys done.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - Wonderful. I can close that now, right.
What we're looking for? Do I have it open? I don't. Lovely. Thank you me. Bear with me a second.

Participant - That's fine. I need to. I'm just going to grab my laptop charger. I've just realised it's dying so.

Interviewer - Yep, no problem. Take your time.

Participant - That wouldn't be helpful.

Interviewer - No, it's fine. I will, I will warn you, both of the last two interviews I've done, my internet's dropped halfway through for about two minutes, so that might happen. I'm very sorry for that.

Participant - Don't worry, that's fine.

Interviewer - Right. If you are ready for the, the main bit?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. So the first bit is about the conditions and the symptoms and the like...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...not about the devices yet.

Participant - Yes, OK.

Interviewer - So, the first question what chronic mobility condition or conditions do you have?

Participant - So I have Hypermobile ehlers danlos syndrome and I have a comorbidity of that, so it's caused by my eds and called pots, which is postural, orthostatic tachycardia. And so the EDS causes it's a connected tissue disorder, so...things like my blood vessels, my skin, my ligaments, they're all too stretchy than they should be. So it means my joints dislocate quite easily. So my fingers, my wrists, my shoulders, my knees, they're like the worst ones. So it means that my muscles have to work...10 times harder to kind of do the jobs that my ligaments should be doing. So that's one of the contributing factors to my fatigue is the fact that my muscles, they can never be off. Otherwise I fall apart like Lego like it's, it's a bit annoying, but the other side of it is the pots. So, what that means is because my blood vessels are rubbish, when you stand up normally they constrict to kind of force the blood back up to your head. Mine doesn't do that. So because my blood vessels are really lax, there's nothing forcing the blood back up to my head. So my heart rate goes up to compensate for it.
And so that means it's quite fatiguing because if even if my rest and heart rate is about 65, it can go up to 140, 145 while I'm just standing like, so it feels like you're running a marathon when I'm literally just standing in the till waiting to go to Asda or something. But the knock on effect of that is one that makes you way more tired two, but it also effects things like your digestion, so you can't refuel as effectively, which contributes to fatigue too. And one of the frustrating things is the medications that I have to take to maintain my pots which help my blood pressure means I can't lie down at certain points during the day.
So, like even when you're feeling tired and stuff and you're like, oh, just a little nap would help me out. Like, you can't. Because if it messes with your blood pressure a lot. So it means that you're constantly having to manage that fatigue constantly throughout the day with medication, feeding, like how your joints are going. So, all of them kind of contribute to you just kind of constantly being tired throughout the day. But yeah, it's a, it's a bit of a amalgamation of chronic illnesses that backfire and so on.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - Welcome.

Interviewer - I think that is the case for everyone. I'm going to be honest, me, me included, never one thing on its own.

Participant - Yeah. No, it's never simple, is it?

Interviewer - No, it never is. One thing causes another, causes another, etcetera. Can you-

Participant - The doctors just look at you mad.

Interviewer - Yeah! Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experienced?

Participant - Yeah. So for my ehlers danlos syndrome, the biggest symptom is joint pain and that can be caused by just the instability in general or the actual dislocations and stuff. The main symptom for my pots is the dizziness and the nausea that comes from it. So they're, they're the ones that follow me around the majority of the day.
But all of those kind of compound with me being quite fatigued, so I'd say like it's probably like third, like my fatigue is like third in that kind of hierarchy of crap. But yeah, so there's my main symptoms I think.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - Yeah, so I struggle for standing up for a long period of time, so I'm OK for the 1st 15 minutes or so, but once we get going like past 15 minutes, my heart rate starts to climb. Like really, really quickly and I start to get problems with my knees. So I'm more likely to dislocate my knees just from standing if I've already been standing for around half an hour to an hour.
So it means things like if I'm going out with my friends somewhere, if it's, if there's a chance that we might not be able to be sit down like say, if it's a busy bar or something, that kind of rules that out for me. I love going to music festivals and stuff, but that's been really difficult in the past few years because I can't stand up to enjoy it.
And things like sports I used to do. I was the captain of the [sport] club in the UNI for a while, but I've not been able to do that recently because of the fatigue and the dizziness, making it just impossible for me to [participate].

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - I was 14, I think, I dislocated my shoulder and I trapped a nerve in my neck in the same, like by dislocating my shoulder so I couldn't move my entire like left arm. I could only like move my wrist. So even though it was just a shoulder injury like my whole, my entire arm had gone completely numb as well. So that was the first kind of like I suppose, major impact of-of that condition, yeah.

Interviewer - And like you said, because of the hyper mobility that is more impactful as a dislocation than the average person and has a longer recovery time.

Participant - Yeah. Yeah. So when you for a normal person, just like it's the shoulder, it does cause like a lot of trauma to the joint and stuff. But with the issue with me, it's because my ligaments are so long, it doesn't take a lot of force for me to knock it out, especially once you've already done it the once. So I did it the first time. I used to do horse riding, which was like, OK, that affect that, that warrants dislocation, that was a good reason for it. But then the months following it, it was, if I sneezed, if I rolled over too hard. When you walk in the corridor in school, if someone like someone's coat caught onto your backpack, that would send it out. It was every little thing would make it pop out, and it was just it became more of an inconvenience the longer it went on, but also means that traditional treatments like surgery aren't an option when you've got hypermobility, because it's just not effective.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Reading between the lines here with more difficult recovery, would you describe extended exhaustion or something like post exertional malaise being something you experience?

Participant - Yeah, massively. So I think that's actually more of a problem with my pots and stuff. So whenever I have anything like big submissions to do in work for my PhD, one of the roles that I have to do is we take all our students to Wales to go for [testing].
And that's like a two week thing. Where was I was having to sleep in my car and do all of this teaching. But then for about a month afterwards, I couldn't get out of bed. I could couldn't keep food down like it was like the worst flu or covid ever. And it was just from the exertion of doing that.

Interviewer - OK, so I'm going to ask the question in its proper form. I didn't, even though you technically answered it. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity? If there aren't any more than just say so.

Participant - Yeah. So there's one like where maybe in like getting like flu, like symptoms and stuff after like major exertion and stuff. But even down to if I take a shower, I can't just immediately go and get dressed like without fainting or I feel like incredibly out of breath so, if I have a shower I have to like sit down for about half an hour afterwards. Same as like eating a big meal. If it's anything more than like half a sandwich, it takes me like a good 45 minutes just to digest it. And then even after that like a good hour and a half afterwards. I'm still really slow and it I wouldn't be able to eat something and then go out for a walk for example that that would just be too much.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - Yeah, being like my doing my PhD is probably like a hard one. So when I've been struggling with having a flare up, it makes it really hard, especially when you're kind of navigating, when do you start coming back to work following like a big flare up of an illness?
Is my brain is soup for days like I can't, I can't even remember my laptop password half the time, let alone what was supposed to be doing with [subject] and all stuff like that. I'm like, that's just that's out the question entirely, when I've got brain fog.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you. And last one on the condition exclusively area...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Yeah. So one of the big things with like digestion, if I eat too close to bed, I can be sick. So when you have ehlers danlos syndrome, there's a condition that sometimes comes alongside it called gastroparesis.
Which is when, when your food enters your stomach, it's like a big bag and it does all the mashing to get it digesting and stuff, and typically for people with eds you won't have that. So the food just sits there in your stomach. It means that when you eat, you start to feel like satiated really, really quickly. So like one bite of food and you'll go, "Oh my God, I feel like I've eaten like a whole roast dinner", but that means that if I eat around bedtime one, I can't get to sleep because I feel like I've-I've eaten enough to pop. But sometimes, even if I do manage to get to sleep, I'll wake up either feeling really nauseous or actually being sick because of eating before bed and stuff so it can cause quite a lot of disruption to my sleep.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. Right. This second section is focusing on the device or application...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...or devices or applications that you are using or have used.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So question one, what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - So this is, I use an Apple Watch series four and I use this app on it called TachyMon which monitors my heart rate and it gives more frequent, it measures it more frequently than the Apple's own one does, that tends to update like every couple of minutes, whereas this one does it like every few secondsA, and it's really useful because especially when my heart rate's creeping up, it like shouts at me. And so it has, like, one where it's like a little high but not really high. So I think I've set it, so my first alarm comes off when my heart rate's between 110 and 130, that's like your "OK you need to think about sitting down like, you know, kind of be careful. Like, yeah, your amber light sort of. And then when it goes above 140, that's my "Right, sit down. Stop what you're doing. This is, you know, you've got to pack it in now", but it's really useful for me monitoring what I'm doing.
Or especially like in a situation when I'm in work. So I do a lot of like GTA teaching. It might be that throughout the day my tolerance for getting up and getting down gets worse throughout the day and if I'm not necessarily paying attention to that, it's useful for having to have my watch go "OK, by the way, your hearts creeping up a bit, you need to slow down and stuff" just because I might sometimes feel OK doesn't mean my body's reacting that way. And when I ignore it, it usually results in me feel either fainting or something a bit later on, or feeling really sick. So.

Interviewer - OK. And just for clarification on why you're using not the Apple one, if my understanding of pots is correct, the Apple device can miss spikes in your heart rate...

Participant - Yeah, it takes an-

Interviewer - ...because of that reduced rate of recording.

Participant -  Yeah, it takes like a mean over every 60 seconds. So if you're, so my heart rate can spike within that minute and it will take the average of whatever my baseline was before I stood up.
And then what it's settling to afterwards, but it very rarely captures that peak where it might go to 140-150, whereas this TachyMon I think it does it every like 3 or 4 seconds. So it's giving you a more constant update of what's going and it is able to capture those peaks from when I stand up.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thanks very much. Have you used any previous tools like this?

Participant - I just used the general apple one for a while and that was good for spotting like sustained high heart rates and stuff like if it was consistently like above a certain point. But it's not good for the peaks and stuff, but it's mainly just those two.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. How do you use this device or application?

Participant - So typically on a day where I know I'm going to be on my feet for a bit is usually when I prioritise wearing it. So when I'm doing teaching and work, is usually the big one. Or if I'm going to shops like, doing grocery shopping, that kind of thing. Those are the situations where I wear it the most just to keep me aware of my heart rate because I'm more likely to, to be distracted by something and not necessarily thinking about my symptoms.

Interviewer - OK, you've inadvertently answered question... The next question and not...technically answered the intent of this question, so I'll reword it. What do you...Oh, oh, words words. I've lost the word suddenly.
What are you looking for when you use this device or application? Like what features do you use and what's the intent behind you using those features?

Participant - Yeah so...
So I use it to look at my baseline for the day. So you're like my heart rate, my normal heart rate throughout the day will be different for a day where I'm working at home versus one where I'm working like in the university working. So it's useful for me spotting my, my baseline for that day and it usually changes based on whether I've had a flare up too.
So I can kind of gauge, OK, am I getting back to normal and stuff by my mean heart rate throughout the day. So that's one reason I use it. But the major thing that I'm looking out for and what I'm using the alerts from these apps is those notifications of the extremely high heart rates and it either means that I've not taken my medication, I've not eaten properly. I'm not hydrated or I'm pushing it, so it makes me go "Let's reassess the situation" and, you know, try and sort that heart rate out of it.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
I, obviously the way that you're describing it, this is different to how I know Apple intends it.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - I don't know what the intent with them TachyMon is.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Why do you operate outside of the manufacturer scope?

Participant - Just for understanding my own illness, so I understand it's not supposed to be used for... I know you get the alerts from Apple Watch saying like it's not for looking at heart attacks like you know, but even though that's not what the manufacturer recommends it for, I've actually had a lot of good feedback from doctors and stuff through the stuff that I've been able to record on my Apple Watch. So I struggled with symptoms of EDS and pots from the age of 14. I didn't get diagnosed with EDS until I was maybe 19 or 20, and it took my pots to get diagnosed a few years after that, even though the doctors suspected I had it right then when I was 14 and 15, that's just how long it takes to get diagnosed, so in terms of building up that evidential base to say, look, you might not spot it when I come into the-the doctor's office for 15 minutes and you, you check my heart rate while I've been sat down for half an hour and it's fine. But you're not looking at every single time throughout the day where I'm getting those big spikes. It was useful for me to bring that to the doctor and go "This isn't normal", like you know, and try and force them to make that referral from that instance instead of being fobbed off by you've got anxiety and you're a woman, are you sure you're not pregnant and all that kind of stuff. So kind of force them to listen to me and help me advocate for myself a bit better because even though I know my symptoms quite often, the doctors are quite easy to fob you off but having it written down on paper, no, this is what's happening. It just meant that it-it was more difficult for them to pop me off in that instance.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. And last question of this section.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - How frequently do you use this device or applications? So that's not just how frequently do you have it on...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...but also how frequently are you checking it?

Participant - So I wear the watch every day and I check, check it every morning just to kind of see what my body's up to that morning. And usually the frequency from then depends on how I'm feeling. So it would be most likely if I'm a day where I'm out and I'm doing quite a lot.
It'll probably be every hour that I'm checking in on it to see unless I have like a big spike in symptoms all of a sudden. It's usually once an hour.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. This next section is the last big section.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - This is the device and the conditions interacting together.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - So can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application?

Participant - So I used to do a lot of weight lifting, so that was kind of why I got the Apple Watch to begin with. And one thing that I was starting to notice was it was kind of alerting to me to really, really high heart rates even when I was working out. So that was when I started kind of using it and based on, because I was constantly going to the doctor and stuff, I think it was on the advice of one of them where they were saying make sure you're monitoring it outside of you working out too because they were concerned that it could have been like... so pots is like an autonomous nervous system problem. It's to do with my nerves not telling my heart to beat properly. But there are lots of like cardiac conditions to do with the structure of your heart that can be caused by EDS.
So that was actually why they investigated it to begin with, was they were concerned that there might have been issues with like the valves in my heart and stuff. But when they looked into it, thankfully that wasn't the case. I had to have, like, lots of ECG's and stuff. But that's when they were able to go, "OK, these, this is happening throughout the day" and kind of forced them to investigate further, but yeah, that's-that's kind of how I started using it.

Interviewer - OK. Thanks very much. Can you give me examples, you've already tapped into this a little bit, can you give me examples of times the device or application has alerted you to over exertion? This isn't just those alarm bells that go off.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - This is, for example, at the end of a long day...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...and then you look back on the data and you go oh wow, that was a lot.

Participant - Yeah. So sometimes when I've not necessarily realised that I've been in a flare up like because the symptoms can, when you feel crap constantly, it's hard to tell sometimes when you're feeling more crap than usual because you're just like, oh, whatever.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - But one of the things is my mean heart rate creeps up and up and up, when I'm having a flare up. So when I'm having a flare up my resting heart rate is close to 100 when I'm lying flat down. So when I start to see that baseline number get a little bit higher, that tends to be like a key that like, that's one thing that I'm looking out for.
And not necessarily when I've got like alerts coming to, but sometimes when I'm sat down, it can just be on like, oh, you know, am I just anxious or something like, you know, what's going on? And like, sometimes it is just anxiety and whatever. And if I look at my watch and my heart rate's fine and everything, I'm like, OK, you know, go and do some journaling. Go for a walk.
You know, address the mental side of it, but it helps me like check, you know, reaffirm to myself, "OK am I feeling symptoms from, like, mental and stuff like that? Do I need to just kind of self-care" or is it "No I'm-I'm starting to feel poorly here and I need to kind of, you know address that and, and make sure I'm eating or drinking or whatever to sort that out."

Interviewer -  OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device or application?

Participant - Yeah, so I tried doing more cardio and stuff to see if bringing down like my baseline and heart rate would help with these and if it would help with my sensitivity to like my heart to see if the, if working on my cardio would help that sensitivity to like, you know, exercise and stuff like that in general, because I've always done more like powerlifting sports, which by the nature of it mean that your heart rate goes crazy high and stuff. So I did try looking into cardio to see if that would help, but I found that the cardio was harder than the weight lifting and stuff because at least you can sit down in between doing the weights and stuff like, you know, you're only having to maintain that effort for a really short amount of time, and it's actually the duration of those activities that take more out of me rather than the intensity of them. So I can do quite intense stuff, just not for very long, but I can, I can't even do low intensity stuff for very long anyway, so...it's, it's the endurance that's the problem for me.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you've stopped participating in as a result of using the device or application?

Participant - Cooking. Cooking was definitely a really big one that would usually, because I'm doing it at the end of the day and I would get massive spikes in my heart rate. And because I was putting myself through that by the time I actually get down to sit and eat a meal, I feel so sick I can't eat it. So I've been quite fortunate that my partner, he does like a lot of the cooking in the house and stuff, but that took a long time to figure out and that was partly through using the Apple Watch to be like, why is it I feel absolutely awful every single time I sit down and eat my tea like. I can't eat it, I can't finish it. I've spent all this time to, like, make this nice meal and I don't even want to eat it anymore and it was noticing that, well, actually my heart rates going like a marathon. So, you know, that's no wonder you feel like crap by the time you get down to eat your tea, you're not going to be in the mood for, fajitas or whatever.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device or application?
