Participant - I probably would have kept doing things like jiu jitsu and quite intense weight lifting, but based on how my heart rate was reacting to it, I felt like it wasn't sustainable. So like my heart rate was going regularly over 180 while I was doing jiu jitsu and while I was doing weight lifting.
And while I was like, I still do weightlifting, the jiu jitsu was hard to keep going because the nature of it, you throw someone, you end up on the floor, you have to get back up again. And it's the getting back up that was really, really difficult. Not, not throwing people, not being thrown. That was fine. It was the standing up constantly that was dead hard. The weightlifting I had to stop it in the way that I was doing before, so like pure power lifting all the time just isn't feasible for me anymore. And because it was just my heart rate was too high and it was putting too much strain on my heart.
But I still do it. It's just I've had to, you know, modify how I do it so I can still participate in it because it's a sport that I love. So I still like doing powerlifting and that, but I have to, I can't do it to the extent that it used to.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - Yeah. So I went to last year, I went to download festival and usually I wouldn't really consider bringing something like an Apple Watch to the festival, just in case it was like something that damaged and whatever, because that just seems like a bit silly.

Interviewer - Or it gets, or it gets nicked, which it probably would.

Participant - Yeah, yeah. But like it was, it was really useful for me to kind of go "Right OK you need to sit down now" because as much as my heart goes, "get in the mosh pit!" My brains like, "That's probably not a good idea" like.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - But also like even, it was my boyfriend was holding me accountable too. So I'm like "oh, I want to go and do this!" And he's like, "What does your watch say?" And I'm like it says I need to sit down like so, no, it's good for I suppose helping me like last throughout the day making sure I'm not like...you know blowing through all my energy too early just because I do get excited and like in the moment you kind of forget that you're struggling with a chronic illness. And I keep thinking of, oh, a 13 year old [me] would be like straight into the mosh pit and it would be fine. And I'm like no. But you're not 13 anymore. Like, you know, it hurts so.
Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. So I think it's fair to say then pacing is an issue?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - And the watch helps with pacing?

Participant - Yes, massively.

Interviewer - OK, I need to look into that actually.

Participant - You should. It, it's really, really useful.

Interviewer - I-I don't know, I don't know if you noticed the the mug, the cup.

Participant - Oh, I-I didn't see. No. Was it?

Interviewer - Bloodstock.

Participant - Oh, I love it. Go ahead.

Interviewer - This year and last, but I collapsed on the way back on public transport this year, so...

Participant - Oh, bless you. Oh, my God. Oh. No, when I went to download, my partner couldn't believe it, because even though there's like planes taking off, like every 30 minutes and stuff, I was getting a full, like, 11 hours of kip every night. And he's like, "How?" And I'm like, "Because I'm exhausted."

Interviewer - Yeah.
Absolutely shattered, yeah.

Participant - I was like literally as soon as you get in he's like, "Aw, do you want to go to like one of the tents?" I'm like straight out. I was conked out.

Interviewer - Yeah, I'm not going to go into detail obviously because of recordings, but my camp is a very big camp and a very large, a very loud camp, but I will conk out at midnight until 9:00 in the morning and none of them understand how.

Participant - Yeah.
I've like, honestly, I don't think I ever slept like such a baby as I have in a field covered in mud and people screaming and falling on the tent.

Interviewer - And then you and then you get back home to your lovely bed with all your covers, and you can't sleep properly.

Participant - I know. Yeah, I'm just kind of like how I'm like, I was literally sleeping on rocks, and I was doing better than this, like.

Interviewer - Oh gods, we, we love the struggle.

Participant - We do.

Interviewer - OK, yeah, I know. I'll have to look into that because I've got Bloodstock tickets for next year.

Participant - Yeah. No, it's just helpful just to be like, OK, like, you know, mentally, you're like, oh, yeah, I want to go for it. But I'm like, I might feel is my body in the same like, are we in the same frame here, like, just kind of helps you, like, take everything into context and stuff?

Interviewer - Yeah, because like an individual gig, you can you can jump in the pit at a gig...

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - ...and you just get home and you'll feel it six hours later. But six hours later, you're in bed, so it's fine.

Participant - Yes. Yeah. When you're at a festival and it's 3 days of it.

Interviewer - But the four day festival...

Participant - Yeah, especially because it was for me, like, my partner doesn't drive. So it's like I'm gonna drive home. So I'm like, there's no good if I'm like, you know, in a in a bag of bones on the floor. He's gonna be like, "Well, we're screwed!"

Interviewer - Umm, right, OK. Last 3 questions...

Participant - Mmhmm.

Interviewer - ...to stop yapping about festivals, as fun as it is.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - These are more speculative...

Participant - Mmhmm.

Interviewer - It's like a different section. You can go full sci-fi if you want, but, if you want to go full sci-fi, I'd appreciate like a variety of answers.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - You can...maybe some people will ignore the sci-fi answer, but you know, obviously say what is most useful to you.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Umm.
And you don't even need to necessarily think about other conditions and the like.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Just what is useful to you as much as you can think. What form would your ideal device or application take? Being a physical form?

Participant - So having watches are really useful, but like I do find like it gets in the way quite a lot like you know, like when you're trying to write, it's like constantly on the bashing on the desk and stuff. I don't wear any other jewellery like apart from my nose ring, I don't have earrings, I don't have bracelets, necklaces, nothing so...for me, I'm like very aware that I've got it like on my wrist. So something if it could be more discreet in that sense would probably be like my go to in terms of it would never be off my wrist and stuff in that instance. But also I've got tattoos on my wrist. So sometimes the, the light which monitors your heart rate...doesn't work as well when you got tattoos, so I have to wear it on my right hand, which isn't ideal because that's the hand that I write with, but it's I don't have tattoos on that side. So yeah, in terms of devices that were still accurate for people with tattooed skin, that would be like a cool one or...ones it didn't have to necessarily be like, you know on your wrist because as well like when my like alarms go off, like, especially when I'm teaching, I've had students be like, "What's that blaring thing on your wrist?" And I'm like, "Oh, yeah, no worries. Just trying not to faint." Like, so, like, if it was, like, maybe not a bright flashing light on my wrist, that would be really cool. But yeah, in terms of the, it's more just the day-to-day comfort with it. That's sometimes a bit frustrating.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove?

Participant - What functions would I remove. Social media, social media notifications? Those are like the big ones that take forever to kind of sort out because it's, it's not a health device. It's like the link to your phone and stuff. And it's like, although the fact that it links to my phone is useful for checking the health information and stuff, it's sometimes all the other stuff I'm like. I don't want to see that I've got an e-mail on my thing that's monitoring my heart rate, that's not going to help my heart rate. It's like, yeah, no emails from work that's going to make it go up. But yeah, I think it's just that separating it from being a, a phone on your wrist versus a health device and stuff would probably be the big thing.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you. And last question, what functions would you add?

Participant - Monitoring breathing would probably be a big one, because they tend to kind of go hand in hand, feeling out of breath and stuff, because if your heart rate's going really quick, you know you've got problems with like oxygenation and stuff. But yeah, like being able to, like, reliably monitor breathing.
And that would be like kind of like a big set because I know a lot of things can't do. It would be blood pressure, because sometimes I'll get blood pressure drops that don't get picked up by my heart rate changing. So my heart rate stays the same, but my blood pressure absolutely tanks. And I'm looking at my watch going" Why do I feel like crap?"
And nothing's changing on the numbers and stuff. But yeah, that would probably be a big one. Was like if there was some discreet device that would monitor your blood pressure, that would be fantastic for me because it would help me instead of having to go, OK, your heart rate's only kind of half the picture. You know, it would let me get a more concrete image of what's going in my body and stuff.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Is there anything else on those speculative questions you'd like to add?

Participant - Not that I can think of.

Interviewer - OK, I'll do a quick overview of everything just in case something comes to mind.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - First section was about conditions and symptoms like brain fog, extended exhaustion, sleep disruption, etcetera. The second section was specifically about your device.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - And how you use it, when you use it, how frequently what you're looking for all of that, the third section was how they interact. So things you've stopped doing, things you've started doing, things you would have started doing etcetera, etcetera.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - And then that those last three were the speculative questions.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Anything coming to mind?

Participant - Not that I could think of, no.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much. I'm just going to stop the recording quickly.

Participant - Yeah.

title: P09 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - Hey, there we go. Right. OK. So I've put in the chat for the first survey, this is your choice of answers for every question. Again, don't think about it too much. You are comparing your last month to what your baseline is, what your normal day-to-day, you would expect to be. So is your last month better than usual, no worse than usual, worse than usual, or much worse than usual. And I'll go through with these 11 questions on the first one just quickly.
You don't need to elaborate for these. Just give me one of the options. Gut feeling. Don't overthink it and, and yeah, and that'll be the first one done over quickly, yeah, happy and understand?

Participant - Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - OK, good. Good. First question, do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Sorry?

Interviewer - Do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Yes, I do have problems with tiredness. It's like ongoing and one of my main symptoms.

Interviewer - So sorry, out of the in, the chat, I've put the four options better than usual, no worse, worse than usual or much worse, you can give me one of those four options, please. The-

Participant - I'm-

Interviewer - Sorry, go on.

Participant - All right. I'll just repeat what you just said, please.

Interviewer - Sorry?

Participant - I repeat the part of our way. You're talking about usual stuff. Just talk about that, please.

Interviewer - So in in the team's chat for the meeting, I've put the four options available for this survey for each question. So I need you to answer each question with one of the four, one of the following things: better than usual, no worse than usual, worse than usual, or much worse than usual.
So...

Participant - Oh, alright, alright. Oh, I even see the-the words you put around...

Interviewer - Yeah.
It's no problem, no problem at all. So for the first question, do you have problems with tiredness? Which of those four options for the last month compared to your usual day-to-day life fits best.

Participant - Worse than, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than udual. OK, the-the proper talkie bit where I will ask you a question and you can talk at me for 10 minutes is a little bit later. I'll let you know when that's happening.

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - Sorry for the confusion.

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - Do you need rest more?
Again, one of the four options, do you need to rest more? Better than usual, no worse than usual, worse than usual, or much worse than usual.

Participant - More worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse or much worse? Much worse?

Participant - Ahh, much worse than usual. The fourth option.

Interviewer - Yep. Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Like right now?

Interviewer - Your last month compared to most of the time.

Participant - Oh. Oh, sorry. Sorry. Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - For that I'd go with worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Do you lack energy?

Participant - Do I like? Do I lack like energy, worse than usual?

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.
Interviewer - Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you feel weak?

Participant - More worse than usual.

Interviewer - So much worse than usual, yes?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - Um, no worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. How is your memory?

Participant - My memory, my memory.
That would be worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. OK, so that is that survey done.

Participant - Alright, thank you.

Interviewer - So two more left.

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - Again, sorry for the confusion. I know some of it is also lag because of how far away you are. Obviously there's a delay with the Internet connection.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, that's true.

Interviewer - So the next one I'm putting some of the options in... It's a numbered scale from 1 to 7.
So #1 is disagreeing the most #4 is neutral and #7 is agreeing the most. So if you wanted to slightly agree, it would be a 5.
Because it's slightly more agreeing than neutral, just a little bit of agreement.
So any number from one to seven with four being neutral, one being disagree and seven being agree. OK?

Participant - Alright, alright, alright, I...

Interviewer - So this this is the last week.
So during the past week, yes?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. During the past week I have found that my motivation is lower when I am fatigued.

Participant - Sorry. So with that, I'll go with seven. I agree.

Interviewer - No problem.
Seven.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - I strongly agree 7.

Interviewer - Seven, I am easily fatigued.

Participant - I strongly agree.

Interviewer - 7.
Fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - I'll do 7.

Interviewer - Seven again, and fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven again. My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven again, fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven and last but not least, fatigue interferes with my work, family or social life.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven. OK. So that's that second one done.

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - Thank you very much.

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - Just one more left...

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - ...this has another different scale. Sorry. Again, it's a little confusing to jump from scale to scale, I know.
So I'm just typing up what each number means in the chat a second. Bear with me.
If I can spell properly, Oh my God.
Right. So that is the scale for the final one, zero to three. This is around how likely you are to fall asleep or doze in certain situations.
This doesn't...

Participant - Alright, alright, I see. I see.

Interviewer - This doesn't have an explicit time frame, so you're not thinking specifically within the last week. This is just if you're in this situation, how likely do you generally think you would be to fall asleep?

Participant - All right.

Interviewer - So, some of these situations are a bit wacky, I didn't write them, they are as they are.

Participant - All right, I see.

Interviewer - Just some, some of them are a little bit amusing. But yes, so situation one sitting and reading.

Participant - Moderate chance of dozing, 2.

Interviewer - 2, watching television.

Participant - You said?

Interviewer - Watching television, watching TV.

Participant - Alright, alright, I get you. I get you, I get you, I get you, watching TV. That will be moderate chance of dozing, 2.

Interviewer - That's a two, sitting inactive in a public place. For example, a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - High chance of I'm dozing.

Interviewer - 3.
As a passenger in a car for an hour without a break.

Participant - High chance of dozing, 3.

Interviewer - Three again, and lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - Oh, I love rest so much so, so high chance of dozing. Of course 3.

Interviewer - Three. Me too.
Sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - Right, sitting, talking to someone you said, you said?

Interviewer - Yep, sitting and talking to someone, anyone.

Participant - Oh, alright, alright, alright. I thought it was a specific person. Like a dog or something.

Interviewer - Nope.

Participant - With that, with that slight chance of dozing, 1.

Interviewer - 1.
Sitting quietly after lunch in brackets when you've had no alcohol.
Like I said, they're a little bit wacky.

Participant - That would be moderate chance of dozing off, two, because I'm quite full.

Interviewer - Two and in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - Right.
In a car that will be, I will never doze. Yeah.

Interviewer - So 0.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Good, good. Thank you very much. Yeah. Like I said, some of them are a little bit wacky, especially for you because I, if I understand right, having alcohol during lunch is not common in America. It's common here.

Participant - Yeah, that's how I was like "Alcohol. Like who drinks during the day?"

Interviewer - Yep. Yeah, that's...

Participant - But I think it's going on your side.
You do it. In fact, you do. I feel like you do it.

Interviewer - I-I mean, I personally occasionally, but I know a lot, a lot of people in the country do it a lot.

Participant - So you're guilty!

Interviewer - I mean, there's, not so much anymore. But yeah, because funnily enough alcohol makes me sleepy.

Participant - Alright.

Interviewer - OK. Demographic questions, surveys and OK, we're into the main bit of the interview. So this is the last thing. It's the main bit, 20 questions.
Excuse me. They're split up into sections. This first section is going to be about what chronic mobility conditions you have and how they affect you. Again. Feel free to ask for a break or to... go back to an earlier question if you remember something, or to say that you can't remember anything or you don't have any examples for any question. You don't have to answer all of the questions, and there is no specific questions that you have to answer or anything like that.
So just answer as many as you feel you can, and there's there's no obligation here or anything.

Participant - All right, all right. All right. I-I get you, I get you, I get you.

Interviewer - OK, so question one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - I have long COVID diagnosed after infection in 2021 and it causes like ongoing fatigue and mobility issues for me.

Interviewer - OK. Would you say ongoing fatigue, is that a large period of fatigue after any exertion? So like several days of... needing to rest, 3-4-5 days.

Participant - Well, what I can really like say is that yes, that's right first of all. And even in small amounts of an exhaustion can leave me fatigued for several days. So sometimes three to five days over needing extra rest. So yeah.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience, any and all that you are willing to describe?

Participant - All right. OK. So I was just trying to put everything together in my head.

Interviewer - No problem. Take your time.

Participant - So I experienced like, sorry, chronic fatigue, shortness of breath, muscle weakness and a brain fog. If I over like exehrt myself, I get post exertional crashes where my fatigue and symptoms worsen for several days.

Interviewer - OK, so I believe in, in the UK we call that post exertional malaise.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. If that sounds about right, can you give me examples of activities this condition has prevented you being able to do?

Participant - I guess I was looking forward to that question. I had to stick my hand up!. So it prevented me from doing like a longer walks exercising, you know, the gym because I used to go to the gym and even everyday things like our housework or social activities. So I often have to like cancel plans or break tasks into very small steps because of the fatigue and breathlessness.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instant you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - All right, I will... The earliest was around mid 2021 shortly after I, after I first had COVID. I noticed I couldn't manage simple activities like a walking short distances without feeling completely drained and that was when my mobility started being affected.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity?

Participant - All right. Nice question. Yeah, sure thing, I can. An example will be if I go to the grocery shop for grocery shopping or take longer walks, I can feel exhausted for several days afterward .Even like light exercises or attending a social event can leave me needing three to five days of rest to recover.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - Talking about brain fog. All right, brain fog makes it hard for me to like, concentrate or remember things. For example, I sometimes lose track of what I'm saying mid-conversation. Forget simple tasks or struggled like our focus or reading or working on the computer.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Quick question because this has happened to people, are you currently struggling with brain fog when trying to answer these questions?

Participant - Alright. Can I be honest or I can have it...?

Interviewer - Yeah, absolutely, absolutely. I mean, these are academic questions. They're difficult for me to ask. So I expect them to be difficult for you to answer.

Participant - Well, yeah, sometimes like I have like this main, mid, like a mild brain fog while answering the questions you're asking me and I may need a moment to think or-or for a question to be repeated. That's why I keep on saying repeat, repeat and I keep on telling you just a minute. Let me bring... bring everything out. So yeah, something I'm experiencing.

Interviewer - Yep.
Absolutely no problem. Thank you very much. Thank you for-for talking about that as well. I know it's not easy to admit that in the middle of the-the thing that you're struggling with sometimes.

Participant - Yeah, I fear being mocked or judged. You know, someone might even call you dumb.

Interviewer - Yeah, yeah, that absolutely won't happen here. I'm struggling with brain fog right now as well, so...

Participant - So we are trading basically?

Interviewer - Yeah, no, exactly. OK, question 7. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?
If this isn't relevant to you, by all means say that it isn't.

Participant - All right.
Yes, 'cause. If I have like poor night sleep or wake up several times, my fatigue is much worse the next day, and I'm more likely to feel weak, short of breath and struggle with concentration, basically.

Interviewer - OK, so it just exacerbates. It makes you, all of your symptoms worse?

Participant - Yeah, in that manner.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. So the next question, this is the next section as well. This is focusing on what device or app you are using.
So what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using them?

Participant - Well, I currently use a step counter app on my phone. I started using it in 2022 to be precise to help me like track of-of my daily activity and notice of patterns within, with my fatigue.

Interviewer - OK. Have you used anything before this step counter app or any other tools at all?

Participant - Well before step counter I mostly track my activity and symptoms manually using my phone's notes up to jot down fatigue levels, steps and I was feeling each day so I didn't use any other dedicated fitness or tracking apps before.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. How do you use this device... or application I suppose?

Participant - Well, just to explain how I use it, I basically just use the step counter app to track my daily steps and distance walked and generally activity levels. So I check it throughout the day and see how much I've moved and adjust my activity on how I'm feeling.
And I also use it to like notice patterns between my activity and my fatigue levels as I said before.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. You've answered the next two questions in that answer. Perfect. Thank you.

Participant - All right, I'm doing good. I see. I'm doing good.

Interviewer - So we're skipping to question 12 because you have answered: When did you use the device, and how frequently. So the next question is part of the next section. This is about how your condition and the device interact together.
So can you give me examples that caused your decision to use the application?

Participant - Well, I decided to use the step counter app because I wanted a simple way to monitor my activity without over exerting myself. So I wanted to see how much I could safely do each day, track my patterns in my fatigue and set realistic goals based on my mobility limits.

Interviewer - OK. And you said you wanted a way to do that that didn't cause exertion itself, so that dia-, manual diary you were keeping before the reason you stopped using that is because that took effort in itself, right?

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you. Can you give me examples of times the app has alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - Well, for example, a few times in the app showed I had already reached my step goal early in the day when I tried to push farther, I noticed my fatigue worsened and I needed several days to recover, so the app helped me realise how, realised I was overdoing it and encouraged me to rest. Instead of pushing through.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device or application? Again, if there are none, just feel free to say that there are none.

Participant - All right, all right. All right. Let's see, since using my step counter up, I've started, I've, like, started taking short daily walks around my neighbourhood and doing gentle stretching at home basically. And I've also started, like, breaking up household task into smaller sessions.
Just pacing myself based on what the app tells me about my activity levels, just normal stuff.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the app?

Participant - No, just give me a moment to just put the, piece everything together.

Interviewer - Yeah, no problem. Bear with me. I'm just going to grab my water bottle.

Participant - All right. All right, that's good.

Interviewer - But feel free to talk. I can still hear you.

Participant - Alright, alright.
Well, using the app has made me more aware of uhh limits, so I've stopped doing longer, like longer walks, intense workouts, or back-to-back errands that I will push me past my step goals. So I also limit social activities that require a lot of standing or walking in one day.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Are there any specific examples of activities that you can give me just off the, off the top of your head?

Participant - Just a moment.

Interviewer - No problem.

Participant - All right. For example, I used to go like grocery shopping. As I said, for an hour or more without breaks. But now either split it into shorter trips or ask someone to help, or do it for me. And I also used to try attending long social gatherings or events, but I've stopped doing that because it left me exhausted for days afterward, and then I had exhaustion.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?
Or application.

Participant - One example is when I noticed I constantly feeling wiped out after even short walks. I wanted a way to track my steps and see patterns with my fatigue, so I chose the step counter app. Another example will be is when I try to go back to the gym or...
But I had to stop early because of the exhaustion, so the app helped me set safer, realistic, uhhh activity limits.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you. So three more questions and then we're done. These are more speculative questions, so feel free to make up something that doesn't exist yet. It will still be useful.
You'll understand what I mean when I ask the first question. What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Alright, I see. I-I see. I see.

Interviewer - So again you, you can make up something Star Trek level if you want.

Participant - All right, my ideal device will be a simple wearable tracker pad with an app. It will track my steps, movement and energy levels, but also let me log, let me log fatigue, brain fog and other symptoms easily. It could give me like personalised recommendations for pacing and rest.
And it just goes based on how I'm feeling each day.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove?

Participant - Functions, I'm talking about functions, so I think I will remove features that are set generic high step goals or pushes us to like overexert. So like constant notifications to reach 10,000 steps. I'd also remove complex metrics that aren't useful for people with mobility limitations so the app stays simple.
Focused onwards relevant to my condition.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you. What functions would you add?

Participant - Since I have partly-

Interviewer - You've already partially answered this, so if you feel like there's nothing, nothing extra you can add, just say that you've already answered it.

Participant - Well, I would add a fatigue and symptom tracker that links directly to my activity levels, and that's when I'm at risk of exhaustion and give suggestions for pacing or rest. It could also provide reminders for gentle like stretching or recovery activities, and a way to track progress over time without comparing to standard step goals.

Interviewer - OK, perfect.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Thank you very much.

Participant - You're welcome.

Interviewer - Right. I'm just going to quickly go over what each section was and if you think of anything you want to add, just say so. Again, you can feel free to add as many things on to the end as you want if you remember something. Or if there's anything in particular you feel should be included in the study, absolutely just... just say so and I've got no problem with it. So first question or first section was around your condition and what symptoms you had. The second section was around the application that you use and how and when you use it. The third section was around how your condition and the application interact with each other and how you change your behaviour according to what you see in the application. And the third section- the fourth section, sorry... Was the speculative questions around your ideal device.

So is there anything you would like to add at all at the end?

Participant - Umm, in regard to what I said I... do I really need to add anything like, maybe just add that it will be useful for future studies to look at how apps could integrate more physical activity and symptom tracking in a way that really adapts to people with chronic mobility conditions, also including reminders for pacing and recovery and the ability to share progress with the healthcare providers, could make these devices even more helpful. And I'm finally, I think it's important to consider mental and cognitive effects, like uhh brain fog, not just physical steps when designing fitness apps for people like me. So yeah, that's definitely what I'm gonna add.

Interviewer - OK. Perfect, thank you very much.

Participant - You're welcome.

Interviewer - I'm just going to stop the recording.

title: P10 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - There we go. Right. OK. So this first one is the last month compared to your baseline, what your normal expectation is. I will say all three of these surveys obviously are meant to be had at the onset. And that's onset of symptoms and that's not necessarily true for, for this study.

Participant - Right. Fair enough.

Interviewer - So don't think about it too much, if it doesn't feel like you're fatigued enough or too fatigued in the answers you're giving just, just don't, don't worry about that. Just like a gut, a gut feeling.

Participant - Mm-hmm.
OK.

Interviewer - So in the last month, compared to usual, do you have problems with tiredness? Is the first question. The options are a little bit strange, just posted them in the chat.

Participant - Hmm.
Right.

Interviewer - So it's not numbers, it's this scale.

Participant - Right. OK. Like, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Again, don't, don't think about it too much. If it's a bit. Oh, well, kind of, I guess. Then that's more than fine.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Do you need to rest more?

Participant - Yes, I, I do.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, or much worse?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse.
Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse.
Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - I'll go with worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse.

Participant - I can see I can see a theme happening here.

Interviewer - No one would ever expect such a thing. Do you lack energy?

Participant - Yeah.
Yeah. Worse than usual, yeah.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Actually, I'm going to go for no worse than usual on that one.

Interviewer - No worse, we love a change of form. Do you feel weak?

Participant - Yeah.
No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse. Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - That, yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - No worse than usual, I think, although feel free to correct me. But, if I do slip up.

Interviewer - No worse.
The funny thing with that question is that's not actually what's written. What's written is do you make slips of the tongue when sleeping, and I've decided that's very obviously a typo in the...

Participant - Oh dear.

Interviewer - Which I quite like and I have to correct myself every time I go through this.

Participant - I know.

Interviewer - Question #10 do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. How is your memory?

Participant - Pretty poor at the best of times, but I'll go with worse than usual on this one.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, I think my favourite answer to that question was "I don't quite remember."
That's... Yeah, that kind of proved the point.
Right. So that's survey number one of three.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - The second one is a scale of numbers one to seven.

Participant - Right.

Interviewer - One is disagree, four is neutral and seven is agree and obviously intervening numbers are varying magnitudes.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - OK, so for example a three would be slightly disagreeing.
And this is...

Participant - Right, I might write this down. So one is disagree.

Interviewer - I can put it in the chat if you want.

Participant - Oh, that'd be super if you could.

Interviewer - Yeah, that's something I-I learned fairly quickly is...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...putting the answers in chat so people can actually remember the scale that they're using. It's something you kind of need when brain fog is a frequent symptom.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, so there it is in the chat.

Participant - Super.

Interviewer - This is during the past week instead of the past month, so a more immediate time.

Participant - OK.
More immediate.

Interviewer - So during the past week I have found that my motivation is lower when fatigued, numbers one to seven.

Participant - OK, go with a 5 on that one.

Interviewer - Go with the five, exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven, I am easily fatigued.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - Six, fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - Six.
Where are we? My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 5.

Interviewer - 5, fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven, fatigue interferes with my work, family or social life.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6, OK. Thank you very much. That is survey #2.
Last one.
Where is it? Where's it gone? Oh, that was the same form, wasn't it? And I've just closed it.

Participant - Oh no!

Interviewer - I keep forgetting that that's two surveys and one PDF document.
Yeah, there it is.
OK, this one's a little, little, little bit quirky.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Obviously these surveys aren't fully up to date, necessarily. This involves certain daily scenarios, so it's not in the last week or the last month or anything particular.

Participant - Alright.

Interviewer - It's if this would happen to you today or on your average day, how likely would you be to fall asleep.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Now the definition of average daily circumstance changes somewhat. So yeah, some of these are quite quirky and it makes me giggle a little bit.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - But the, the scale is in the chat. Zero to three.

Participant - OK.
OK, right.

Interviewer - So yeah, it's just your chance of unintentionally falling asleep.

Participant - Fair enough.

Interviewer - But in general...

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - ...your chances of dozing when sitting and reading.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - Two, watching TV.

Participant - Also two.

Interviewer - Also a 2, I can hit the right arrow key, sitting inactive in a public place. For example a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - Umm.
1.

Interviewer - One, as a passenger in a car for an hour without a break.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3.
Lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3, sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - 1.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Sitting quietly after lunch in brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, in a car will stop in traffic.

Participant - Am I the driver?

Interviewer - It doesn't specify.

Participant - Oh God.

Interviewer - I would assume not, but...

Participant - I would hope not. If I was driving, I would be concentrating. So no, but if I'm a passenger, then probably a high chance, yeah.

Interviewer - Which, which, how would you read that? Which do you want to go with? Again, don't think about it too hard, to be honest. What's, what's your gut?

Participant - I...
I, I will assume that I'm the passenger and therefore 3.

Interviewer -  That's a 3. OK. Thank you very much. Yeah, this is what I mean. Lunch brackets when you've had no alcohol. As if that's an exceptional circumstance that needs to be specified.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And also in a car. Whilst stopped in traffic. No, we won't specify what role you're playing in the car.

Participant - Yes, if the DVLA is watching. If, please don't take my licence away.

Interviewer - Yes.
It's, it's quite a quirky little survey that.

Participant - Yes, isn't it just?

Interviewer - Right. OK. So that's all the, the more boring stuff. Now it's the more talky stuff.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So this is up to 20 questions, some I will only ask if you mention the thing or if I read between the lines and think you might have a thing.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - If I'm not sure I will ask and then you could end up getting that question. So the first section is on your mobility or fatigue conditions and how they affect you.

Participant - OK, OK.

Interviewer - So Are you ready?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Good, good question one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - I have been (hoo-hoo!) diagnosed with hypermobility Ehlers Danlos syndrome and postural orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome, abbreviated as pots and heds.
It took me a hell of a long time to get those diagnoses. I, it was over a decade of going back and forth between doctors, getting people to believe me, but...
Yes, that's what I've been diagnosed with.

Interviewer - OK. Is there anything that you are self diagnosed with?

Participant - Ooh.

Interviewer - Or would self diagnose yourself with? I suppose that's the wording.

Participant - Not anymore. As I say, I was pretty confident that I had EDS. I wasn't sure which type of EDS, but I was pretty confident that I had EDS. But I have finally got a diagnosis now, so, yay!

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Congratulations on the diagnosis?
I think?

Participant - Well, it means I get access to treatment now. So that's a good thing.

Interviewer - Which is good. The downside is that you have the condition, but swings and roundabouts.

Participant - Well, yeah.

Interviewer - Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Yes, so chronic pain, especially in my joints, it's never gone, though some days it's worse than others, especially after exercising, or really any physical activity is, is when the pain is at its worst. I've had that since early childhood. It was dismissed as just growing pains. It did not stop when I stopped growing, so it was not that.
I suffer from fatigue. It's worse in warmer weather, which is why I've been feeling not so hot recent. Well, I have been feeling hot. I've not been feeling so...good, recently, because of our sort of mini second summer that we've had though hopefully now that the temperature is cooling down again, I should start to feel a bit better. Again with that fatigue comes brain fog. And as we've already discussed in the survey, likelihood of me falling asleep. I tend to fall asleep after eating like a an hour or two after eating I will conk out. Apparently that's because of blood flow to my digestive system.
So that's fun. The pots affects me, particularly with moving, standing, changing position. If I stand up too quickly, I can faint. So I've...had to develop strategies of like, how to get up without fainting. Also, my heart rate is a lot, my resting heart rate is a lot higher than it ought to be. I have now got medication to control that so I am able to do more than I would previously, but I still need to monitor my heart rate regularly to make sure that I'm not doing too much because that will make all my symptoms worse in the long term and one of the things that could happen long term if I did not control my heart rate is the particularly the left side of my heart could become enlarged and eventually fail, so that needs to be monitored. I think every five years I get a echocardiogram for that. But yeah, monitoring my heart rate is very important for managing my condition.

Interviewer - OK, just to check I've got my terminology right, echocardiogram. That's the thing that's shortened to ECG, isn't it? Isn't it?

Participant - Umm...
That it's confusing because there are two things that can be shortened to ECG. There's the echocardiogram and the electrocardiogram. The electrocardiogram is where they put the dots on you.

Interviewer - Mmm, yeah. OK

Participant - I have had those as well. The echocardiogram is where they use an ultrasound machine to look at your heart and see the structure of it.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - I do in fact have both of those, but yeah...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - It's, it's, it's confusing, but the, the shortening is the same. It's the same initials.

Interviewer - Yeah. Obviously as a study, not in the medical department...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...good to get this specified. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you being able to do?

Participant - Mainly at the moment working. I've been unemployed for a while and it's difficult because there's not a lot of options that are fully remote because like commuting is a problem for me, having to get up and go to a place of work all the time. So I've, I've not been able to do as much of that as I'd like. I've not been able to... go out and socialise as much as I'd like, because of course, if I'm feeling fatigued and in pain, I can't really do that.
It has impacted the amount of housework that I've been able to do. I've not been very good at keeping my space tidy because I find it difficult to like bend over and pick things up.
But I have been getting help with that, especially from my parents, so I've been able to just about keep on top of things, but it's... that's where most of my energy goes into so...
Yeah.

Interviewer - So anything beyond maintaining that basic living standard or living condition of your, your immediate living area is...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Difficult.

Participant - Pretty much, yeah.

Interviewer -  OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - Oh, good Lord.

Interviewer - Again, you've mentioned childhood.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - If it's lifelong, just say that it's lifelong.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Give it, if give an example if you want and say this probably isn't the first instance, but.

Participant - Yeah. Like, when I was.... It is lifelong. When I was young, like as a toddler, I was quite late to walk. I would often like, sit on my arse and point to things and ask people to get them for me rather than doing it myself. Probably not the... I don't know. It's certainly the earliest instance I can remember, but no. Yeah, walking to school is something that I really struggled with. I was absolutely...
You know...
Severely in pain. By the time I would get to school and that would obviously impact my ability to do class work. Because you know, I couldn't concentrate because of the pain. But yeah, it is a lifelong condition.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. This is one I'm going to ask about because between the lines maybe. Extended exhaustion, post exertional malaise. Would you describe that kind of symptom?

Participant - Yes, I would.

Interviewer - OK. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity please?

Participant - Well, as I say, part of what I've been doing to, as I've mentioned in the survey that I filled out earlier, I... spent over a year bed bound because my symptoms just got progressively worse because the more pain that I was in, the less exercise that I could do and so my tolerance to exercise decreased.
Now... through like managed exercise, a lot of pacing...
I've been able to do about half an hour of exercise physiotherapy at the gym twice a week. However, a couple of days after that I am absolutely knackered so...
I can't... You know, I will be very tired. I will be in quite a bit of pain. I'll be asleep for most of the day. The day afterwards. And so I tend to space it out once at the beginning of the week, once at the end of the week. So I'm...
I have time in between to do other things because I don't want to be completely fatigued completely in pain.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - Oh gosh, yes, umm...

Interviewer - If you are struggling because of brain fog, do you mention that, that-that is probably the most common answer I've had is, oh, I'd love to give you examples, however...

Participant - Yeah, I know it's, it... It is... It is difficult to think of things on the spot.
Like I-I do struggle a lot with my short term memory. I could not tell you what I ate for breakfast.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - I... Did I have breakfast? I'm starting to doubt myself now. I hope I had breakfast. But no, yeah, again... It's difficult to find and hold down a job.
Because obviously you need to be able to think to do work. It does help if you're not making mistakes. I find it... I've been finding it increasingly difficult to like...
read books. I find it difficult to like concentrate on them for a long period of time. I've been listening to audio books instead now which I, has helped somewhat, but I do sometimes fall asleep and have to rewind them.

Interviewer - Right, OK. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Yeah, if I'm in a lot of pain, I will find it hard to get consistent regular sleep. It'll be hard for me to fall asleep at the correct time. Might wake up in the night because of the pain.
That'll make me feel more tired and more likely to sleep during the day, which makes it then harder to sleep correctly during the night and it just gets, goes around in circles like that.

Interviewer - OK, right. So that is the end of the first section, which is exclusively about your condition.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - The next one is basic descriptions of your device in in the exact same way.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Well, the exact same way in a very similar way.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using them?

Participant - I am using this. It is a Xiaomi Me band six, I believe? I've been using that for a number of years now.
It was... I bought it the year after it came out because they'd replaced it with the newer model and it was on sale...

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - ...so I suppose I could look that up.
But yes, it is a smart watch that can monitor things like my heart rate and my blood oxygen level.
And I use it in conjunction with the app notify for me band because that gives me a bit more flexibility and what I can do with it than the...official Xiaomi app.

Interviewer - OK. Have you used anything previously?

Participant - Previously to this, no, it says it came out in 2021, so I would have bought it in 2022.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
How do you use this device?

Participant - Mostly it's for monitoring my heart rate. I especially use it when I'm exercising to make sure that my heart rate stays... in the... appropriate range for exercise, which is around 110 to 120 beats a minute, so it aids me with pacing that it has for the exercise, it has a continuous monitoring mode, so it will... instead of measuring it once every 15 minutes, say it'll measure it continuously so you can see, but that does use more battery. I also use it, it has...
Because it has a like a gyroscopic motion tracker in it, it can tell me if I've been stationary for too long, and that helps me with my posture, because if I maintain the same posture for too long, that increases my joint pain. So if it will give me a little buzz if I've been sat in the same position for like half an hour say, reminds me to move about and that helps with my joint pain. It also monitors how well I slept. So that that give me an insight into, you know, if I've been sleeping well, do I need to focus on getting to bed at a more reasonable time? And if so, can I change some things about what I've been doing to get better sleep, so yeah, very useful.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. That continuous monitoring mode for exercise, is that something you manually enable? Is it something that it automatically starts doing or can it do both?

Participant - Yeah, it's, it's something that I have to manually enable.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - It-it would be convenient if it could do that, indeed something that I would improve about it, that seems fairly trivial to code... would be if it detected my heart rate over a certain amount during its regular 15 minute intervals to automatically start that continuous monitoring mode and also to like notify me to say "oh, your heart rate is raised. Maybe it's time to take a break" sort of touch. It'd be nice if it could do that, but it can't.

Interviewer - OK. Hold, hold, hold that thought.

Participant - I shall.

Interviewer - OK. Yeah. I'm just asking because I know pots, a lot of people have issues with Apple devices because they don't measure it regularly enough, so it can completely miss a pots heart rate spike.

Participant - Yeah.
Yes it can. One thing that I do like about it is that you can, well with the notify app, you can adjust the intervals in which it takes the measurements.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - So I've-I've-I've set mine to 15 minutes, but it can be any arbitrary number that you set so...

Interviewer - OK. That's very good to know. Thank you. So when, when, just to clarify, when do you use this device all the time? You're always wearing it?

Participant - Yeah, all the time.

Interviewer - OK. How? OK. So I guess that answers how frequently, but I suppose I'll slightly reword that. How frequently do you check the device or application?

Participant - How frequently do I check it?
Usually a... Two or three times a day. Unless I'm feeling like particularly bad at one moment, in which case I'll, I'll look at it to see how I'm doing in that moment. But like a flare up of symptoms, I'll check it an extra time, but usually about two or three times a day.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much, right.
Third section.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - This is third section of four. The third section is about your device and your condition interacting together.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So, can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device?

Participant - Definitely exercising, physiotherapy, because in order to get out of being bed bound, in order to sort of recondition myself, it's important to be able to exercise,
safely and in a controlled manner. That's not doing me any harm, but it's doing me good. So that's-that's the primary motivation for it.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
Good, good can... Oh, God. Where am I? There. Can you give me examples of times the device has alerted you to over exertion?

Participant - Yeah.
If I've had it in continuous monitoring mode for exercise, yes it has and I'm able to slow down, take a break pace myself.
If it isn't in continuous monitoring mode, then it hasn't. So as I said earlier, one of the things that would be... an improvement is if it did detect that I was having a heart rate spike that it could automatically start that continuous monitoring mode and give me an alert to let me know.

Interviewer - So currently with the way that you use the device or are able to use the device because of the way that it works, the only times you're alerted to over exertion actively as it's happening is during exercise.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK, which you have to manually enter the mode for?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK, slightly different take on that. Are there times say at the end of a day when you're looking back on a day when you check it to see how today has been, that you looked at it and went "oh, wow, I've overexerted today."

Participant - Yeah! There has definitely been that. There has definitely been times like that.

Interviewer - OK.
So frequent, but no ability to give a specific example.
Again, you don't have to. You can just say it does happen a lot. If, if you can't think of a specific example.

Participant - Yeah, I, I'll, I'll. I'll go with that. It does, it does happen a lot, especially if I've been walking somewhere, that-that can, that can happen. I don't necessarily realise that I'm overexerting myself at the time until afterwards I notice "Oh s***." And then I definitely feel it in the fatigue and the pain so...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Umm.
Regular physiotherapy exercise. I wasn't really doing that beforehand because I was finding it difficult to pace myself properly.
I-I-I would, I would often over exert myself trying to do too much and then I would feel unable to do enough afterwards. So...have, having, having the device has been able to help me do the exercise properly.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - Were I not using the device? I can't really think of anything. It hasn't inhibited me from doing anything.

Interviewer - OK, since you have passed that question, that is a "Is there anything you would have done that your data divide, decide made you decide Oh, that's probably a bad idea actually." Would you have tried something against... better judgement.

Participant - Oh, right, I think I've, I think I've slightly misunderstood the question then.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. I'll keep, I'll keep this bit then because obviously that happens quite a lot with brain fog and academic questions.

Participant - Right. Yeah. Now, 'cause. What, what, what you mean is, I believe the device monitoring the device and looking at it has alerted me to over exertion and therefore I have stopped.
As opposed to wearing, the device has prevented me from...

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Yeah. Yeah, no.
It has... like if I'd been planning on going out and doing something, say, going on a walk to somewhere.
If my device has been saying that like my heart rate is too high, I have been overexerting myself, I would choose to postpone or delay that to a time when I'm feeling more able to. So yes, in that in that regard, yes, it has.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Yeah, it's, it's always worth clarifying these kinds of questions because, not entirely clear unfortunately.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Right, last question for this section.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - Umm.
Mostly having tried exercise in the past without the device and overexerting myself causing my symptoms to be worse.
Uh, and thus not being able to...commit to like a-a regular schedule to-to-to be able to be more active and mobile.

Interviewer - So it's more a on reflection...
Umm...

Participant - No. It's like before I got the device I was trying to be more active and do physiotherapy, but I was struggling with it because I-I-I was... not really able to pace myself properly, so that was a contributing factor to me buying the device, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. Final Section 3 questions. This is a speculative...

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - ...set of questions, don't necessarily feel bound by reality. If you want to make something up, then feel free.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - But also if you want to use real examples or base everything off of your device, you can also do that if you want...

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - ...so completely up to you. No, no boundaries in this.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So what form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - The ideal form.
Honestly, the wristband that I've got at the moment is pretty good because it's not too large. It's not too in the way it's-it's-it's-it's quite a nice form factor it, it's not... It's sort of slightly slimmer than a regular watch, so it's it doesn't inhibit my movement in any way. It's not too...
What's the word?
Can we go back and change that answer where I say I forget the words?

Interviewer - No problem, I-I can get. I can go ahead and say this still happens.

Participant - Yeah, no, it-it-it doesn't. It doesn't get in the way. It's not... It's-it's-it's-it's nice. I like it.

Interviewer - OK. So, thank you very much. Yeah, if-if there's examples of symptoms popping up in the recording, I will shuffle stuff around and use that as needed.

Participant - Okie dokie.

Interviewer - Trust me, you are not the 1st and you will not be the last. And then even just talking about me, I will exhibit symptoms in these, these interviews, which is very funny.

Participant - Oh, bless you.
Umm, presumably that's part of your motivation to do this particular study, that it affects you personally.

Interviewer - Yeah, kind of I can. I can have a look chat afterwards if you want.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Yeah, I do have a very reality based motivation.

Participant - Oh bless.

Interviewer - What functions would you remove?

Participant - Would I remove?
Let me go through to see if there's anything that I would remove.
Honestly, nothing. I think all the functions that it has are quite useful. Oh, another thing, scrolling through the functions on it that I do find useful is that it has... breathing exercise timers built into it so that if I need to like control my breathing, it can say, breathe in, hold, breathe out, and it will do that on the device. So that's that's pretty useful. I have made use of that.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So nothing to remove?

Participant - Nothing to remove.

Interviewer - Lovely. Last one, what functions would you add?

Participant - What functions would I add? As I've said before, the ability to, if it detects a raised heart rate to automatically enable that continuous monitoring mode and to alert me. Maybe like buzzing or something.
Or send a notification to my phone. I don't know if it's necessarily counted as a function...

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - ...but the waterproofing is a little dodgy because although, although it is waterproof. It's not necessarily pool proof, so my brother who suffers from the same conditions as me also has one. Well had one, would take it, would take it swimming regularly, but I think it's the chlorine... in the water like dissolved the glue around the screen and so it fell apart and short circuited and failed. So although... it is waterproof in presumably, clean drinking water which is what they tested it in. Umm... pool water with the chemicals that make is sanitary does mess with the glue that holds it together. So I don't know if that's necessarily a function, pool proofing it, but it would be beneficial to be able to use it in a pool without worrying about it breaking, because it means you can do hydrotherapy as well as physiotherapy.

Interviewer - And if I understand rightly, with hypermobility as opposed to something like long COVID or general chronic fatigue syndrome, the way to make hypermobility better is measured exercise.

Participant - Definitely, because it's important to do some exercise, otherwise you become deconditioned and you end up bed bound like I was. But it's in, at the same time, it's important to not over exert yourself because that will also make your symptoms worse.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So it's about managing that sweet spot in the middle where it's enough, but not too much, just the right amount.

Interviewer - Yeah, in contrast to CFS where rest is good for you, flat.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - OK.
Right. OK. So I'll give a basic overview, assuming there's nothing else you want to add to that last question.

Participant - OK. Yeah.

Interviewer - I'll give you a basic overview of the sections. If you are reminded of something, feel free to, to jump in and add anything.

Participant - Sure.

Interviewer - Section One was about your. your conditions, symptoms, onset, earliest instances and and the like.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Section 2 was about your device when you started using it. How you use it etcetera. Section 3 was how they interact. So what made you use the device? How it alerts you to symptoms and things that you have started doing or stopped doing as a result.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And the last one is that little speculative thing about your ideal device?

Participant - Okey dokey.

Interviewer - All happy.

Participant - Great.

Interviewer - OK good. I will stop the recording then. If there's nothing to add.

Participant - OK.

title: P11 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - Come on Teams. There we go, right. OK, this is 20 questions in total.
The first section focuses on your conditions. So question one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - Um, EDS, um, Sjogren's syndrome and chronic fatigue.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Um.
I'm hypermobile. Um, so um...
Joints are a bit unstable, so I get joint-joint pain and subluxate sometimes. Thankfully I don't fully dislocate.
And Sjogren's is an autoimmune condition, so um...
Chronic inflammation, which means I'm in pain all the time.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - Walking. I'm a power chair user. Um.
I feel like they've prevented me from doing most things at some point or another.
I'm struggling to...

Interviewer - Yeah, that's no problem.

Participant - Sort of place activities like specific examples, 'cause it's just like this is a lot of things.

Interviewer - OK, that's no problem at all. Thank you very much. That's more than enough. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - About 2015.

Interviewer - Thank you very much.
You've not mentioned it in symptoms. Do you get an extended exhaustion PEM kind of thing? Post Exertional Malaise?

Participant - Uh, yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity?

Participant - It tends to kick in like not immediately the day after, but like...
The day after, the day after, like the day after next. But then I tend to get the post exertional malaise s*** kick in around then. Um...
Like for example, I-I tried to do like a, a gentle workout at the gym like...2 weeks ago.
And like, I think pain wise, I-I would only say like I'm back to normal within the past couple days. I've been feeling that pain for like over a week.
In terms of malaise, just...
I don't know it's hard to...
How do you descr-
Honestly, like fatigue is kind of just my default to such an extent that it kind of doesn't stand out in my brain to give examples sometimes. I don't know.

Interviewer - OK, that's no problem at all.

Participant - It's, it's been like a decade.

Interviewer - Yeah.
No, that's, that's completely understandable. I mean, again, if you can't pick out specific examples or you can't answer a question, that's no problem. Just say so by all means, OK? No, no pressure to answer every question.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - Ummm...
I guess, sometimes I like to watch TV shows with friends of mine or whatever.
Who's calling me?
I am not answering that call.
That is a spam call.

Interviewer - We love spam calls in the middle of study interviews.

Participant - Yeah, I like to watch TV shows with like friends of mine. Sometimes there's-there's certain because I've got ADHD as well and I will struggle to watch certain TV shows on my own, but like I'll find it easier to concentrate on with... other people.
Um...
Yeah, I sometimes.
Part of the difficulty I have with some of these TV shows is that I'm face blind and so I'm constantly getting confused about characters. No, I'm like, have we met this character? And I'm like, no, we, we that's like the main character we we've seen since the beginning of the show. I'm like...
Oh, right. Yeah.
Um.
But yeah, like cut, like this. I feel like I'm probably annoying my friends because I'm just like...
Why are they doing that? Because I've already forgotten what the plot point that's led to this point.

Interviewer - OK.
Thank you very much. Um...
OK, I'm going to read between the lines again.

Participant - That might not have been spam. Oops.

Interviewer - If you need a break to give to send out an important call, just say.

Participant - I'll call them back after.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you. Right. I'm going to read between the lines with the post exertional malaise, sleep disruption. I'm assuming that's a thing?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Well, I don't know my sleep...
When I've already slept like 12 hours or something and I wake up in the morning...
And I'm still f****** tired.
I'll try and like push through whatever, but if it eventually reaches the point where I'm still tired then...
I've got to sleep during the day.
And by that point I'm clocking up over 12 hours of sleep, so of course I'm not going to sleep that night.
And then it just gradually shifts you more nocturnal sometimes.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - It's not, not really an example, but.

Interviewer - No, I mean again, how you give the answer and how you word it is still valuable on its own.
So thank you very much. Your second section starting now is about the device or application or whatever it is you use.
OK, so question one, what device or application do you currently use and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - I've got an Apple Watch. I think I got that about...
Oh, when did I get that?
Total guess, 2020?
Actually, it might be earlier than that.
So 2018, 2018.

Interviewer - OK. I'll go., we'll go with 2018 then, yeah.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, thank you very much. Have you used anything previously or is this the first device?

Participant - And this is the first wearable I've had.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. How do you use this device or application?

Participant - Um, when I first got it, it was to be, to track sleep.
Since then I have switched to other apps that track my sleep instead. Nowadays I more use it for like exercise, more or less, to be honest, um.
Sometime, yeah...
Yeah...
Mostly for exercise, mostly when I work out.

Interviewer - OK, you said you use other apps to track your sleep. What are these applications sorry?

Participant - I went through a period of using the app Rise, more recently Pokémon Sleep.

Interviewer - OK, no problem at all. Is that what the, the one you use currently?

Participant - Yeah, totally.

Interviewer - Could you please include that in the answers so when I ask, for example, this most recent question, how do you use the device or applications? Can you provide an answer for the Apple Watch and for your Pokémon Sleep app please?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Since, since you use both.

Participant - So what are we? So is the criteria for this thing just like any kind of, I guess, digital thing that you use to help manage your health sort of symptoms?

Interviewer - So it, we, it's what we would describe in the lab as health trackers.

Participant - Right. OK.

Interviewer - Obviously in in the grand scheme of the industry, fitness trackers is both like the really niche area of stuff for exercise...

Participant - Mm.

Interviewer - ...but it can also apply to the wider area of keeping you fit and sleep trackers and things like devices that track how much water you drink...

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - ...come under that that umbrella. Unfortunately, there's not really a single, good term to encompass everything other than wearables...

Participant - Mm.

Interviewer - ...but not all wearables are applicable, so...

Participant - Right. OK. Yeah.

Interviewer - Apologies for the, for the confusion.
Um, but yes, OK, so... Next question, when do you use this device or application?

Participant - Umm, I...

Interviewer - So you, you said during exercise, but...

Participant - Yeah, I tend to try and make sure it's charged up and stuff if I know that I'm going to the gym. I have been wearing this more than usual recently just because like I said, I'm...post-op at the minute, so I'm probably sort of keeping an eye on health stuff more than I typically would. I use the app every night.

Interviewer - OK.
Um, so obviously you've answered this for the, the sleep app already, but how frequently do you use the device?

Participant - I'm gonna tell on myself in terms of how often I exercise now, aren't I?

Interviewer - I guarantee it's more than me, you're fine.

Participant - Honestly, I hadn't used this in quite a decent amount of time until recently.
Because I bought a gym membership recently. Um, so...
Over the past month?
I have exercised twice, but I have... I guess I have worn it about 3/4 of the month.

Interviewer - Okay. Thank you very much.
Right. So we're on to the third section now, which is your condition and these devices interacting, OK?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Excuse me. So can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device?

Participant - You-could you repeat that?

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities that caused yourS decision to use the device?

Participant - I'm finding the wording of that a little confusing.

Interviewer -  OK. Um, can you give me...examples before you started using the device that of, of activities that made you want to buy a device or use a application.

Participant -  Oh, OK, right. Um, like I said, I bought it first for sleeping.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - The sleep tracking, because I don't know, I'm, one of my special interests is sleep and most of the sleep apps tracked by just...having a phone on the bed and I just didn't find this satisfying enough.
The tracking. Um.

Interviewer - OK.
Is there anything else or?

Participant - Didn't give enough, didn't give me enough data.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Fair enough. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples-

Participant - Um.

Interviewer - Oh, sorry, were you still answering that previous question?

Participant - For anything else I guess...
Yeah, I guess I knew that it could be useful for just health, sort of more generally. Obviously I got a bit more cause than most to keep a closer eye on my health, I guess.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - Um.
So I was a bit curious about that.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the device alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - Oh God. So I've discovered that my pulse during exercise reaches truly terrifying rates.
I don't know why I haven't had a heart attack yet.
Yeah.
Over exertion...
I'm getting caught up in my head again about specifics. Um...
So overexertion for me personally...
It's more often about the fatigue that I feel in my body, in my muscles. Um...

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - And it doesn't always reflect... in things like pulse and other things that wearables track.
So...
Whilst I can say that it's-it's-it's...
It would let me know...
Like it, for example, like I don't know...
Hmm...
Yeah.
I don't know.

Interviewer - OK, that's no problem at all.
Thank you very much anyhow.
And again, no pressure to answer specific questions at all.
So next question, can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device or application?

Participant - I've started after... but since using the device?

Interviewer - Yes.

Participant - Oh.
Like as a result of using the device application or just...?

Interviewer - No.
Just after.

Participant - Oh.
Well, exercise more generally, I guess. Um.
Things like swimming or going to the gym or whatever when I first was like, when I first became ill...
I think I...
I don't know. I don't. I don't know if I was just wasn't used to this new sort of baseline...
Because I was very um...
Obviously it hurts more. Your fatigue is worse after exercise...
And...
Sometimes that would go on for quite a prolonged period of time that I would mistake as "Ah, exercise is making things worse...for me overall", and so I kind of avoided the exercise earlier on...
Um...
Yeah, there's also a social side to this all. I'm a, I'm, like I said, I'm a powerchair user and I...
Live on benefits, more or less.
So there was definitely a long period...
Where, I would have been very scared to go to the gym or go swimming...
Because there's this belief that if you're a power chair user, you must be fully, fully paralyzed. And if you, if you are ambulatory to any extent, then you're a faker and somebody's going to f****** report you to the DWP or something.
So it's-it's-it's kind of happened more with my confidence, I guess...


Interviewer - OK.

Participant - As well. That's a very roundabout answer. I'm sorry.

Interviewer - No, no problem at all. Again, the way in which you answer a question is useful. We are not looking for specific methods. Roundabout answers are fine, OK?
Thank you very much. So can you give me examples of regular activities you've stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - I stopped participating in?

Interviewer - Mhm.

Participant - I can't think of anything.
