Interviewer - OK, that's no problem.

Participant -Just, I just need you to see what's going on down here.

Interviewer - Oh, to be a cat and not be worrying about taxes.

Participant - Boo, taxes.

Interviewer - Oh God, I-I-I see you're having a much cosier time in this interview than I am then?

Participant - Yeah. Where are you? Oh, you said you're at home.

Interviewer - Um.
Yeah, I'm in the uncomfy office chair that makes me do work because it's uncomfy and I have no...

Participant - Boo. Oh, oh, oh, I see. I see. It motivates you to do work by virtue of making you uncomfortable, I see.

Interviewer - Yeah.
The ADHD is strong, um, and I have absolutely no pets whatsoever, so there's nothing fluffy to, to motivate me in that way at all.

Participant - Understood.
Well, at least work's getting done, I guess.

Interviewer - But hey-ho.
That's the hope.
Right, so this is...
This is the second to last question for this section. We're almost at the end of the study. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device or application?

Participant - I can't think of anything.

(brain fog means question 17 was not asked)

Interviewer - OK, that's no problem at all.
OK, so this is now the last section. 3 questions. These are more speculative.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So you don't have to keep it grounded in reality if you don't want to. So question, the first question of this section, what form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Um...
So honestly, the, the watch is not ideal 'cause I got like contact dermatitis from, from that before.
Um...
And the phone is not ideal because it doesn't track biometrics.
As well as movement...
I would like it to...
Hmm...
Because it's like, there is this impulse to say some kind of implant, isn't there? But also there is also some...
Benefit to being able to take the thing on and off as you desire.
Well, I think...
Even I couldn't think of anything...
That would make me stop doing a thing...
When you asked me earlier.
I think some form of implant would probably be fine. Just not... Oh, then again, I have MRI's a lot.
Sorry.

Interviewer - If, if you can't land on anything ideal, by all means rattle through several forms and how they would and wouldn't work for you if you want.

Participant - Yeah, 'cause obviously the...
The first thing that sort of springs to mind when you think of those kinds of things is like...
In the brain. Um...

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - But I'm highly dubious of, of Musk and his neuralink and all that. I don't know if I approve of all that, and I've had quite enough brain surgery to last me a lifetime.
Umm..
I wonder if you could have like a... a necklace.
Maybe, if there was a way that a necklace could also track biometrics.
Say by being connected to like...
A microchip or something that is made out of a non magnetic material.
So like when it's on, it's connected, it connects to the implant, like when you're wearing it and when you take it off, it disconnects.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - And doesn't track.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - That could be cool.

Interviewer - So that's, that's a way of getting an implant to also be toggleable.

Participant - Yeah, maybe. And also, I think a necklace wouldn't cause contact dermatitis like the watch does.

Interviewer - Fair enough.
OK, thank you very much. Um, what functions would you remove?

Participant - What function would I remove?
From what?

Interviewer - For this ideal device, compared to devices that you know about or devices that you currently use, what functions would you remove? What is superfluous to what you think it should do?

Participant - Right, yes. Um...
Hmm...
Honestly, I wish it would only give me notifications about texts and calls, but then just leave everything else out.
I know there's a lot of things that just the screen is too small to...
Use the thing that it wants me to be able to use.
Like I'm scrolling through the apps on my watch and night sky is on here. I'm not going to look up at the stars using my Apple Watch.
That's, that's no use to me. I don't know.

Interviewer - Yeah, OK.

Participant - Yeah, that's probably it.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - I'm kind of a maximalist sometimes, so...

Interviewer - That's no problem. So, what functions would you add?

Participant - Hmm...
So assuming this is an implant thing, then I would assume it would also be possible to track blood pressure.
Um... As well as just pulse. Um...
Would it-...
I think, I think we could go. I don't know. Um...
I guess it would depend... I'm thinking about the medical feasibility of some of these things now.

Interviewer - You don't, you don't need to do that if you don't want to. You can preface something with "I don't know if this is feasible, but..." and then just say something wishy washy if you like.

Participant - I don't know if it can track things like nerve signals and stuff like that to give, for example, things like seizure warnings.

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - And...
I think that is a feature that I would like. I think that if you could track that and you were and it can track the cardiovascular side, then there's no reason why it, it's not giving you basically a sleep study's worth of information every night.
Because between like the EEG and the pulse and side- pressure side of things, that's and resps, like respiratory, that's, that's all the information that they, they track in a sleep study anyway really.

Interviewer - Mhm.

Participant - So it could definitely give much more rounded sleep info.
If that were possible to do.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - It would be nice.
On a like...
On a more like simple, more grounded level, like outside of the realm of like utopian possibilities.
I still haven't found an app...
That works particularly well, to be honest, for me.
In terms of like monitoring...
Fatigue, pain, symptoms...
Stuff like that. I know that there are some out there, but none of them I've really got along well with.
We haven't gamified that. That's what it is, I think. I think that all of the ones that exist for like symptom tracking and stuff, they're too medical. They're too medical still, and medical is just very boring and not good for neurodivergent people.

Interviewer - OK. Is there anything else?

Participant - I'll leave it there.

Interviewer - OK, so I'll give a quick run through of what each section was, just in case you're reminded of anything or want to add to anything.

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - So the first section was about your condition, specifically how it affects you and the symptoms. The second section was about your device, how you use it, when you use it, etcetera.

Participant - Mhm.

Interviewer - The third section was about these two things interacting, so activities you've started or stopped or-

Participant - (Alexa) This is a reminder. Take your meds. Just let me know you're done with the reminder.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Um...

Participant - No, no, she's not done. She is not done.
(Alexa) This is a reminder. Take your meds. Just let me know you're done with the reminder.

Interviewer - The really funny thing is I'm gonna have to transcribe that.
Oh Gods, for the sake of the transcription, what-what extra device is that?

Participant - That is...
That is my Alexa.

Interviewer - That is Alexa. OK.

Participant - Stop.

Interviewer - Stop. OK, yeah.

Participant - We can't say the name that must not be said in this household.

Interviewer - It's, it's the Amazon one, OK?
Uh, that's gonna be... That's gonna be an amusing thing to transcribe, OK.
Section 3 was your condition and the device, how they interact and activities that you do or do not do as a result. And Section 4 was your speculative section.

Participant - Mhm.

Interviewer - Is there anything you'd like to add?

Participant - No, I can't think of anything.

Interviewer - OK, I'm going to stop the recording then, if that's all good.

Participant - Yup.

title: P12 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - And there we go. Right teams is up to speed now. So, the first set of questions has the following set of answers that I've put in the chat. If it would let me. There we go. So every question for this first survey., if you can give one of those 4 answers please.
OK, so for example the first question is do you have problems with tiredness?
And then you would say much worse than usual. Better than usual. Whatever it is, I believe it's the last month compared to your baseline. So for the for the last month, has your sleep been better than usual, worse than usual, etcetera.

Participant - All right.
So there's there isn't a question there, it's just got the list of answers.

Interviewer - Yeah, so-so I'll be reading each question every question, every question has that scale attached to it.

Participant -  Ah, right. OK, right.

Interviewer - So for every question I say, I would say, how much have you been enjoying your cups of tea lately and you would give one of those 4 answers?

Participant -Right.
Right. OK.

Interviewer - If for the last month compared to your baseline, OK, so the, the first question is, do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Right. OK.
Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah. Do you need me to type it or do you just write the answer?

Interviewer - No, I'll, I'll repeat it every time just to confirm that I've heard it correctly, if that's OK.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So do you need to rest more?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Just to pause because obviously you, you have disclosed that you have possibly a similar condition. Do you find at this time of year that everything seems to get worse and it's a bit of a downward...spiral?

Interviewer - Hmm.
Yeah, I mean it's, it's paired with the fact that my-my family possibly also have SAD. So that obviously has an effect on the mental side. But yes, the cold tends to be more sapping.
Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Especially with... anecdotally, this goes with everyone. I-I'm kind of friends with who have similar fatigue things, even things like IBS. It-it gets worse in the winter just as a blanket rule.

Participant - Yeah.
Yeah. Yeah. Because I feel like all my answers to this because of the time of year, we're just going to be worse than usual.

Interviewer - Yeah, I-I will, I will say, obviously I will take that into account and there will, I expect that there may be a trend with the later interviews giving more negative answers than the earlier interviews because of how much further into winter we are, if that makes sense. So if that trend happens, if you are a part of that trend that will be accounted for.

Participant - Yeah, yeah.
Right. OK. Sorry. OK. Yeah, what's the usual?

Interviewer -  OK. No, it's not. No problem. It's-it's a very good point and not one that I've heard someone explicitly mentioned so far. So it's good to have that recorded and in quotes because then it makes it much easier to discuss. So thank you for that.

Participant - That's all right.

Interviewer - Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you lack energy?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Yeah, worse than usual.

And I still haven't read it because I haven't had the-the capacity to do so. Do you want more examples or just one?

Interviewer - As many as you're willing to-to provide.

Participant - I have carers come in and this morning and the carer asked me at the end if there was anything else that I wanted her to do and I said no thank you and then she left and I realised that there was something that I had wanted her to help me with but I hadn't been able to recall it in the moment.
I don't know if I can give you, like a specific example of this off the top of my head, but because it happens all the time, but I'll be talking to someone and I'm trying to express a thought and I can't find the right words, that happens quite often. I do sometimes say the wrong word, but it's more often that I just can't find the word that I want. So that happen. When I make phone calls and things I have to plan them ahead of time, so I have to write myself like a bullet point list of what the things that I want to say are or. what I want to ask, or sometimes like what you know, like what the actual objective is, so I can make sure that I leave with-with those things actually having been answered. And so yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. I have a feeling I thought of something to ask halfway through that I can't remember it.

Participant - We can come back to it if it comes back, yeah.

Interviewer - So we'll-we'll move on and maybe I'll remember it.
Oh, would you... So this inability or this lack of ability to recall specific examples, would you describe the brain fog... being a component in why you're unable to do so?

Participant - Yes, absolutely.

Interviewer - And would you say that it's so frequent that individual examples don't stick out it all just kind of melds into this memory soup sort of thing.

Participant - Yeah, that's absolutely it.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Yeah. And so because of my MCAS, I have allergic responses and one of them is to my menstrual cycle. So twice a month I get these allergic reactions that disturb my sleep. Sorry. Can you repeat the question just so I make sure that I stay on track?

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Right. OK. So yeah, when that happens, all of my symptoms get worse the next day or for several days afterwards. Anything that disturbs my sleep, so if it's, like, really hot in the summer and that will then affect my symptoms.
And so sometimes it's things like that that aren't, you know, not really in my control. But sometimes it's things that are in my control. Like if I go to bed too late because I'm watching TV or something, that will also impact on my symptoms the next day. And it's it, I can't really like pick out specific symptoms that get worse. It's just everything.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. That is the end of Section 1.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So now focusing on devices or applications.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So this can include sleep trackers, hydration trackers, heartbeat monitors, fitness trackers, etcetera.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - If it monitors your health getting it's included.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - What device or application do you currently use and when did you start using them?

Participant - I have a Garmin at the moment that I have used for probably about three or four years and before that I had a Fitbit for maybe a couple of years before that.

Interviewer - OK. Do you recall the specific model just to specify it's a Garmin Smart watch, right?

Participant - Yes. And it's a venue. I think it's a two and the Fitbit, I don't know if I could recall off the top of my head, but it was like a narrow one.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Were there any previous tools before the Fitbit or was that the 1st?

Participant - I don't believe so. I think that was the first one.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. How do you use the device?

Participant - It changes, so it hasn't been like static the whole time. When I first got the Fitbit, I was quite focused on looking at my sleep and my heart rate. And then as I've had the Garmin, sometimes I do use it to track my hydration, and my sleep, although not recently, either of those two. I mainly use it to track my heart rate, and Garmin has like a stress measure, which is to do with your heart rate variability. I track that one. I also track my menstrual cycle and how that is correlating with my other metrics and actually if you hold on, I'll just have a quick look and make sure that I'm not missing anything in my app.
So, so body battery which is, you know, an indication of my energy. I have in the past used it to track my steps. It's not something I'm particularly focusing on at the moment.
So yeah, mainly I'd say it's the heart rate, the body battery and the stress are the kind of three key ones at the moment.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When do you use this device?

Participant - All the time.

Interviewer - So permanent wear, yeah?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. And like that answers the next question of how frequently, although I will slightly augment that question, how frequently do you check the device?

Participant - Multiple times a day, I always check it first thing in the morning and then I will check it...it will either be that I'm having symptoms and I'll check it to see what it's kind of telling me that might match up with that. Or perhaps I'm thinking about how to plan my day or whether to do something or not do it. So yeah, I check it all throughout the day really.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of Section 2.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So on to Section 3 now. Question 12 of 20. So we're more than halfway.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the device or application?

Participant - Yeah. So I was aware that I was having issues. This is before I got diagnosed with pots. I was aware that I was having issues with my heart rate. I actually got it to track my steps, but I tracked my heart rate as well. And so I was able to kind of use it for that.
And also I then when I did get diagnosed as pots, went on medication and so it was then helpful for me to track what impact the medication was having on my heart rate as well.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the device alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - Yeah. So it does it in a few ways. So I have a heart rate alert so that if my heart rate goes above a certain you know number of beats per minute, it will alert me. And I have that set for at rest, but also during exercise. So it helps me keep within a certain kind of limit, if I'm exercising and sometimes it will go off, you know, if I'm just quite stressed and then, you know, that's a kind of signal to me to try and do something about that.
Can you just repeat the question again? I'm so sorry.

Interviewer - No problem, no problem at all. Can you give me examples of times the device alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - OK. Yeah. And so then also the next day, if I've overexerted myself, I will be able to see that reflect in my stats. And so I mean, it doesn't always tell me things that I don't already know, but sometimes it reinforces things that I am feeling or thinking are... the situation.

Interviewer - OK. So in terms of when you're being alerted, it's both in the moment with the device popping up and saying you're doing too much...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...but also on reflection, like a confirmation tool.

Participant - Yeah, I would say so, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device? If there are none, just say there are none.

Participant - I don't think there's anything that I started after using it, no?

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - I'm not sure there are any of those either.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - No, I don't think so.

Interviewer - No? It's no problem. Thank you very much. It's a really good way of getting through the questions quickly, I must say.

Participant - I'm not trying to do that, I promise.

Interviewer - No, it's fine. It's fine. I-I realise that some of these questions are circumstantial. Yeah. And obviously fatigue takes, all, all sorts of forms. So for some people, some questions just don't apply.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - And that's completely fine and expected, obviously. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - Demonstrate my decision to use the device. Yeah, I think it's really helped me confirm things that I'm feeling or thinking about. For example, is this dosage of the medication right for me?

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - And sometimes, you know, I might be feeling a certain way about that, and then I can look at my stats and see whether they, you know, agree or not. I think it's helped me with my exercise to help make sure that I'm able to do that kind of safely within my limits.
And... Sorry, I'm so sorry. I'm gonna have to ask you to repeat the question again.

Interviewer - No problem at all. I mean like-

Participant - I get halfway through my sentence. Then I realised I've forgotten what we're talking about.

Interviewer - Like I said, I'm symptomatic as well. There's absolutely no shame in asking for this at all. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - OK. Yeah.
I also get feedback on like things that I've eaten, for example, and how they've affected me and that's really helped me to... kind of hone in on where the problem areas were in my diet and even things like mealtimes as well.
And how-how that you know relates to what time I take my medication and it's been helpful in that sense as well.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Final section now, just three questions in this one, what form would your ideal device take or application?

Participant - I think I'm quite happy with it as a watch as I seen that's what you're asking, right?

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Yeah. Yeah. I don't really like the rings. And I've seen, like, there's one that's an armband that is not really my-my thing. So I'm quite happy with it as a watch. And I am quite happy with the app on my phone as well.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove?

Participant - It's a really good question and you might have to give me a minute to think about it.

Interviewer - No problem. You can take a minute to look through the device if you like.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Obviously some people find it very difficult to remember functions that they don't use.

Participant - Yeah, that's such a good point. So I can give you an example actually, one of the reasons that I don't track my sleep is because it was just always telling me that I'd had a bad night's sleep and that wasn't very helpful and it was giving me very, very generic sleep tips. Like I don't... I can't even remember because it's so long since I've used it, but it was things like, you know, "try turning the lights off before you go to bed" and it's like, oh, you know, "try not using your screen before you go to bed." And by that point, you know, I'd already implemented all of those kinds of things. I just found it a little bit patronising, but also just not helpful. Like, it's not helpful to wake up every morning and see that you slpet badly, like you know. And there wasn't, you know, I just didn't find the information very useful. So it's not necessarily that I would, I'd probably remove the helpful tips or make that an option that you could kind of opt in and out of. And maybe you can, maybe I just never got that far. I don't know that I would remove it because other people might find it useful. But yeah, maybe the ability to kind of toggle it on and off.

Interviewer - OK. So just to clarify on that sleep baseline, it's not adjustable and it never self adjusted to what you were baseline of a good night was?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - It just it was comparing you to some baseline that you were never informed of and have no control over I'm assuming?

Participant - Oh yeah, that was my experience of it.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you add?

Participant - In an ideal world, I would love to be able to monitor my blood pressure through it. I am supposed to monitor my-my blood pressure daily and I don't because I find the, you know, the monitor a bit of a pain to use.
So that's something I would add I think.
The I- so I think that my watch has the ability to do your oxygen levels, but I think it can only do it overnight and I think that it drains the battery so I don't have it switched on and I've never used it, but that, if I was able to kind of monitor that all the time I would find that quite helpful.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. So that's all of the questions. I'll go through a quick run through of each section and if you'd like to add anything you can.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So section one was about your condition, Section 2 was about your device.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Or devices, and Section 3 was about the interactions of them and the examples of certain things...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...like the-the activity alert for example.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And Section 4 was your speculation on what your ideals would be and what you would change.

Participant - Yeah, I think that's probably it. The only other thing that I would mention, I don't know which question it really fits with...

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - ...but I've actually found it quite helpful for being able to visualise how my emotions affect my symptoms as well. So like if I've had like a really stressful day or if something or someone has upset me, I can see that reflected in my stress results and obviously there's quite a lot of stuff around the interplay between you know your physical symptoms and your psychological symptoms and-and you know how they interact and-and-and I found that quite interesting.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. That's actually a really good point. Why didn't I think of that? No, no, jokes aside, thank you very much.

Participant - That's OK.

Interviewer - Is there anything else you'd like to talk about?

Participant - No, not that I can think of.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. I'll stop the recording and then we'll finish up.

title: P14 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - Right, OK. That's all started. So question number one, do you have problems with tiredness? Again, in the last month compared to your baseline.

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you need to rest more?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Umm...
Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual, do you lack energy?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you feel weak?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse. Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse, do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Oops. I've put 2 answers in the same one, never mind. Right. Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. How is your memory?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. OK. Thank you very much. That is survey one of three done.
Where's the others gone? There they are.
OK, so this next one is the last week instead of the last month.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - It uses a numerical scale, which I'll just type up quickly in the chat for you.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - OK, so it uses that scale, obviously 2-3 and 5 and 6 are intermediate kind of values.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - And yes, this is just a some statements that I'll read out and then, give me a number. And again, it's the last week compared to your baseline.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So my motivation is lower when I am fatigued.

Participant - Sorry, I'm, discalculia kicking in.

Interviewer - No problem.

Participant - Errr... 5.

Interviewer - Five, exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven, I am easily fatigued.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6, fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 5.

Interviewer - 5.
My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6, fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 5.

Interviewer - Five, helps if I press the key. Fatigue interferes with my work, family or social life.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7.
OK.
Lovely, lovely. And the final one. This doesn't have a set scale. It's just if this circumstance were to happen, what are the chances that you would accidentally fall asleep?
So I'll just again type up this scale in the chat for you.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - This one is a little, little bit quirky. I think it's the oldest of the three and it's, it uses the, well, the descriptions of the scenarios can be a little bit amusing, we'll say, and some of them are non specific.

Participant - No worries.

Interviewer - So if you're a little bit umm... confused?

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Just, just mention it, just ask.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So it's that scale...

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - ...zero to three and again it's just some, some, what's the word... scenarios?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So scenario #1, sitting and reading.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, watching TV.

Participant - Sorry, my phone just decided that me speaking was me saying Siri and absolutely wasn't.

Interviewer - No problem.

Participant - I didn't even know I had that turned on. I'm like, that's right. Sorry. Go again.

Interviewer - Watching TV.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, sitting inactive in a public place, for example, a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero, as a passenger in a car for an hour without a break.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3, sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - 0. Sitting quietly after lunch brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero. OK, lovely. That's all of the surveys done. I think, what's amusing the specification of no alcohol and the last one... it doesn't say whether you're it doesn't say whether you were the driver or the passenger.

Participant - Yeah, it's interesting.

Interviewer - It just says you were in a car.

Participant - I was like, I'm assuming that I'm driving, in which case I was sitting there like, yeah, I do love that. It's like, if you're really p****d, are you likely to have a wee snooze?

Interviewer - Yeah, yeah. If-if I'm being honest, I genuinely don't know if it means when you're the driver or the passenger. I-I can't tell. I can't parse it properly.

Participant - Oh, I mean, I've tried to be a good passenger as well. I'm like, no, if I'm the passenger, my job is to entertain the driver. I have to sit there like the court jester being like...

Interviewer - Oh, God, yeah. They're, they're quite amusing those ones.
Right, OK. So we now have the main body of the interview.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - This is split into four sections. The first one is about your conditions.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - The second one is about the devices.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - The third one is about how these two things interact and then the 4th one is just a short speculative...session. Session? Section, at the end just with a couple of questions.

Participant - Yup.

Interviewer - So that's, that last section's much shorter than the rest of them.
So again, you can say that a question doesn't apply to you if you like, take as much time as you need as well. I'm not going to hurry you up for an answer or anything. So- oh, and if memory or brain fog... interferes and you think of an answer to an earlier question, feel free to loop back whenever you'd like.

Participant - Perfect.

Interviewer - Perfect, thank you very much. Right question one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - So I have endometriosis and I have ehlers Danlos syndrome.

Interviewer - Thank you. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - So endometriosis I get chronic pelvic pain, which spreads down my left sciatic nerve.
I used to have a lot of pain most days. It was worse around my menstrual cycle and I would get a lot of fatigue because of the pain, basically it was... you know. EDS, I've, I've, I've had all my life, but it got a lot worse following my 2nd endometriosis surgery I got a, an infection in-in one of the surgical wounds and turns out that that is one of the things that can make EDS a lot worse. So that was really quite debilitating. That's where a lot of my fatigue came from and that I was suddenly not able to, you know, walk to the bus stop or... yeah. Really, really, do more than one thing in a day where I used to go out and, you know, go for a run in the morning, do a full day at work and go into a class in the evening. I was massively reduced in what I could do.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities this condition has prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - I basically stopped doing any sort of... cardio activities, like, I have to be really gentle with what I'm doing. So I can I can do a walk, but I need to like stop halfway because I'm like "oh, I need to get back." So it's a lot less than I used to do. I still swim, but that's pretty much it. I don't, I can't run or things like that anymore. It's just, it's too much for my body and in terms of things like strength training, which I was really getting into, because alongside the fatigue my-my, my joint function reduced massively. I get subluxations, so partial dislocations, really easily in my hands and my wrists. So yeah, a lot of my...my-my exercise-y type hobbies basically just had to go by the wayside.
Yeah, I think that's...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - Probably about eight years ago. That's when I was really aware of, like, the-the fatigue aspect of it really kicking in. Before that it was like, oh, I've got, you know, I get bad periods or oh, my joints are-are a bit lax. Oh, that's fine. But yeah, it's about 8 years ago that properly.

Interviewer - So that was 2017 then.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. I'm not been able to read between the lines on this. Do you get extended exhaustion or some sort of post exertional malaise behaviour?

Participant - Yeah, I got like... like what most people would say, like the DOMS, the delayed onset muscle soreness. For me that lasts days and it can be like quite severe.
And yeah, like in terms of like recovering, I will usually need to rest for the fatigue, for-for a good couple of days once I've done something. Something in that case, but one of the things I refuse to give up is going to, to gigs, going to, to live music.
It's just one of the things that yeah, it is one of the things in my life that just brings me utter joy and it just completely wrecked me. But I refuse to stop doing it. But yeah, if I've, if I've gone to a gig, I'm like, that's me for a good couple of days, that's...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. I didn't actually ask the question, but you did you, you answered it anyway, so thank you. Likewise on the gigs if I'm perfectly honest.

Participant - We-we all need these things like that make us light up inside. Seriously.

Interviewer - Yeah, exactly. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities?

Participant - I think the most frustrating one is that... I'm I-I really enjoy conversation and I'm-I'm a researcher, so it really frustrates me when I can't...
Sometimes it's not thinking of the right word, but just not be able to express myself properly because it's just, I can't make my brain cooperate. That is immensely frustrating, and it's still despite the fact that I work in equalities and I do a lot of work around accessibility and disability, I still feel like saying "oh brain fog" when I say it, it sounds like it's excuse. For everybody else it's totally OK. But when I apply it to myself, I'm just like, "Oh my God. Get, get yourself together. You need to do this. You need to be a professional", but yeah...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - I wish I had a proper sleep schedule.
Because I'm neurodivergent I, if I, if I sort of get into a hyper focus or you know I'm just in the swing of writing or researching, I will easily stay up till 3-4 in the morning.
And then I go to bed and I'm wired and I don't sleep till about 5 and then that's, you know, it just has such a knock on effect the next day that my ability to control... control? Not control, but my ability to... do stuff while having pain symptoms is reduced like I-I just can't cope with pain and joint laxity as much as I normally can, and the fatigue is just 10 times what it would be because I've...
I've not got enough hours of sleep and I've done them at a weird time of day where it's disturbed sleep. So yeah, that's, I know that it's a bad thing to. I did it last night. I know it's a rubbish thing to do, but I do it because my neurodivergent brain is just like...
"We need to follow the dopamine", yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What device or application... Sorry, I should say that's the end of the first section.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - So we're on to the-the device section now, what device or application do you use for these conditions?

Participant - So I use an Apple Watch now. I've used that for the last year. Yeah, previous to that, it was a Fitbit.

Interviewer - OK, when did you? Uh, sorry, you about the last year you said?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. When did you use the, start using the Fitbit before the-the Apple Watch.

Participant - 2016-15-16?

Interviewer - So...

Participant - Probably quite a while ago.

Interviewer - From before the onset of your fatigue?

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - How do you use these, this device?

Participant - So I really like that I can monitor my sleep quality.
Prior to-to doing that, or if you know I'm not wearing it, I'm just like, oh, oh, I was in bed for eight hours. It doesn't mean I was actually getting... I was a) actually asleep or b) it was decent quality sleep. So yeah, I can see that I, you know, even if when I am asleep, I'm not getting... proper R.E.M. You know, restorative sleep.
I used to use it a lot more for step counting.
For a while I had quite sort of... I don't know quite sad relationship with I guess because I used to be very active and now I'm much less active. I had to switch off the things that like the reminder to move. It's like mate, I know, I'd love to move, it's not happening. So yeah, I-I...
You know, I've-I've brought down all the things like, you know, your step goal and stuff because I kind of think 10,000 is just pulled out of the air anyway. Probably. It's probably not based on solid research. So yeah, I-I-I halved that and... that feels like a bit more of a-a reasonable goal, and sometimes I have trouble making anything like that, but yeah, monitoring how well I'm sleeping with how much I feel able to do without destroying myself.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you. When do you use the device?

Participant - So pretty much all the time.
There's usually like, I don't know, a half day or a night per week that I haven't charged it, so it... isn't functioning, but I'm pretty much all the time.

Interviewer - OK, just to put a little different aspect on that question.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - When do you check it?
How often in the day or at specific points in the day or anything like that?

Participant - Yeah, I tend to check it when I first wake up in the morning to see how well I've slept.
I sometimes don't do that. If I... I won't remember to do it if I feel all right, I guess. It's-it's when I wake up and go "Oh, God!" That's when I-I will check and be like, "Oh, OK. Well, that explains it." And I will look in the evening. You know, once I've done the things I need to do in the day and go "Oh, OK. You know, I actually walked a fair bit more. I did a fair bit more..."
If I have, I'll check it during the day. If I've been doing activities that I've, I feel that my heart rate's increasing.
I didn't used to be particularly aware of my heart rate, but... I've had COVID like four Times now, and I'm aware of how much it affects my-my heart rate, so I'm just a bit more aware of that now.
I also take asthma medications that can do weird things as my pulse so, yeah.
Usually like beginning and end of the day. But if-if things are weird, I will check in during the day.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So you've answered, how frequently do you use it as well. So that's the end of the second section.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Third section now is those two things interacting.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities that caused you a decision to use the device?

Participant - Umm...
I mean, sleeping isn't really an activity, is it? I really, yeah, the fact that it monitors my sleep, my sleep quality is really helpful. But yeah, because I'm not doing anything like as much like, exercise and in direct commas like I-I don't go 'running' for example anymore. Using a wearable device and just seeing like how much it adds up when I'm just wandering around campus or things like that. Like for a while I-I was using Strava alongside it and I was literally monitoring "Look, I went for a walk... around Sainsbury's."

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Anything. Like my goals very much changed. So yeah, I do find it really interesting to look at it after I've been a gig or a festival. I'm like, Oh my goodness like...
Yeah. How-how much, how much exercise is involved in just dancing.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. Can you give me examples of times the device has alerted you to over exertion?

Participant - So yeah, it doesn't...it doesn't actually alert me to overexertion because I've got everything set so much further down. Yeah It doesn't quite have that capacity, umm, feature set, y'know? But yeah, it doesn't really do it, it just relies on me keeping an eye out and going "Oh that's, I know myself that that's-that's a bit much."

Interviewer - Yeah, OK. I mean, the aspect of looking at it and, and it's alerting you by you looking back on something and going oh, that's a bit too much.

Participant - OK. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - That does also count...

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - ...even though it's not obviously the intuitive...

Participant - OK, cool.

Interviewer - ...initial thing. So yeah, if there's any specific examples you can remember of getting to the end of the day and looking back at it and going "Oh wow, that was a lot", then that counts as well.

Participant - Hmm.
Yeah, I think it's, if it's things like when I'm, when I'm away from-from home, away from my usual thing like so when I'm down in London, for example, that's where my research is based.
And pretty much every day that I was out doing my field work, I was getting back to my friends that I was staying out and going. "Oh, I'm going to feel that I'm going to feel that tomorrow, and I have to go and do field work again tomorrow." And because it's-it's outside of my usual routine...

Interviewer - Mmm.

Participant - ...so I was just like, "oh, but I'm just walking to the tube" and "I'm, oh, I'm just walking to the site" and "I'm just doing that" and how it all added up, I wasn't, I wasn't as aware as I would be in my sort of familiar surroundings.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Oh, that's a tricky one I feel like...
Actually, no. Yeah, outdoor swimming.
And like pool swimming, but mostly, but mostly sea swimming.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - That was part of the decision to, to go the Apple Watch because it was like I actually trust that it's waterproof. And yeah, I really wanted to-to be able to, because that's pretty much my-my only exercise outlet these days. I wanted to see like, you know, how-how that was contributing to, to things. So yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - I don't know if it was like so much directly a result of using the device, but...
Mm-hmm.
So I-I used to live in in Edinburgh, which is it's very hilly, but other than that it's, it's quite a sort of walkable city in lots of ways and I used to... I used to do a lot of walking like, you know, like I could get the bus or I could just walk. It'll take the same time.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - And being able to sort of correlate... flares in my symptoms with saying
"Actually you know what? You're pushing yourself to do stuff that you used to be able to do and that you feel like on some level you should still be able to do despite the fact that you know that you have become more disabled."
So I think having that proof almost, that like, you know the device is capturing this information. So I'm like, "Oh my God, I feel wrecked." And to be able to look at and go "Yeah, mate, that's because you-you walked up the hill and your-your BP was up to 126. No wonder you feel wrecked."
So yeah, it was it was, it was useful to sort of go "Oh, I'm not just being a drama queen" or something or, you know, this, it's, it's real. Like my body is, my vital signs are actually correlating with this as well as... fatigue, which I think I find can be quite a sort of nebulous... and like difficult to quantify thing?

Interviewer - OK. So it's more in the... the day-to-day how you get about places?

Participant - Mm-hmm.
Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - I think I would be more likely to still push myself to do things like, yes, your sort of day-to-day stuff like I was mentioning. Like walk places rather than getting public transport or take the stairs instead of the lift and oh God, you know when you say something out loud and you're like, "Oh, that is that is just... all of the internalised ableism right there."

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - But yeah.
Yeah, I think like I know what my EDS is like and I know that doing things like trying to go running would be a terrible idea. I, if I didn't have this sort of... evidence, this sort of backup from-from a wearable device... I fully think that's the sort of thing I would just be like, you know, "Go on, do it, do it anyway. You-you know, you've no reason not to. You totally should."

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - So yeah, it's-it-it really...it helps me to... visualise, I guess and quantify and say "Look, nah. Your body's saying very clearly this is this is difficult for me."

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Yeah. I like graphs. I like things that I can see as opposed to just being like...I-I know the difference between fatigue and "Oh, I'm a bit tired", but I-I can be really harsh to myself and be like, "Oh, you're just being you're just being a wuss", ugh, gross, yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your a decision to choose this device, or indeed the previous device?

Participant - Well, the previous device like I-I had so many different Fitbits because I kept, they kept just like dying on me in new and exciting ways, like things would just fall apart or stop functioning and it feels like is this, is this like built in because I would contact the company and be like "Hey this thing is not working" and they'd just be like "Cool we'll just send you a new one." Oh okay. Cool. Thanks. I don't want to spend any more money but like wow Okay.
And also my-my, I was getting like an allergy reaction to wearing it like where the-the wearable sits on my skin. In fact, it was very much like, "No, that's a you problem" like I don't think it is lots of people seem to be experiencing this.
And I haven't had that with the Apple Watch. I was like, I know Apple are not a friendly company. They're quite a big evil conglomerate, but they... the devices seem to last longer. They don't seem to have the same like allergy thing.
And they had good ratings in terms of their waterproofness so... I might have gone off piece from the question there, sorry.

Interviewer - No, it's no problem. Like I said, there is no off-off piece really. So it's more of a, the transfer from Fitbit to the Apple Watch was more of a practical decision in-in terms of reliability, yeah.

Participant - Yeah, reliability and yeah, like saying I do still want to be using wearable tech to monitor my energy levels and you know, yadda yadda, but ideally one that isn't making me try and tear my skin.

Interviewer - Fair enough. A minimum requirement I think.

Participant - Right? Right.

Interviewer - Thank you very much. Right, that's the end of Section 3.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - So it's just the short little speculative section at the end now. Can you give- oh, no, that's the one I just read. Thank you, brain. What form would your ideal device take?

Participant - I quite like the fact that it's, you know, ideally I do like, you know, I-previously to wearing wearable tech. I wore a watch, so it feels quite normal for me. That's like, yeah, that's fine. Ideally, it's such a silly one because I'm hyper mobile. I often press like the little button on the side of it when I don't mean to because my wrist bends that far back just normally, so yeah, in my shiny ideal world, just like a watch. That's great. But with the buttons on the side that...
Or the choice just to change where things were so that, yeah, my, my dodgy joints aren't... changing settings without my knowledge.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Just a note, you have the, so then you have the hypermobile flavour of EDS?

Participant - Yes, yes.

Interviewer - And you have hypermobile, have hypermobility and the symptoms of that that go with, correct?

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - OK, good. Good, because that contributes to fatigue as far as I'm aware. So I think that's-

Participant - I'm really bad at not specifying. I'm like, oh, actually this is the sort of thing that it's good to specify.

Interviewer - If anything, I can say "Hey, someone not quite remembering to mention the-the boundaries of their condition is brain fog and memory issues."

Participant - Right, right?

Interviewer - So it's-it's-it's not an uncommon thing in this study at all...

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - ...to have something come up later and go "Oh wait you have that?" It's completely fine.

Participant - Yeah. No, I can imagine people like, oh, yeah, no, I didn't mention when we meant to.

Interviewer - Yeah, I mean, I've done the same. I've not mentioned something that I was meant to at the start and whatnot. So we're all as bad as each other. It's fine.

Participant - It's-it's true.

Interviewer - What functions would you remove?

Participant - Umm. Any of the things that are really pushing it to be like "Get fitter!" Like I see the benefit of like the reminders to move, but for me that was... it was a reminder that I had become significantly more disabled and it just hurt to be honest. It made me quite sad and I think there is... like disability is neutral. It's not a bad thing to be disabled, but when your symptoms have changed quite dramatically and you, I think there's a period of mourning, for like the-the things that you used to be able to do and the way that your life used to be. And for me that just was quite a painful reminder.
I used to have a very active job and now my job is mostly sitting down in-in a, you know, in an office chair especially designed for my dodgy joints to support it in different positions. You know, none of this used to be considerations that I had to make. So yeah, I would, I would like to remove the functions that... assume that I am a... non disabled, non chronically ill person.

Interviewer - OK, so to...
To reword it, would you say that you would like to... be able to repurpose the device so that it's not treating you as a fit person who wants to get fitter?

Participant - Yes. Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. Thank you very much. And last but not least, what functions would you add?

Participant - To be honest, like I don't use half the functionality on the damn thing like...
Better battery life, probably, and partly that was just me being cheap and not buying a model with better battery life. But yeah, I would, I would use more of its functionality, like its GPS or using it and start like carting my phone around to listen to music if I wasn't like, "Oh man, it's just going to munch through the battery." So yeah.
That's the main thing, to be honest.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Yeah, just like, oh, and possibly a bit more like, difficult to break. Like, I put a screen protector on it. But like, I'm...
Yeah.
I-I-I'm not like diagnosed with.... anything of, I forgot the word, clumsiness generally, but yeah, I've been told that they're strong, strong, indicating symptoms of those sorts of things. Like, yeah, I'm... I will just bang into things and it's-it's just not uncommon of me to like, smack the outside of my arm off like a wall. And I'm like "I spent so much money on that Apple Watch. Is it OK?" So yeah. But again, it's-it's the planned obsolescence. I think that they've built into these things.

Interviewer - OK, lovely. Thank you very much. So I'm just going to give a quick run over of each section again...

Participant - Mhmm

Interviewer - ...just in case there's anything you'd, you'd like to bring up. So section one was about your condition, the, so, the conditions, you have, the symptoms that you have and how they affect your life.
Section #2 was about your device, how you use it, when you use it and your previous devices as well.
Section 3 was about how these things interact. So why you started using the device, what it does in relation to your conditions.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Um, things you may have started or stopped as a result, and Section 4 was the speculative section about your ideal device.

Participant - Yeah. Cool. No, I don't think there's anything else I want to add.

Interviewer - OK.
Lovely. Lovely. Thank you very much. I will stop the recording then.

title: P15 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - Come on Teams, you can do it. There we go.

Participant - There you go.

Interviewer - Right question, #1. So in the last month, do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Yes, ummm, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - I'll repeat everything back just to make sure I've heard it right.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - Do you need to rest more?

Participant - Oh yeah, worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Yeah.
Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - Yeah.
No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you lack energy?

Participant - (laughs) Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Oh, no worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you feel weak?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. And how is your memory?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Lovely lovely. Right. The...The-my favourite thing about that survey in particular is "do you make slips of the tongue when speaking" for some reason the, the original survey is "do you make slips of the tongue when sleeping?"
That's not quite right.

Participant - That's not quite right yep.

Interviewer - Something about that's off.

Participant - Somewhere there, that's a typo.

Interviewer - Right. OK. So the next one, I'm just going to type up the scale, but this uses numbers and if I can spell things correctly, that would help. So it's uses a basic number scale.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - So it would just be like saying ah, 6 or whatever it is.

Participant - Yep, yep.
Yeah, yeah. So a standard Likert. OK.

Interviewer - Yes. Yeah, yeah. The Likert scale.

Participant - Yeah, up to, so top is 10 or 7.

Interviewer - Seven, this is one to seven with obviously 2-3 and five and six being intermediate values.

Participant - Nothing....To seven.
Yep, Yep. Gotcha. Cool.

Interviewer - OK. Happy happy. Right. This is in the last week as opposed to the last month.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - OK, so, during the past week I have found that my motivation is lower when I am fatigued.

Participant - Uh...6.

Interviewer - 6. Exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - (laughs) 7.

Interviewer - Seven, I am easily fatigued.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven. My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven. If I would actually press the key, that would help. Fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven and fatigue interferes with my work, family or social life.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven. Lovely. Lovely.
Just the one more, this is this is the quirky one. It also uses a slightly strange scale, so this uses 0 to 3, and it's about the chances of falling asleep.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - This, it doesn't use a specific time scale.

Participant - Yeah.
Yes.

Interviewer - It's more if this happened now, what would be your expectation? So there's the scale.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - So there's the scale.

Participant - OK, cool. Yep.

Interviewer - OK, Zero to three.
So the first circumstance is sitting and reading.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, watching TV.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, sitting inactive in a public place, for example, a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Zero, as a passenger in a car for an hour without a brake.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - 2.
Lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three. Sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - 0. Sitting quietly after lunch in brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - Two, and in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - I hope you're not driving but three.

Interviewer - Three, it-this is what I mean. It doesn't specify. I hope you're the passenger, but...

Participant - Yes, let's assume that I am because I can't drive.

Interviewer - Yeah, let-let's assume that. Yeah. OK. So that is that done. So...and now it's the lovely big talky bit, OK.

Participant - Cool.

Interviewer - I...
OK. So, do you, just going to check in, do you need a break at all?

Participant - No, no, I'm good.

Interviewer - Perfect. So this big talky bit is 20 questions split into four sections. The 1st is about the conditions, the 2nd is about the devices, the third is about these two things interacting, and the 4th is at some speculative maguff.

Participant - Cool. Sounds good.

Interviewer - So question number one, what chronic- Oh, sorry. You can also remember something and loop back if you want.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - There's no obligation to answer the question specifically in the order. OK?

Participant - Yeah, cool. Yeah, perfect.

Interviewer - Good. Question number one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - I have functional neurological disorder. Specifically, I have a functional mobility disorder.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - I also have mild Vertigo, mild visual distortion which does impact on the mobility at times, you know, combined with that you know your vision is slightly wonky, your balance is slightly wonky, and you can't always make your legs work. Yes, all of that combines to mobility problems.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Yeah. So FND is when you have a software fault in your brain and the signalling goes wrong, structurally, totally normal, signal fault, regular. So like a computer blue screening, my blue screening is that I shake and that I also look fairly parkinsonian in my walking and things like that so I get slower and slower and slower the more I walk. It also triggers the shakes, so that's not good as... so I use an electric wheelchair the vast majority of the time to get around and that includes getting around my bungalow which is about 15 steps from my bed to my loop sort of thing. But yeah, use the wheelchair all the time. Significant amounts of fatigue that go with that and with the fatigue as the brain, brain, fog. And then of course, you do get the anxiety and depression gremlins popping up occasionally.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you being able to do?

Participant - Walking.

Interviewer - Nice and simple right to the point.

Participant - It's very simple. I-I do not walk. I pretty much, I-I can transfer to and from the chair. I can walk a very short distance, but I try not to because it aggravates everything. So I don't.
But that then impacts on everything else that you do. So, and the energy limitation means that you have to pace lots of energy. So not too many things of going outside and doing things too often together, pace what activities you're doing.
Work from home. Work mostly from beds.
Yeah, that's sort of thing.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your condition inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - 30th of July 1998. When I started shaking, I started shaking one day. Suddenly out of the blue.

Interviewer - OK, OK.

Participant - That's basically it. Brain went on the Fritz, then haven't, haven't walked normally and consistently since then, and it's gotten worse. So for the 1st 10 years or so of my condition I did not need a wheelchair and then it became, I became slower and slower and slower in my walking and it became more-more of an energy drain to do so. And so I got a wheelchair and I was initially a very part-time user.
And that has increased to now I'm a permanent wheelchair user.

Interviewer - OK, so that first instance, if you're comfortable doing so, can you describe to me the difference it made in, in the onset?

Participant - So I was working in the labs as a postdoctoral researcher...doing, I was working on a mouse model of of rheumatoid arthritis, and I started shaking and I couldn't stop shaking.
And it did affect my walking right from the get go in that I was shaking and shaking so fiercely that my walking was affected. I got hospitalised fairly quickly and they ran every single test and they all came up negative so it was FND...eventually. It took about six years to actually get that diagnosis.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. I'm going to read between the lines. I'm assuming with your descriptions of fatigue, is there some sort of extended exhaustion, some sort of PEM type behaviour?

Participant - Oh yeah, PEM is, yeah, definitely PEM and it's a really erratic PEM. It's not a "if I do too much one day it will be two days later and then I will crash the next day". I can crash immediately, I can crash the next day, I can crash the next week.
I do try and put in place what I call planned crash landings when I've done things that I know are going to be a bit much, but they, it rarely hits on those days even though I tried to plan it for all the days when I think it might hit and all the rest of it.
I basically pace as best I can, try as best I can, and then it's as variable as it can be.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - But you know, if I've overdone it, the EPM (PEM) can last for quite extended periods of times. So for example, I no longer lecture undergraduates because the last time I did that it took me 3 months to recover.

Interviewer - Yeah, PEM is not, not nice. Yeah. Thank you very much.

Participant - It's a bugger. No, it's a bugger. If you really crash it, it will take you months to recover. If I crash it minorly. It's a few days. If it's, you know, fairly standard that it's a week or two and then yeah, it's not uncommon that at least once a year I'll crash it fairly hard and takes me a while to really come back.

Interviewer - Thank you very much. I need to use that description of erratic actually that fits quite well.

Participant - Yeah, I mean, I know some people for whom they can always predict with clinical precision of it, "I'll do something too much on Wednesday. Fridays a wipe out definitely" and I'm kind of going "how lucky for you. I wish my symptoms was at least that predictable. I mean, it's a bugger it's a wipeout, but at least you can predict it!"

Interviewer - Yeah, yeah, I know exactly.

Participant - And also, although I can usually predict the things that will trigger it to be a crash, it sometimes just does it because it wants to.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yeah. No. OK, thank you very much. Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities? If brain fog inhibits this, please do say so.

Participant - Yeah.
It's actually been reasonably good by this afternoon. I've-I've seemed to have warmed up. I was struggling a bit this morning, so brain fog.
I have always been very bad with names. I'm increasingly bad with faces, so I-I-I'm almost face blind. So that's part of the brain fog.
I and then it also that travels over to other abstract concepts such as you know mod- names of models or authors or you know, being able to find the name for something. Yeah, but that that drops out fairly regularly.
And that's kind of the baseline stuff. And then if it's having a bad brain fog day, then I will struggle to string sentences together and stuff and it gets the point of I, I call it almost... It's almost a migraine level.
In that I don't get pain, but I get the impact on my cognition and I get to be I end up speaking very slowly and I really struggle to put find the words and it's a really weird one. It's kind of I know what I want to say. The, the breath, the mouth just can't say it so it can do that.
Also, when reading large chunks of text or writing larger chunks of text, I can, you know, send back a quick text message or quickly respond to an e-mail. But if you're wanting me to coordinate my diary, I get what I call diaritis and...things get put in the wrong time on the wrong date, so I turn up at the wrong time in the wrong place and that sort of thing. But then also then, like I say, reading or writing more complex screens of text, etcetera. Yeah, I try not to write when I'm badly brain fogged, because then I rewrite, read it on the next day when I've got more brain and then it's sort of what the f*** is that? I have no idea. It's totally all over the place. It doesn't make sense. It's not well structured, and English is obviously an optional language in my brain at that day, so I try not to do that anymore. But it's also when you're trying to critique a student's work or colleagues, yknow, grant application or whatever it is you're looking at, really hard to look at it and kind of make any sense of it. You know, you end up rereading sentences multiple times, and they still don't make any sense or you look at it and go, oh, it's all fine.
And knowing that done well, it isn't that, but you've got to go back and look at it again at another time to actually apply critical thinking and critique to it. So it's that sort of thing, really helpful in the academic post.

Interviewer - OK.
Very, very, very, very helpful.
Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - (cackles) Yeah.
Yeah. Sleep schedule. That's novel novelty. I described my sleep as I sleep like a baby.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - A really, really young one. So I get 2 hour blocks of sleep if I'm doing well, 4 hours if I'm doing exceptionally well, and I don't sleep more than four hours at a time and then-

Interviewer - So...
So sorry, just to just to interject, you sleep like a... teams has dropped out for a moment.

Participant - Baby.

Interviewer - A what sorry?

Participant - Like a baby.

Interviewer - Like a baby, OK, yes.

Participant - But a real one, not, not the oh sleeps like a baby. They're totally undisturbable, and all the rest of it. No. No wakes up every two to four hours like a baby will wake up at the slightest external noise will you know?

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Yeah, badly add menopause into this sort of symptoms. And yes, so hot flushes and palpitations and occasional panic attacks waking up into a panic attack has been a recent thing that I've done a bit of as well. That's fun.
Not. Highly recommend not doing that one. It's like somebody set off a fire alarm. Basically, you wake up in a panic attack and it's like, hey, what's going on? Oh, f***. It's just that right, deep breathing and buggery, it's a pest.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Umm.
…Does poor sleep disrupt, of course. Makes it even worse. But my poor sleep is normal. It can be even poorer, but the baseline is it f****** sucks. So yeah, but I can also then have periods where I have to sleep...
18-20 hours in a day, so I sleep and I eat and that's all I do. But they're not 20 hour blocks. They're two to four hours. Wake up, do a little, sleep, wake up, do a little, sleep.
So yeah, it's, it's sucky. I'm addicted to audiobooks because they're the only thing that keep my brain from going totally crazy whilst I'm trying to sleep.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So in terms of your sleep schedule, making your other symptoms worse, it's difficult to describe because the disruption to the sleep schedule is there all of the time.

Participant - Yeah.
It's all the ways there, has been right since the beginning of the FND is that the sleep has just always been absolutely f*****.

Interviewer - OK.
Yeah. OK, lovely. Thank you very much. So that's the end of the first section.

Participant - Oooh!

Interviewer - So the second section now focuses on devices.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - I have the visible tracker and app and I've been using it oh about a year and a half, something like that.

Interviewer - OK. Have you had anything previous to that?

Participant - Nah.

Interviewer - OK.
How do you use this...this application?

Participant - It's been quite useful in identifying how much energy goes into a typical activity, so it-it has what it calls pace points and you have a set...budget of pace points per day. Can't remember what it gave me, but because all of my days are so variable, I just disregarded it and get to gave myself 20 and that seems to be about the right level for if you're not going to totally blast yourself out of the water, 20 is around...right for my pace point, I think it gave me less and it was sort of no, that's that's just silly. I won't-I won't even manage to do anything if I was working on 12 pace points or something. I think it was. So it's sort of nah. So I overrode and sort of gave myself 20 and that seems to work reasonably well, it's helpful to know that a shower is around 1 pace point. It's helpful to know that an hour on a team's meeting is 1.2 pace points. But if you want to have that very same meeting face to face on-on campus, then it will be 3.6 pace points and it's really helpful to be able to tell my colleagues that seeing you face to face is three times the energy expenditure...

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - ...and therefore, yes, that explains why I don't do it that much and all the rest of it. It also helps me identify when the system is crashing badly and and I-it's flipping. So I had an experience this summer when I had a bit of a bad crash whereby like I say, my average hour is usually about 1.2 pace points when I'm working and stuff.
I actually was, you know, really struggling with energy, really not having restful rest. And I looked at the pace points for a period when I was just lying down and listening to an audio book. I was awake, but I was lying down flat.
And that was over 3 points. I think it was 3.2 pace points. My system had just gone, *bink!* and just decided that instead of resting, we'll just go crazy and it's sort of a, oh, right. f*** it. The system's gone crazy. I now have to totally stop. And that was the point at which I took a week sick leave and rested fiercely so that I reprogrammed it to not go crazy when you're trying to rest.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So it was helpful to be able to identify that and helpful to be able to kind of go, right, that's it. Stop. I didn't know I had to, but it was a... Yeah. f*** it. If resting is actually taking more energy than the teams meeting you had beforehand, then the system is absolutely screwed and you have to reset it by fiercely resting at it and trying to get it to reset. So it's been useful in that way. I will admit that I am really averse to things that ping at me and and stuff like that I hate e-mail notifications and all the rest of it, so I do not have any of the notifications that you can have on it set up so you know it can ping at you. If you will get into your overexertion zone and all the rest of it. I don't do that because that would drive me nuts.
And often I don't have a choice when I'm doing those things anyway. I'm on a train. I've got to get off it. I've got to get going. I've got, you know, work to do, etcetera. So it's kind of, it's trying to pace within that, but also within the pragmatism of, well, yeah, I'm lucky. I don't have a system that...completely crashes all the time and I have very few pace points in a day to manage. I have enough that I've got some leeway and I can keep doing my job so I get on and do the job, but it does help me, you know...
I look at it erratically through the day and it helps me keep track of am I doing OK and stuff, but equally...
The days that I'm fully crashed, of course I'm using far, far less pace points because I'm just asleep, but that's not actually reflective of the fact that I, do I have energy no, because effectively that day is a 5 pace point day. So you know, it's kind of so it's, it's-it like I say, it's actually quite helpful in terms of explaining to colleagues what the energy limitations are. So you know, I talk about spoon theory with them and stuff and I took and I sort of explained that, oh, and I've got this tracker and it shows me my spoon allocation etcetera.
And what I'm using them on and by the way, being here with you is a very high activity thing, or doing that interview was really quite high stress and so I recognise that and that sort of thing. So it's it's useful in that way. But I will admit...that I am not...I-I don't use it to sort of go "Right that's it. I've used my pace points for the day. I stop now." I usually don't have that choice, or at least not realistically. So I just try and make sure that my days are generally in the 20 zone.
And it also flags to me if a day has been particularly hard. So I had the most horrendous journey back from Coventry the other day. And yeah, the-the pace points went into the 40s and it's sort of OK, right? I'm really glad I've got tomorrow marked as a, you know, planned crash landing.
But yeah, let's-let's keep it gentle since then. And that's part of the reason why you're "how have you been in the last month" survey was coming up with so many. Oh, yeah, that that's the maximum because that's what I-I've crashed. I've crashed myself a bit this month because... of awful train journeys, et cetera, getting to and from Coventry so...

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - But yeah, not a lot of choice about that. You know, it's kind of either you get home when you don't get home, you know, so.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. You've answered the next two questions in that which was when and how frequently, so lovely, lovely.

Participant - Yay.

Interviewer - I will just ask because I know with visible there's several different levels of subscriptions and whatnot.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - What, what level are you at and do you have the device in addition to the application?

Participant - So I have the device that, the tracker on the arm. I have the version one version of that so it doesn't do night time tracking and things like that and I have I think the basic version of the, of the app etcetera.
Like I say, I find it useful, but I-I-I also know my...
OCD tendencies to would, would go, it would feed those gremlins a little too well, and I would get a bit too, you know, stuck on "Oh my God, it's taking me this many points" so no, I don't want to be doing that. I just need to get on with my life. It helps me review what I've done.
It helps me plan a bit, but I refuse to use it too much to track every minute as it were.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of Section 2. So the third section of four set of four, can you give me examples of activities that caused you a decision to use the device?

Participant - Sorry, what? Say again, I didn't understand that.

Interviewer - Can you? I can reword it if you like?

Participant - Yeah, please.
