Interviewer - Oh, now I've got to think about rewording it. Can you describe to me something that...made you decide to go and get a device.

Participant - Oh, right. Yeah, OK. It was case of, you know, like I say, dealing with a very variable energy levels and very variable responses to fatiguing activities.
I thought that data might help me track that a bit more. It kind of does a bit, but it also highlights that, yeah, some of that stuff is really quite variable so (laughs).

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the device alerted you to overexertion? Now, I know you don't use the notifications. You've said that already.

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - This would in, with your respect, this would be more, in retrospect.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of times when you've checked it and gone afterwards "Oh well, that was a lot."

Participant - Yeah, I mean, like I say, it allows you to tag zones and find out how many pace points you used on a certain activity. So it's been useful for me to know that like I say, an MS Teams meetings is usually 1.2 pace points per hour.
It's useful to know that if I was to do that face to face, that's three times the amount. It's useful to know that if I'm travelling on the bus, it's, you know, I'm pushing it towards heading up towards 2 pace points an hour in that sort of zone. Obviously I'm not necessarily travelling for that long, but you know it's a high activity, high exertion activity. So it helps me identify the things that are more high exertion. It helps me identify when my system has gone crazy. Like when I say over the summer where I had overexerted myself and...rest was not restful anymore and it absolutely correlated with that that it identified that yeah, when I was lying down and resting, it was not restful and the base pointing did highlight that that was yes, that that was a true....you know that was scientifically provable as well as what it felt like to me. So yeah, because I mean, part of FND is that effectively part of it is that the feedback loops have gone crazy and that the brain is not interpreting the input from the systems, whatever they are, correctly, so it's quite helpful to have an objective observer on that and to double check in as to what's going on and stuff. So yeah, it's kind of helpful to know what activities or higher-higher load, what-what roughly they usually take. So you know it's knowing that...a shower is at least a whole pace point, and it's kind of yeah, if it's a day when you're looking at and going, there's a lot going on and I'm already tired, OK? It's wet wipes today rather than having a shower. And it allows you to make those sorts of decisions and be a bit more sensible about...
you know, yeah, I love my showers, but yeah, it's high temperatures, you've got always got problems with all of your automated processes. Let's, let's jump that today.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, perfect. So it's used more as a as a confirmation...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...and like you said, like an impartial observer than...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...a than a kind of something that taps you on the shoulder and tells you to stop instead.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, yeah. No, I couldn't bear that.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, lovely. Thank you very much. This is in particular one of the questions that tends to not apply to about 50% of people. So if this doesn't apply to you, that's completely fine. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Nope, no.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - Like I say, it really did highlight that showers were quite high pace points and therefore I probably shower less now than I used to. And I, you know, I make a choice to use fresh wipes instead.
It has allowed me to explain to my colleagues why I don't do the face to face meetings.
But I didn't do them anyway. It just helps me explain it a bit easier. So yeah. So it hasn't, per se stopped, but yeah.
No, I think more that it's the pacing. So I do similar things, but I just make sure I'm pacing the energy and stuff.

Interviewer - So it's-it's less about stopping things and more about timing them to when you have the points to do it.

Participant - Planning, yeah.
Yeah, that's it.

Interviewer - OK, lovely. Lovely. Thank you. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - Like I say, I think that time when I was crashing in the summer, I might have kept going a bit longer had I not spotted that my my rest was not restful and that that really was true and it's sort of, oh, f***, that's the system got totally wonky. Let's stop.
Umm.
That's really about it.

Interviewer - OK. Obviously you've mentioned a few things that I think will be an answer to this question. So if you feel that way, just refer back to what you've said before. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - Yeah.
Yeah. I mean it, like I say, it's been that usefulness of working out that there's about a 20 pace point budget for the day and then tracking roughly how many pace points it takes to do given activities.
And so on. And so that I know how I schedule the day, when I know that I'm going to have to schedule some rests in and things like that and...
Yeah, that that's really it. It's the scheduling.

Interviewer - OK, I'm going to add my own little question in here because I have a feeling this is quite relevant. Does things like the brain fog and the cognitive symptoms mean that coming up with specific examples or remembering specific events is quite difficult and instead they kind of blend into this general memory soup?

Participant - No, it like I say, it's reasonable this afternoon, so we're OK on remembering exact examples. It's just, yeah, like I say, I'm probably a relatively poor user of it, so I don't...
I don't don't rely on it a huge amount, but it is a useful quick look at and track.
Also, it's not that good at spotting my trends because it does this thing of "every day as a 20 pace point day" and crash days are 5 pace point days, so it doesn't do that variation which is actually would be more useful so it sometimes thinks, "oh, you're having a great day" when I'm kind of going "no, that's because I just didn't do anything." And so that's actually isn't a good day. So it's, yeah, it's it. Although it's good in that it doesn't promote you to do activity like some activity trackers do that, you know they're giving you messages of "oh do more etcetera." And this is good in that in terms of the messaging it gives you is around pacing yourself and doing planning and being cautious etcetera. So those are more helpful messages.
Yeah, it's-it's kind of... It's still only a computer, it's still only tracking my heart rate and heart rate variability. That's pretty much all it's tracking plus my symptom reporting which is erratic as in my own regularness of doing that.
So yeah, it's...
It it can only do what it can do. You know, in terms of the limits of its algorithm, etcetera. So...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. 4th and final section, this is just three questions. Doesn't have to be based in current products. You can get imaginative if you want. What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - It would be more discreet, at the moment, that's still fairly visible when I'm wearing short sleeved things. I know that they've got like I say, so the new version can be worn on the wrist so it looks a bit more like watch and things like that, but I think...
Yeah, small and more discreet is definitely good. I think something that could track probably more variables, so O2 saturation, blood sugars possibly, and definitely tracking sleep.
I am considering getting the next version on so that I can actually have it that it tracks sleep and stuff like that. The battery life on version one is fairly crappy, pretty much does one day, and then you have to charge it whereas the new one has apparently a week or two battery life and it charges within an hour. So a lot more useful. So yeah, that's a really positive thing. And I think that needs to continue improving basically.
But yeah, I think having more variables would be helpful to-to so you actually got a more comprehensive, holistic view of how the body was doing. Because I absolutely know that my blood sugar levels are definitely absolutely critical to whether I can do stuff or not. I know that at times... I haven't managed to catch COVID as far as I know, probably have just asymptomatically, but I do know that if I get anything that is respiratory that my breathlessness can kick in quite seriously and that really does fatigue me a lot.
Like, say, tracking sleep and sleep quality so that it actually did track whether or not you were in deep sleep or not. And that sort of thing again would likely be helpful. And then again like I say, having a way of sort of saying because the app has sort of crashed, no crash, yes, no, you know, are you all, are you not having a day?
It's kind of, yeah, there needs to be a bit more nuance in that because it isn't just am I crushed or not. And for it to effectively reset the number of pace points for a day where you go, I was just resting that day and that's all I could do. That was, there was nothing else I could do. So yeah, I only used 5 pace points that day, but that was all that was available to me. In other words, it's 100% of what was available to me. Not that I only used 1/4 of my pace points that day, and it views it as the second at the moment, and it's when it's calculating things.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Umm.
Yeah. So it's that sort of thing. And I think if you were to make it that it was the most useful. It would also be linking in with things like medication adherence, etcetera, so that it would prompt you to take your meds, that sort of thing because obviously that really helps.
And if it's all on one device and you're not having to faff with multiple reminders, ping, you know things going off etcetera. And of course, medication adherence is it's one of my research areas. So it's one thing that one of the things I'm passionate about is that that can make a massive difference to people's quality of life, and I think if you're doing things that are around fatigue management, medication adherence, positive messaging, you know things that promote well-being like I say, I like the fact that the visible app is very much focusing on pacing and cautious energy use and that sort of thing, and that's really helpful. So it's that sort of mindset. I like the fact that the Visible guys are doing some research with the, you know, the data sets that they get.
Again, that I think is really critical that this is, you know, this is a free data set. It should be being researched on, you know, an anonymized date, you know, mega data set of everybody's stuff. But I think they need to be asking more questions. They have one monthly...effectively ADL question and it's kind of it's it's not detailed enough, it's not relevant, you know, and it again, it's the things of...
Being able to sort of say...
Because they do things like, you know, "you're far less mobile than the average person", and you kind of go. "Yeah, I use a f****** wheelchair. Guess what? No s***, Sherlock." So that's actually not a helpful output to sort of, say, "are you far less mobile than you usually are?" That's an output that's useful to you.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Sorry, I'm losing you a second. Bear with me. Come on, teams.
There we go. Right. Are we back?

Participant - Cool.
Are you? Can you hear me?

Interviewer - There's a little bit of interference, I'm just not sure if the recording- and are we back. Hello.

Participant - Yeah. Hi, can you hear me?

Interviewer - Oh OK, I can just about make you out, but I just know the recording won't pick it up properly.

Participant - Should I turn off my camera and see if that helps a bit?

Interviewer - Bear with me. I'll just have a look into if it's me or...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - The joys of working from home.

Participant - Oh yes.

Interviewer - Oh, OK. Hello. Oh, OK. Turn the camera off. Has that helped?

Participant - Oh yeah, yeah. I've turned the my camera off. Is that helpful?

Interviewer - Yes, it is right. I'll do, I'll do the same as well just in case.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, yes, indeed. Yes, I've got lovely men resurfacing all of our footpaths outside, so every once in a while they seem to vibrate the Internet lines a little heavily and we then have Internet connection problems. So it might be me.

Interviewer - Oh lovely.
How...how generous of them. So I-I lost you at about the point that you said it was telling you you were less mobile and well, yes, obviously.

Participant - I think...
Yeah. So you really need to have it that it's, it's not a problem as it were for the mega anonymized data sets. For it to say, yeah, you're less mobile than the average member of the population, but what you need as an individual is, is this a deterioration for you over time? And what does that really mean? And it's not just the what can you do? It's the impact aspect of it, you know. Are you still able to work full time? Are-are you still able to feel on top of your work that you're doing and whatever else it is, you know those sorts of things so you can track things, and so you can explain things because like I say, we're within awkward times in terms of gaining benefits and things like this that this sort of data could be useful for you to prove the impacts and stuff and that you are able to more easily download that data, use that data for yourself. Not that it can ever be shared with the Department of Total obscurity etcetera, it's-it's sort of no, we're not sharing it with those b*******, but that it can be useful to you to explain to others or to justify that. Yeah. Actually I can show you my track of energy levels over time and you can see it's still going down or it's still bizarrely low or whatever it is.

Interviewer - OK, I have a feeling you have in quite a lot of detail answered the next two questions, but I'll ask you them anyway in case there's anything else.

Participant - Cool.
Yeah.

Interviewer - What functions would you remove?

Participant - No, I don't think there's anything I'd remove because you can opt in and out of them and I can certainly see that there might be times when I would want it to prompt me about overexertion and things like that if I was really struggling or something. So no, I'm-I'm happy. I think it is that it has flexibility.
So you can pop in and out of various things, so yeah.

Interviewer - OK. So that's, so there's no mandatory functions like you would have with say, Fitbits and Garmin trackers and Samsung gears and everything where there are options with those devices that you can't turn off.

Participant - No, I mean this one, it will track your heart rate and heart rate variability and if that's all you do and you just stick it on on the morning and you do nothing, everything else is optional, you can do a morning check in, you can do an evening check in, you can, you can set it up that it alerts you to overexertion and things like this, but all of those are entirely optional.

Interviewer - OK. That's, that's really good actually.

Participant - It is.

Interviewer - OK, final question, what functions would you add? Obviously I think you've talked about this...in in detail already, but if there's anything else then feel free to add.

Participant - Yeah.
I think it'd be really positive if you could record for good days.
You know, I mean, I know that that a lot of this is around managing performance against the bad days, but if you don't track what it was, that also led up to you having a particularly good day, then you might not know be able to notice that and repeat that. So I think being able to sort of do a-a peak day, a good day etcetera.
It's-it's important.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - I'm all for, all for my appreciative inquiry.

Interviewer - Yeah. No, that, that sounds like a really good feature actually. I don't think anyone's mentioned that up to this point. It's always focus on the bad and not, not the good, isn't it?

Participant - Yeah. And, and I mean evolutionary it, it makes sense. We are absolutely, we're set up neurologically to focus on the disasters because you can't repeat those again and survive. But actually it's incredibly important psychologically that you understand, A) that you recognise, that you had a good day, but also that you can start planning for how you make those happen more often, how you plan for having successful times. And I think particularly in the context of having long term conditions that are energy limiting, focusing solely on the prevent it being a bad day can actually be quite bad and that's why I don't have things like the, the overexertion trackers and things like this. Because I'm kind of going. Yeah, I'm having fun. Usually when I'm doing that.

Interviewer - Yeah. Yeah. OK. Yeah. No, that that makes sense. It's-it's quite negative reinforcing usually.

Participant - Yeah.
That's it. That's it. And I think it's it would be cool if there was a "OK, let me track how am I managing to get the good days" and also tracking that those aren't triggering and particularly such bad days and things like this when you have really overdone it and stuff.
But like I say, I think it's important that you do track the good stuff as well.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, that that that's a really good, given us a really good suggestion. Thank you very much.

Participant - Like I say, as a researcher I I do like !appreciative inquiry! it's called as a model. It's-it's a really useful one to flip the agenda and sort of go "Yeah, we know how to do it disastrously badly. Does anybody know how to do this well?"

Interviewer - Yes, exactly.

Participant - And it's kind of, yeah, you know, and I work a lot within healthcare research and things like this. And I'm constantly saying "so what's the team that does it really well, who's the nurse that really is brilliant? Who's the physio that you'd refer your friends to? Right. I want to talk to them. I want to find out how they're doing it, in the midst of a NHS that is clearly breaking down. How are they still being brilliant?"

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yeah. No, exactly.

Participant - Because we don't learn from that and we really should. So that's why I sort of do the we should be asking about the good stuff as well.

Interviewer - No, that's, that's a wonderful approach and I think it's it's really good and really demonstrative of the situation that a lot of people in our sort of situations develop outlooks like this because bluntly it's very difficult to exist if you don't.

Participant - Oh, I've lost you.

Interviewer - Oh, am I back. Hello.

Participant - You're back now you're back.

Interviewer - Yeah. Good, good. I wonder if me closing the velux window because it started raining, decided to disturb my laptop. That would be very Microsoft.

Participant - It would be very Microsoft. Yeah. No, I mean it's, it's that thing of joy as an act of rebellion.

Interviewer - Yes. Yeah. No, exactly. I-I think it's-it's really important to reflect that a lot of the people in our situations develop this outlook because bluntly it's very difficult to exist if you don't.

Participant - Yeah.
Yeah.

Interviewer - So yeah, it's, it's wonderful to have that described by a participant in a more formal, researchy way, I suppose.

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - So yeah, thank you very much for that. I'm going to go do a quick overview of all of the sections just in case there's anything you are reminded over anything you want to add.

Participant - No worries at all.
Yep.

Interviewer - So section one was about your condition specifically.
So what chronic conditions you have the symptoms and how they relate to the impact on your life.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Section number 2 was about the device, when you started using it and how you use it. Section 3 was about the device-hmm... the use of the device and its impact on your life.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So decisions that you've made and things that you've changed and things that it's helped with.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And then Section 4 was the speculative ideal device section.

Participant - Yup. All sounds cool. Yep.

Interviewer - OK, nothing to add?

Participant - No, no, I think that covers all of it. So I hope that's useful to you.

Interviewer - Wonderful. Thank you very much. I'll stop recording a second.

title: P16 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - All right. OK. So do you need any breaks or anything?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK, good good. So this is 20 questions split into four different sections. The first one focuses on your conditions. The second one focuses on the devices or applications that you use. The third one, is focused on these things interacting and the 4th one right at the end is just a little speculative couple of questions, OK?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So question number one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - OK, fibromyalgia.
Umm.
So chronic fatigue syndrome, which is kind of a symptom of POTS, which is postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome.

Interviewer - OK. What was that first one, sorry.

Participant - Fibromyalgia.

Interviewer - Fibromyalgia. OK. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - OK, so fibromyalgia is...Like...Chronic pain, um throughout the body.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I would say.
Chronic fatigue is exactly what it sounds like. I don't know how to describe it otherwise. I guess with no, errr, like fatigue with no direct...obvious cause.
And then POTS is a syndrome that affects different parts of my autonomic nervous system, but...one of the...parts of the syndrome is essentially not being able to exercise without getting absolutely exhausted, and my heart rate going really high.

Interviewer - OK.
Forgive me if I'm wrong, but off of memory of other participants who have POTS...

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - ...that's heart rate instability usually going incredibly high when doing simple things and can be accompanied by dizziness.

Participant - Yes, that's correct.

Interviewer - OK.
Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - Umm.
Running.
Honestly, working out in general.
I guess not directly, but driving just because I don't feel comfortable getting behind the wheel of a car if I am concerned, I may like...pass out or start feeling bad, yeah.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - And yeah.

Interviewer - OK.
So anything with any component of-of...significant exercise, then, when you said working out in general, so that's most or all sports and that kind of thing, yeah.

Participant - Yes.
Yes.

Interviewer - OK. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - 13 years ago.

Interviewer - So 2012?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK, thank you. I'm going to read between the lines, extended exhaustion and post exertional malaise. Is that relevant to you?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yes. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity please?

Participant - Umm.
Taking a shower standing up for, say 15 minutes and then needing to lie down for half an hour afterwards.

Interviewer - OK. So it's a lot of just normal daily...activities needed to look after basic needs.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Do you suffer with the cognitive symptoms as well? Things like brain fog and word loss.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities please?

Participant - Umm.
Oh, I-I went grocery shopping and with brain fog and bought chicken nuggets. Despite being a vegetarian of...like 7 years because I misread it and I thought that they were vegan chicken nuggets.

Interviewer - Right. OK. I've-I've not heard that one before. That's really interesting.
Yeah. Yeah. No one's ever mentioned anything to do with packaging and, and how clear or not, that kind of thing can be.

Participant - Yeah, I've-I've... I think that one was surprising because it has never happened to me before. But it is. Yeah. Reading things and, like, misreading things is not uncommon with brain fog.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Umm.
Just.
I guess insomnia is that...

Interviewer - Umm...

Participant - ...descriptive enough or?

Interviewer - So...
If you have insomnia, then describing the insomnia would.
Be part of it, but then explaining how that can make the rest of, can affect the rest of your symptoms. And if they do in in what way it affects the rest of your symptoms. So I know for example with my...ADHD, If I hyper focus late at night, I might be up until 3:00 or 4:00 AM without realising it and then I have 4, 3-4 hours to sleep and I know that the next day...all of all of my movement will be inhibited because everything will feel like it's made of lead and my lungs won't be working properly and I will have horrible brain fog and all of this. So if you can, that kind of description on how a lack of sleep would then affect your other symptoms and conditions.

Participant - OK, so I sometimes have trouble falling asleep if I am in more pain or if I am feeling like my...blood pressure is too low or too high, or my heart rate is too low or too high, so it can take like hours to fall asleep and then I will need...to function the following day without as much sleep as I typically have, and it generally...makes it, like the fatigue after whatever activities I've done, makes it worse than like the normal amount of fatigue that I would feel.

Interviewer - So it just generally exacerbates everything fatigue related.

Participant - Yes.
Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of the first section. So the next section is focusing on devices and applications and trackers and all of that.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So what device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - I have a smart watch. I don't know. Do you want like the brand or?

Interviewer - If you can, if you can specify yes, but it's not necessary per say.

Participant - Yeah, it's called Withings, Scan Watch, I think is what it's called. And gosh, when did I, I think a year...ago?

Interviewer - OK.
Ohh I think I've found it. I've not heard of this one before.

Participant - Hmm.

Interviewer - Oh, OK, so it looks like an analogue wristwatch.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Oh.
Wow, OK, I've not seen anything...look that normal before.

Participant - Yeah, that's part of the reason why I got it. I also wanted, like continuous heart rate tracking. So because my heart rate can fluctuate a lot, I...and other like fitness watches on the market will have a general like-like an average heart rate over a period of time. And I-I wanted it to just like see the...fluctuations exactly kind of...

Interviewer - If I'm correct with...with pots, your heart rate can fluctuate to abnormal and then back to normal again within a minute. And most devices measure on a minute or by several minute basis so...

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - ...if I'm correct in your decision making, the, you needed something more frequent than that, because otherwise it will completely miss pots symptoms, correct?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK.
Do you-? Yeah. Excuse me. Have you used anything previously? Any other previous tools?

Participant - I, yes, I don't know how to describe it, but it was like a strap that goes around your chest to measure your heart rate.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - And then you can. I don't even remember how it worked because it was literal years ago, but it was not something that I could wear comfortably regularly.

Interviewer - OK. Then there's no-no memory of any possibility of brand because as you said it was years ago, correct.

Participant - Correct.

Interviewer - OK.
How do you use the, the wristwatch?

Participant - So because it tracks my heart rate, I can use it to like see trends over time. So ideally...my heart rate...will like not be at such an elevated level in like at rest. So like when I'm sleeping or sitting down or something so...right now I am attempting this like heart rate training protocol that's recommended for people with pots and it is,
it's kind of like hit training like the high intensity interval training. So yes, I that's why I got the watch.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Like for those purposes.
And then I also...
I don't know how accurate the, the sleep tracking is, but it is kind of just interesting to see.
Like how long I slept and average heart rate during sleep. And you know, whatever.

Interviewer - OK, so it's got the full sleep tracking in addition to the, the heart rate stuff.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So I'm assuming that you wear this all the time almost all the time.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. When do you check the-the device?

Participant - Um.
It kind of depends like what I, like why I'm checking it I guess...

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - ...if I suspect that my heart rate is really high, I will check it...like...several times a day. Sorry. What was the question?

Interviewer - So I mean, the original question is when do you use the device? But obviously wear it all the time, so I've changed it slightly to be when do you check the device, so when are you looking at the-the data it's recording?

Participant - Yes.
Yes. So I would say that I-I check it when I'm feeling like my heart rate is higher than it should be and then...I check the...like the app for sleep tracking or like step count and everything...like once at the end of the day, usually.

Interviewer - OK, so the only...guaranteed check is at the end of the day, but then you, you check it also if you're if you're feeling like you were experiencing symptoms.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
OK, so that's the end of Section 2.
Section 3. Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use this device?

Participant - Umm.
Example activities.
I guess...
Like...
Daily activities that would make me feel really exhausted.
And...
They shouldn't have made me feel exhausted.
So I...
I don't know. Like...
It-it-it offers some sort of...
Validation for the...
Symptoms that I'm experiencing that...
Oh gosh, I don't know how to wear this.
Yeah, it...

Interviewer - It's no problem. Take your time.

Participant - Yeah, it...
It's...
Yeah, I examples...
Oh, for example, if I walk for 5 minutes and I can, I'm feeling really exhausted and sweaty and overheated and...just not feeling well, I can check my heart rate...after doing that walk, and if it is high, which it often is very high, then I feel slightly less like...crazy.

Interviewer - So it's...
Less about specific activities and more around confirming your feelings when going through the day-to-day activities.

Participant - Yes, I-I would say so. And then as I mentioned before, I did initially get it with the purpose of being able to track my heart rate in order to keep it within certain...zones for a certain amount of time when doing like cardio.

Interviewer - OK. Do you do cardio anymore?

Participant - I haven't...I haven't been consistent with it, no.

Interviewer - OK.
Can you give me examples of times the device has alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - Hmm.
I...
I honestly don't think it has alerted me with like...
A formal notification or anything.

Interviewer - OK, to slightly change the definition of alerted, I suppose in retrospect has it...have you got examples when you've got to the end of the day and realise that you've overextended?

Participant - Oh yes, definitely. At the end of the day, I will look at the average heart rate and there is a little graph that it shows me.
So I can see like...
Oh, at 2:00 PM, my heart rate got really high and I think back and remember. "Oh, yes, I was, I don't know, walking at that point". So it, I guess it, alerts me when I'm looking back kind of.

Interviewer - Yeah. In this retrospect kind of way.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Started.
Umm.
Uh.
Uh.

Interviewer - If there are none, then just say that there are none.

Participant - Yeah, none.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - So there's nothing that you were doing and then you started using the device and it told you you were overextending when you did that certain activity?

Participant - Not, not really? I guess I...I can tell when I'm overexerting myself as a result of using the device, but I it hasn't actually stopped me from overexerting myself.

Interviewer - OK, no problem at all.
Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - I...
Activities that I would have started if I weren't using it?

Interviewer - If you'd like me to...If you'd like me to reword questions, just, just say so.

Participant - Ah.

Interviewer - So things you might have tried to start to do if you didn't have the information that the device was supplying you.

Participant - I can't think of...
Specific...
Activities I've...
Maybe I would have started jogging with my friends...
Out of like a sense of...
It's not really peer pressure, but like wanting to join in on group activities such as like a running club, but I...

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - ...Ha-... I Don't I-I didn't join that.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this device?

Participant - I'm not sure I understand the question.

Interviewer - So can you give me things that have happened that have...that tell you the using the device was the right decision.

Participant - Oh yeah...
Pretty much anytime I am...
Noticing that my heart rate is...
Abnormally high and...
I...
Just the fact that I like can know about those events...
In a...
Without needing to...
Like do anything additional if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Because before I would often I had um, a, I still have it, but it's a like a...cuff that goes around my wrist and it would measure my blood pressure and heart rate and so typically that would be the only time I would know what my heart rate was. And it obviously is only measuring it at that...exact point in time, whereas with the watch I can...
Look at trends in my heart rate...
Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, perfect. Thank you very much. So it-it performs the function of that cuff, but all of the time.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. So that is Section 3 done. So just Section 4, just three final questions. These are speculative. You don't need to be grounded in what is currently available or anything like that. You can get imaginative if you want.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Umm...
Hmm.
I guess...
Potentially less visually...
Obvious? IE...
The...
Smaller..
I don't know ring or something.

Interviewer - OK, so something less intrusive than something that goes around the entire wrist.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, what functions would you remove?

Participant - Oh.
I mean, I don't think I would necessarily remove anything.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Maybe...
Potentially the like number of calories like estimated calories burned, but that's just more of like a personal thing.

Interviewer - Yeah. Oh, I didn't realise that. Scan watch measures respiratory rate?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Oh.
Does that measure that all of the time, or is it just an activated...
something you-you activate for a short amount of time?

Participant - Yeah, you have to activate it.

Interviewer - OK.
Right. Sorry. I was just having a flick through the website during because I don't think I've ever heard of these devices before. What functions would you add?

Participant - Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Hmm.
And I can't think of anything.

Interviewer - So there's nothing with your symptoms that you feel you're assessing on your own and that you would like a device to do it all?

Participant - Oh, I guess I don't know if it would be like possible, but it would be nice if it could record blood pressure as well.

Interviewer - OK. Anything else?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK, lovely. So I'll give you a, a quick rundown of each section, just in case there's anything you're reminded of all that you want to add on.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So section one was about your condition, your conditions, the symptoms that you experience and how they affect you.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Section 2 was around the device, how you use it, previous devices, how frequently you use them and that kind of thing. Section 3 was around your symptoms and your device interacting during activities like overexertion and changes that you've made as a result of using the device.
And Section 4 was the little speculative bit we just did.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - OK. Is there anything you'd like to add to the, the recording at all?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. I'll stop that now.

title: P17 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - There we go. Right. OK. So these are three short surveys. They're NHS ones or ones that the NHS used. They're a little bit different because they are designed for people who have onset. So it's meant to be at the very start to work out how bad your condition is and if you have chronic conditions. The answers can feel a little bit wonky if you've had it for a long time. Don't worry so much about that. We know that's a limitation we're not concerned about that, so don't feel like there's a right or a wrong answer. Don't feel like you're giving numbers too low or too high or whatever. So yeah, it's nothing too... too important, I suppose so. The first, they each have their own scales. The first one has... that scale in the chat where.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So I'll ask you a question and you'll give me one of those answers.
And that that's the basic structure of this first one.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So- oh sorry, I forgot to say, this is the last month compared to your baseline.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So this is why it's described for onset. So it's meant to work out if you're worse in the last month than usual, which is why it can feel a bit wonky.

Participant - OK, OK, got it.

Interviewer - OK? Sorry, I forgot to mention that start.

Participant - No, that's OK.

Interviewer - OK, so for the last month do you have problems with tiredness?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Sorry. On the, on the scale in the chat.

Participant - Worse than usual?

Interviewer - Worse than usual.
I'll repeat each answer back to make sure I've not misheard it.
Do you need to rest more?

Participant - Yeah. Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, do you feel sleepy or drowsy?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual. Do you have problems starting things?

Participant - No worse than usual.

Interviewer - No worse than usual. Do you lack energy?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse than usual. Do you have less strength in your muscles?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.
Do you feel weak?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual.
Do you have difficulties concentrating?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual, do you make slips of the tongue when speaking?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse than usual. Do you find it more difficult to find the right word?

Participant - Worse than usual.

Interviewer - Worse than usual? And how is your memory?

Participant - Much worse than usual.

Interviewer - Much worse than usual. OK, that's the first one done.
So the second one... is within the last week instead of the last month.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - And it has a slightly different scale that I'm just typing up for you now.
OK. So the scale is numbers one to seven. So if you want to strongly disagree with something, you'd say one. If you want to slightly agree with something, you'd say 5. OK?

Participant - OK. So three is middle?

Interviewer - So 4 is the middle.

Participant - Four is middle, OK.

Interviewer - 4 is the middle. OK, so four is neutral, one is full disagree 7 is full agree, yeah?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - OK, so during the past week I have found that my motivation is lower when I am fatigued.

Participant - Two. Oh, wait, no, sorry, I got it the wrong way around.

Interviewer - Yeah, that's no problem.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - Six, exercise brings on my fatigue.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven, I am easily fatigued.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6, fatigue interferes with my physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue causes frequent problems for me.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7. My fatigue prevents sustained physical functioning.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with carrying out certain duties and responsibilities.

Participant - 6.

Interviewer - 6, fatigue is among my three most disabling symptoms.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - 7, fatigue interferes with my work, family or social life.

Participant - 7.

Interviewer - Seven. OK, thank you. So that's the second one done. Right, this third one's a little bit strange.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - It's about the chances of falling asleep.
It doesn't have a specific time frame, it's just if this was to happen to you today, what would be the chances of? So there's no in the last week or in the last month, OK?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - The scale I'm just typing up now.
The scale is in the chat.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So for example I would say during a scientific interview and you would say what are your chances of falling asleep...

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - ...just according to the number scale there.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - This is the oldest of the three. Some of the circumstances are nonspecific or a little bit wacky, and some of the descriptions can be a little bit wacky, OK?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So situation one, sitting and reading.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - 3.
And watching TV.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - Two, sitting inactive in a public place, for example a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, as a passenger in a car for an hour without a brake.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, and sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - 1.

Interviewer - One, sitting quietly after lunch in brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - 3.

Interviewer - Three, in a car while stopped in traffic.

Participant - 2.

Interviewer - 2.
OK, very good. Thank you very much. That's all the surveys out of the way. The thing that I like about that, that last one is it doesn't say if you're the driver or not, just that you're in a car and it stopped.

Participant - Yeah, well, I'm not, I don't think I can drive. I can't drive anyway, so I'm not bothered.

Interviewer - I think everyone's assumed that they're not driving, which I think is the sensible assumption.

Participant - Yeah, I don't I think I would trust myself to drive anyway because it's, yeah...

Interviewer - Yeah, no, fair enough.

Participant - Yes, yes.

Interviewer - Fair enough, right. OK. Do you need any pacing bricks or anything?

Participant - I'm OK. Thank you for asking, though, yeah.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Just like to check in every now and then, make sure you're doing all right.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So this is the main bit now. This is the talking bit. 20 questions. Are you the one who I sent the descriptions of each section to?

Participant - I think so, yeah.

Interviewer - Yes. So the first one's about conditions, symptoms and how they affect you. The second one is about your device, how you use it, when you use it. The third one is about how your device and your symptoms interact and the 4th bit is just the speculative... make your ideal thing section, OK?

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Good, good. So question 1- oh and obviously if a question doesn't apply to you, you can skip it. If you think of an answer to a question that's come before, you can say I'm just, I'd-I'd like to circle back to this question and then just add on to it as and when, OK.

Participant - Yeah, sounds good.

Interviewer - Good question one what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - So hyper mobile Ehlers Danlos syndrome, chronic fatigue syndrome.
And pots, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Main one would probably be fatigue and kind of generalised like joint and muscle pains. The Pots ones only really get activated when I'm like... standing or moving, so I can kind of minimise that with pacing. But those kinds of symptoms, I get like palpitations, shortness of breath, that kind of thing. And yeah, I would say those are my main symptoms.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you from being able to do?

Participant - I would say biggest one is probably like heavier housework, things like laundry vacuuming, cleaning, preparing meals can sometimes be a bit harder as well. I would say, exercise and stairs as well. Um, yeah

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - Anything kind of like, higher energy.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - Ever since I was a kid really. Because, like EDS, is obviously genetic, but the fatigue and pots symptoms really started when I was like 16.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.
OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - No problem.

Interviewer - I'm going to read between the lines with the chronic fatigue. Do you have extended exhaustion, PEM type symptoms?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah. Can you give me examples of extended exhaustion after an activity, please?

Participant - So like for example, if one day I have to go to... for example, I'm a medical student, so I have placements. So like the day after a placement I'm usually like, in bed all day.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - I'm not really able to do very much other than like basic self-care and like I usually don't move around very much, kind of confined to my room.

Interviewer - The, the minimalist bed bound kind of day?

Participant - Exactly.

Interviewer - Yeah, so I'm obviously I'm not a medical student. Placements are being in hospitals and...

Participant - Yeah, so hospitals and general practises.

Interviewer - Working a shift?

Participant - So it's like you're on your feet the whole time and interacting with people also. Practising like your clinical skills. So again, kind of upright moving and doing stuff like that. And that's usually like a full working day. It's around 8 hours and it really depends how like, it depends how long I'll be in PEM after that. If it's like a good week or maybe just like one day. But if it's a bad week, maybe, 3-4 kind of thing.

Interviewer - Yeah.
OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - No worries.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting activities?

Participant - Forgetting words, forgetting people's names, things with like birthdays and just like managing different aspects of life altogether, I guess.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Like I-I-I'll like forget people's names even though I've known them for a really long time and just be like who are you? And concentration is a big one as well.

Interviewer - Quick follow-up question, how does that affect your hospital placements?

Participant - It can be tricky, especially if I'm like interacting with patients, and I'll like, forget their name, make conversation. Usually I'm quite good at playing it off and kind of like cover it up, especially if I'm in a professional environment with my friends.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I'll be a bit more like open and tell them like oh sorry, just like having a brain problem.
But it is difficult on placement. I'll usually be like "Is it OK if I go to the bathroom?" Go to the bathroom, like compose myself. But yeah, it-it can be difficult to manage.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - It's really weird because like I'll be really tired, but I'll still not be able to fall asleep.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - It's more kind of like a, it's not like a fatigue that [inaudible] you smoothly. It's a fatigue that... makes kind of like moving, like even breathing seem tiring. But falling asleep is still hard. So I'll get insomnia sometimes. But I would say the main thing is like waking up is a nightmare.
Yeah, I'll-I'll have to set 10-20 alarms to be able to get up in the morning.

Interviewer - Just to ask, are you anything like me where you need two to three hours to get up?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - Where what most people will need one, because breakfast takes 40 minutes instead of 10. And all of this?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Shower takes a long time.

Interviewer - Yeah.
Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of the first section. We're on to devices now. What device or application do you currently use these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - So I used to-I used-I've recently got the visible band, so then obviously I only literally got it last week. But before that I was using the Garmin like watch and I was using that since 2023.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I would mainly use it to track like my heart rate obviously because when I started getting it, I was just diagnosed with pots and I was having like, I was passing out quite a lot and I would, I was using it to like check the like threshold, like heart rate zones, if that makes sense? To see like, oh, it's like, high, I'm going to sit down now kind of thing. And I feel like it's definitely helped me with that.

Interviewer - OK. Was there anything previous to the garment at all?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - OK, how...? OK, so you've described kind of how you use it. Is there any other way that you use these devices at all? Are there any new ways that you're finding of using them now that you have the visible app?

Participant - Yeah, the visible app is really helpful because it has like the pace, the pace points thing. I don't know how exactly it's calculated, but they'll like... estimate how much energy you have for the day or something, and I can kind of like budget and see which activities take up the most kind of energy. So for example, I've noticed that showering, even though it's quite a short activity, it's quite intense, so like I'll try to limit the length of time that I've been in the shower, for example. And also like I said, heavier housework is something I already need that would make it worse, but... I can actually see how much it like drains if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - It's nice to have like a number to quantify that.

Interviewer - You mentioned the placing points. Did you were Garmin watch not have the body battery function that they have?

Participant - Yes, I did, I did use that as well. It was, it was helpful, very much so.

Interviewer - OK, using it in in the same way as the pacing points?

Participant - Yeah, basically.

Interviewer - Thanks very much. When do you use this device? I'm assuming it's all the time.

Participant - Yeah, I usually don't use it at like when I sleep, just to give kind of like my wrist a break because sometimes obviously wearing something on your wrist for the whole time. Sometimes if you get like a rash or something.
So I like to not have it on when I'm sleeping, but when I wake up in the morning, I'll put it on and use it throughout the day.

Interviewer - So you don't use any of the sleep function and sleep tracking on it at all then?

Participant - I did for the Garmin but it was kind of like on and off because I started getting like a rash on my wrist because I was using it so much. So I started like not using it when I was sleeping.

Interviewer - OK. OK. Thank you very much. So I'll skip how frequently because the answer is all of the time, unless you're sleeping. So third section now, we're over halfway. Can you give me examples of activities that caused you a decision to use these devices?

Participant - So I'd say going to the gym was probably, because I started going to the gym in 2022, end of 2022 and that's when I kind of noticed it was like my first real flare up fatigue. And so that's when I started using the Garmin watch because I would notice kind of like if I was on the treadmill for example because I was a noob and I didn't know how to use anything at the gym, so I would just be like OK, treadmills easy one. Obviously that made everything a lot worse and I would kind of use the heart rate like, monitors on the treadmill and be like, oh, that doesn't seem... good.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - It was, it was a bit too high. So yeah, my mum was the one who bought the Garmin watch for me soon after that.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of times the device has alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - So I can like set for the Garmin... I used to set the heart rate zone and it wouldn't let me if I was like above that.
Yeah, I think I've put it as like 120 and yeah, would just like buzz every time it would go above that. And that was really helpful for me to kind of see okay time to sit down.
And it was... a very effective kind of mechanism for me because obviously pots is triggered by standing.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So yeah, it was it was helpful.

Interviewer - OK, just to... ask a reworded version. If I was to say that getting to the end of the day and looking back on the data and realising that you'd overstepped a lot, counted as it alerting you, is there any examples of that kind of reflection and, and realisation?

Participant - I think so, yeah, definitely. For example, like after like a travel day, things were like, one time I was on the train and I was like, "Oh, I'm going to, I'm going to stand because I don't want to ask anyone for a seat." But then I realised... actually not worth it for the payback, like the next couple of days. So yeah, the Garmin watch did say like, oh, your bod- your body battery went down by like 20 points in this half an hour.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Great. OK, so now I know, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the device?

Participant - Things like incorporating like naps into the day and like rest breaks. Didn't really used to do that before, I would just kind of go over my limit and like pay for it the next day, days.
Also I tried starting like mindfulness and stuff. It's not for me. And stretching, which I did... I was doing a lot, not so much now.
But yeah, I-I aim to do that at least once a week as well.

Interviewer - OK, so you're-you're better pacing yourself to be able to do the things that you were already doing?

Participant - Definitely.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the device?

Participant - The gym, definitely. I haven't been in a long time. I have, like, very rarely I'll, like, do a little bit in my room... but... it's rare.
But anything else? Probably... I can't really think of anything else, yeah.

Interviewer - OK, that's no problem.
Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the device?

Participant - I don't know. Maybe I would, I would probably have taken a bit too much on and kind of not really realised.
My like... sorry, brain.

Interviewer - No problem.
Take-take your time. Like I said, I show symptoms as well. Take as much time as you need.

Participant - Yeah. Thanks.
I don't know. I think I'd feel like I would probably if I didn't have it, I would just... kind of push the feeling of... push the symptoms down and kind of just ignore them until, yeah. So I feel like I would probably just end up taking a lot more activities on than I should if I didn't have it.

Interviewer - So for example, would you have kept forcing yourself to go to the gym?

Participant - Yeah, and probably force myself to like... obviously we're at Uni, go out when I didn't feel like it and like, do, see people when actually I should not for example.

Interviewer - Hmm.
OK. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this, this device?

Participant - I don't really understand the question I think.

Interviewer - OK. Can you tell me something that when it happened, you went "I am glad I chose this device. This is why I'm using this kind of device."

Participant - Things like being able to... like prevent a flare up because of pacing well.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - By using the device I've been able to prevent quite a few using like the body battery function and the heartbeat function so yeah, it's been very helpful.

Interviewer - So... it's using those moments where it alerts you to over exertion or...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - ...pre planning the day according to these pacing points.

Participant - Exactly.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK. Thank you very much. Right. Final section, just three questions in this one. What form would your ideal device take?

Participant - I'd say the watch is pretty good. I think it's convenient and it works. I'd say it would be probably nice to have the band, though made out of a material that's not kind of like irritant.
Hypoallergenic for people with comorbidities like MCAS for example is very common with pots and HEDS, so that would be nice, but I'm pretty happy with the watch at the moment.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove from either the Garmin or the visible?

Participant - I would probably remove the, on the Garmin there's like the exercise like, the 150 minutes of exercise thing, and it would just make me feel like crap because I was like obviously I'm not getting that so I could be removed that for my own sanity, yeah.

Interviewer - OK.
OK. Thank you. Anything else?

Participant - I don't think so.

Interviewer - OK. And finally, what functions would you add?

Participant - That is a good question. I probably add a sleep tracker. If it was for visible because it doesn't have that and I really like the Garmin function for that and...

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - ...possibly like a menstrual like tracker as well for visible because sometimes like for AFAB people it can be a problem as well and a trigger.
For Garmin I think already has quite a lot... I can't really think of anything.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Right. So I'll give you a quick run over of each section, just in case there's anything you'd like to add. So section one was about conditions, symptoms and how they affect your life. Section 2 was about what tools you're using, how you use them, when you use them. Section 3 was about how these tools interact with your symptoms and how you use the functions of each tool and make decisions as a result, and Section 4 was the speculative idealistic questions around what would your ideal devices be.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK? Is there anything you'd like to add?

Participant - I don't think so.

Interviewer - OK, lovely. I'll stop the recording then.

title: P18 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - It's not the first time it's happened so.

Participant - But yeah, I'm going to say, I'm going to say it again. So you've got it transcribed. I can't fall asleep in the day. There you go.

Interviewer - Yeah.
OK, no problem. So situation one sitting and reading.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Watching TV.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Sitting inactive in a public place, for example, a theatre or a meeting.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - As a passenger in a car for an hour without a brake.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Lying down to rest in the afternoon.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Sitting and talking to someone.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - Sitting quietly after lunch brackets when you've had no alcohol.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - And in a car, while stopped in traffic.

Participant - Zero.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - Sorry, I felt a bit bad about all the zeroes.

Interviewer - Again...
No, no, no. Again, don't worry about quirky answers or anything, because it's still important. The fact that a quirky answer exists is, is interesting in and of itself, so don't worry about it at all.

Participant - Yeah.
Thank you.

Interviewer - So.
So you're, you're definitely not the first to give some quirky answers, and unless you are the last interview, you will not be the last.

Participant - Thank you.

Interviewer - Right. So just going to check in again, do you need a break of any kind?

Participant - I'm OK. Thank you.

Interviewer - OK, good. Good. So this is now the main bit of the interview.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - So this is the 20 questions where you can talk as much as little as you want.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - It's broken into four sections. The first one is about your conditions and symptoms. The second is about the devices that you use and how and when you use them.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - The third one is about these two things interacting. So how device users changed your relationship with your symptoms and the 4th one is a really short...What's the word speculative thing about ideal devices? OK?

Participant - OK, cool. Cool, cool.

Interviewer - OK. So again you can come back to any question if you suddenly remember an answer for it, you can ask for repeats or rewordings or anything like that if you want. OK?

Participant - OK, cool.

Interviewer - Good. Question number one, what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - Well, I am waiting for a diagnosis, so I'm having the genetic testing of Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, but I've kind of been given a quasi like diagnosis of...joint hypermobility syndrome. So yeah, that's like my main mobility kind of quirk.

Interviewer - OK, so that's the Hyper, Hyper mobile variant of EDS then?

Participant - No.

Interviewer - Or separate things?

Participant - So basically that and they're actually separate things funnily enough. So the joint Hypermobility syndrome isn't genetic or something, whereas the hypermobility ehlers danlos is. But I'm waiting for the genetic test to all come back and Yeah. So...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Is there anything else any self diagnosis or...Things similar.

Participant - No, not really. No. That's like the main kind of overarching thing.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of this condition that you experience?

Participant - Yeah. So I am very tired. I have a lot of. Have you heard of something called like subluxations?

Interviewer - I have heard it before, but brain fog is striking.

Participant - They're bas-, they're basically partial dislocations of joints.

Interviewer - Yes, yes, yes, yes.

Participant - Yeah, which means that basically my joints come undone a lot, and there's a lot of pain associated with that. And you know, fatigue and all that kind of thing.
So yeah, I think that's kind of like the main overarching kind of...The way how it affects me, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Would you describe the fatigue and pain that you get as a result as chronic?

Participant - Oh yeah, 100, that I've been basically... So this is where it gets a bit complicated. So I had like a surgery when I was younger. I'm not going to like disclose what? But I had a surgery when I was younger.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - And I had that when I was like a teenager. And so I basically worked out, I've basically been dealing with this for like 10 years now.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - So it's been a long time. It's been well, even though like obviously I was. Either way, I was born with it, but like, it really didn't start having an impact until then. There was like little signs here and there. But you know nothing too apparent until I was like 12 years old. So it's very chronic.

Interviewer - OK, so to just while that's fresh in the conversation, I'm going to jump ahead a question. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - There's a few instances and I, because of the way things are, I have absolutely like no clue if they were associated with the condition or whether you know it was just life being life.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - But I remember one of the earliest things. So like when I was like a child, my parents couldn't, like, pick me up by the hands, you know, like, like that, because I'd always like, scream the place, down. And I think in hindsight, that was probably me having partial dislocations. I just couldn't really describe that as a child.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - There's also been signs where...
It's like for example, I think when I was like a child, I want to say like 8 years old. I landed on a trampoline a bit wrong and...you know, I-I could hardly move for like a few, few days. And I think in hindsight that was probably, you know, I don't know whether something subluxed back then, but I, you know, but like regarding the mobility issues like strongly, it's probably when I did have that surgery and...
Umm...
I was in so much pain that I couldn't even move and I before that surgery, obviously I was disabled before that surgery in another way. I kind of came out from that even more disabled. So yeah, bit of a long winded answer, sorry.

Interviewer - No, no problem. Again, I've got no problems with how however much or however little you want to talk. That's completely fine. Thank you very much.

Participant - Thank you.

Interviewer - To jump back to where we were, can you give me examples of activities this condition has prevented you being able to do?

Participant - It could even be as basic as walking, so sometimes I really struggle to [inaudible], you know, socialising. I have to really kind of think about things that I'm doing. I know it sounds a bit silly, but like if I was to, you know, meet with some friends...it's-it's like for example like I was supposed to meet with friends like last week, but I was going to London for an event the day after and I kind of realised that, you know, I was going to make myself ill if I attended the, the thing with my friends because, to be fair it wasn't even late in the day, but I wouldn't have been getting back until probably about 8:00 at night at the earliest. I just kind of thought I can't do that. Not with London the next day. So it can even be like the really kind of basics, things like that. So yeah.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Do you have extended exhaustion or post exertional malaise symptoms at all?

Participant - I don't think so. I don't think I've ever really been diagnosed with any of that kind of stuff.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. We'll skip that one.

Participant - Don't get me wrong. Like if I if I was to walk like and go out and about, I'll be bloody knackered when I come back. But I think because I have so like the extent to my disabilities and I don't mean this didn't undermine anyone else. I'm like, please don't take it as that but.

Interviewer - Hmm.
No problem at all.

Participant - I think because, like my disabilities are so like all over the place, it's really like and especially because of the subluxations, I kind of felt like a lot of the time that's taken the focus, if that makes sense. So because like if it is EDS, you know, it's very easily like all of my symptoms are very much explained by EDS and I know the doctors at the minute are very like hesitant to add additional diagnoses. I hope you don't mind me saying that, but I have always just had the fear though.

Interviewer - Hello?

Participant - Hello?
Hello?

Interviewer - Oh.
Am I back? Hello?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Right, sorry about that. But doctors are hesitant to add additional diagnosis.

Participant - Yeah. So I feel like at times when you've got like the main condition and everything can be explained via that, they tend to just ignore it. So I'll be honest, I don't know if I do or I don't or, or what. But like I do get quite tired after, you know, like walking. And I have to really be, like, very considerate about...like, you know, like shopping for example. I can't have too many bags. Like, you know, there's a lot of different kind of factors really.

Interviewer - Hmm.
Do you...is there ever a delay to the main hit of fatigue after an event? So for something like if you had a meeting and then had to go shopping later, you'd feel quite tired and then the next day it would completely floor you.

Participant - Oh my God. The next day, the next day I have really bad habits, but I don't. Well, I don't want to say it's a bad habit on me because I let my body do what it needs to do.
I came home from London and slept 12 hours and my body still hasn't caught up.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - And I'm still tired. So...

Interviewer - OK, that's...
Wonderful. I'm glad we didn't skip that question because I'll use that as an example of extended exhaustion because that sounds very familiar.

Participant - Oh really?

Interviewer - So... but yeah, depending on the medical professionals that you have, sometimes they can be very hesitant to add comorbidities. So I understand that a lot.

Participant - Yeah, no. Yeah.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting activities? If the answering of this question is one of those activities, do say so.

Participant - Umm.
I'm actually going to use that because I do sometimes struggle to...think on the spot and I struggle to...this sounds really weird and I don't know how to quite explain it. I will know an answer. And that, but me trying to communicate that answer out from the top of my head doesn't really work.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So I yeah, so that's...So like at work like I always get a bit like worried because you know, it's so academia heavy and you know, like recalling the theories. I'm really looking and none of the jobs I've ever had to do has to has to have me recall on things. But it's like I was at an event and I...said that...
Like my part, how do I explain this outside explicitly? So like I look at this like hypertension which is high blood pressure. I accidentally called it low blood pressure in front of an entire group because I was that tired. I was like, Oh my God, mortifying. Um.
But yeah, what other ways? What other ways? I notice that I tend to stick towards activities where I don't necessarily have to think on the spot, or where there's a bit more visual clues. Like I love video games, right? Especially The Sims this is...This is so weird. I'm so sorry.

Interviewer - No, there's no problem at all.

Participant - But if you think about what The Sims is right, it's like an open world tool, so you can do anything, right? And I don't have to remember what just happened. I can just do it. Whereas like, I don't know, other video games just don't really call to me because I think you've got to remember so much of the story and things like that.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - Umm.
Sorry, I can't believe I just went on that. That's...

Interviewer - No, no, there's no problem. So, so it's struggling to in, in recreation when you're meant to be relaxing, struggling to follow a consistent through line and instead wanting something that's open-ended where you can just do what you want when you want.

Participant - I think so, yeah.
Yeah, I think, yeah, that's a good way to sum it up. Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - OK.
And is there anything else you'd like to add to that?

Participant - I can't think so off the top of my head. That's a bit ironic, isn't it?

Interviewer - OK, lovely. Not the first that that's happened to I promise. Next one, can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Oh, my God. OK, this week, perfect example. I went to London. Right. Two big days when I-

Interviewer - Yeah, sorry, bear with me a second.
Sorry, I had a housemate knock on my door, please continue.

Participant - OK, so I went to London.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - And then I, obviously on the days in London, I had to like have an alarm fine, but the day I got back...

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - I slept 11 hours and then the next day.
I think I had to sleep like. No, no, no, no, no. I slept 8 hours the night before. No, no, no, I'm not working this way. I slept 8 hours the day after London, but that's 'cause I had an alarm for work, the day after I slept 12 hours.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - The day after that, I slept 10 hours and then this morning I had to set an alarm early because we had people coming round. And you know what? I'm wiped. I'm wiped. It's been... I came back on Thursday and I am wiped still.
But like mostly and and then the worst thing is right. So my normal sleeping hour. If I'm to like go to sleep normally it's between 11:00 and 12:00. Now, it's like 1, half 1.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I don't get it. I don't get it, but...

Interviewer - Despite needing sleep more, you're going to sleep later and it's having a knock on, yeah.

Participant - Yes, yes. So I'm going to have to try and set some alarms to try and get it back in schedule a little bit. And then the worst thing is right. So I keep thinking to myself, this is a really bad thing to say out loud, but I keep thinking to myself, OK, when's my next opportunity for a lie-in so I can sleep a lot. And then I'm thinking about the entire week and I'm like, you know what? I'm not going to have a chance to have a good sleep. And that's stressing me out a little because I can't fall asleep earlier and I don't know what it is about my body just will not fall asleep at the minute before 1:00.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I'm hoping tonight I change that, I have a feeling, but like the thing is as well, like I have this really bad issue and everyone notices it, right? Where, I'll be tired all day. So I'm tired now. I'll be shattered and about 7:00 or 8:00 at night my body will act like nothing's ever happened and I'm normal. And I'm not tired.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - And then so it's a pain in the arse to be quite honest.

Interviewer - Yep, that's not this is, that's...
That's quite inconvenient. So I guess the, the best way that I can describe that that sounds quite difficult to live with.

Participant - It's fine because I don't notice it's happening.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - But yeah, I don't notice. I just, I just don't really tend to notice it until like, my boyfriend's like, oh, you've been tired all day and then you're wide awake and he's like, this happens every single day. So it's not...

Interviewer - But it's quite difficult to recover from-from a big exertion like the London trip, because of this irregularity.

Participant - Oh my God, yes.
I think so. I think if I have a good night's sleep tonight and maybe the next night, I'll be grand.

Interviewer - OK, fingers crossed. Here's hoping.
Right. So that's the end of the first section. We've now got the second section about devices.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - So what device or application do you currently use for this condition and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - Do you mean digital?

Interviewer - Yes, the tracker.

Participant - OK, OK, yes. So have you heard of an app called guava? I will be so upset if you haven't.

Interviewer - I think someone mentioned it early in the study, but yes.

Participant - I love guava. Guava changed my life. Guava changed my life for the better. It was originally a-an app used...for, sorry, I'm getting warm. It was originally an app. Well, I think it was. Do you know pots?

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Postural orthostatic... Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So it was originally an app.

Interviewer - Tachycardia syndrome.

Participant -  Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So it was originally designed for them. But, but it's so, like, versatile that you can shape it. Basically. And it has correlations. So I'm like a scientist. I love a good correlation and it meant that I could actually track my subluxations this TMI. But I'm going to give it to you anyway. My subluxations to my periods.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Right. Because obviously, hormones, joint laxity, there's a lot of science behind it and you know what? It all added up. But yeah, going back to, sorry, I'm so excited about guava because I love it so-

Interviewer - Yeah.
No problem. Talk as much as you want.


Participant - I've-I've recommended it just, I recommended it to so many of my chronic illness friends because they and they turn to me and go "We love it" so, yeah. When did I start using that? It wasn't this summer. It was the summer before that. I used it for a while and then I...so the thing is right, because my disability is so...linear I think kind of, in the sense that X will cause Y. And 9 times out of 10 there's not going to be some random variable in this, it tends to be the same things causing the same problems.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - So I used that for. I want to say, let's say six months as a you know conservative, you know figure and then I-I just went, I don't need this anymore. The only thing I do kind of use it for is tracking meds.
But I don't use it anymore and I think the reason for that is-is because it got so helpful that helping me understand what variables are causing why, because I, I always had feelings, but I-I never could 100% say that it was, if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.
