Participant - So when I was having this like scientific, scientific P value stuff, I was like this. This is perfect. I love this. But because I knew then what was causing what.
I didn't necessarily feel the need to use it any further because at that point I knew what was causing what and I could kind of, you know, describe it all like for example, like my sleep. If I'm sleeping like a, you know a 12 hours a day you can guarantee the day before and the same day the pain variables are high, so it-it got to be kind of, you know, associating, it was more of an association thing than anything else, I'd say.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I can't even remember the original question I just put on a tangent about how much I love guava.

Interviewer - No, no. It was about what device or application you currently use and when you started using them.

Participant - Oh, currently.
Sugar, at the minute I'm not really using any other than really alarms for-for when, for when I need my meds because the guava app figured everything out for me.

Interviewer - OK.
Is guava the thing you used most recently?

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - When did you start and stop using that?

Participant - It was about the summer of last year and then six months for about 6 months.

Interviewer - So from the middle of last year to the end of last year, last year being 2024?

Participant - Roughly, roughly, roughly.

Interviewer - OK.
No problem. You know what? Let's carry on and let's talk about guava as the centre of the...that will be the primary device, yeah.

Participant - OK.
The centre of the universe, yes.

Interviewer - Yes.
Uh, right. OK. So how, how did you use this application?

Participant - So I, so there's, there's multiple ways that you can use guava and this is why it is so great. So first of all, you can drink your symptoms, right? Any symptom you can make your own symptoms. Perfect. Great. And then after about tracking it for a month from for a month, you can then like over time it just tends to you know get the, the P value stuff and...and helps you associate what's causing what. And you know what's associated with what. And you know those kinds of things. Secondly, there was also the alarm bit on it. I found this more useful for when I was...not having not, no, when I was...
So my repeats, it don't get me wrong it tracked them, but it wasn't necessarily useful for the tracking of those. It was more helpful for actually the tracking of the non prescription medications. So like you know your ibuprofen and your paracetamol, your codydramol.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - All that kind of stuff, that's what it was more helpful for. And then I also kind of used it for like period stuff as well because there was a big association with that because also I have endometriosis.
Long story, but essentially-

Interviewer - Oh.
So sorry, just to note, going back to the, you have endometriosis. So that includes chronic pain with that kind of thing?

Participant - Yes.
Yeah, the whole thing. Good.

Interviewer - Yeah, OK, no problem.

Participant - It's so a whole thing. Good. It's so-

Interviewer - Sorry if-if that's OK, I'll add that to the the EDS for conditions?

Participant - Yeah. Yeah, that's grand.
Grand.

Interviewer - Lovely.

Participant - Yeah.
I think that was it.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When and how frequently did you use the application?

Participant - Every day, every day.

Interviewer - All day, every day?

Participant - Because, no, I would like track it at, say, like I don't know. 7:00 at night. 9:00. I think it was actually 9:00 at night. And now I'd log all my symptoms for the day. Unless there was anything like super alarming.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - So, like, a subluxation I'd mark at the time of the subluxation, roughly just to ensure that I actually- because that was. Yeah. Because I don't know how to describe it, but I had I had multiple at one point, so it was a case of, yeah, sometimes you'd be surprised about how many subluxations I could go through in a day, honestly. But yeah, that's kind of my usage for the most part.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
Right. Can you... So this is that's the end of the second section now.

Participant - Yep.

Interviewer - So this third section is about guava and your symptoms interacting.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - *joint click* Thank you for the click. That was lovely. Ow.

Participant - Oh no.

Interviewer - Right. Can you give me examples of activities that caused your decision to use the application?

Participant - I can't remember what it was specifically because I think everything's been so linear that like you know, but what sold me, I think I saw it odd, TikTok and it was people saying, oh, how good it is because, you know, you can get those associations and blah blah blah.
The correlations and all that stuff, and I think it wasn't necessarily the symptoms that caused me to want to use it. It was actually more the functionalities that it offered, if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Hmm.
OK.
Can you give me examples of times that the application alerted you to overexertion?

Participant - I don't, I don't think there was any feature in itself that actually did that, but I think...
Umm...
I'm just trying to think that I ever use it.
Is it bad to say in that sense we used it more of a validation tool than an alertion tool?

Interviewer - Not at all.

Participant - I think...

Interviewer - If I was to reword the question to include look-, checking it at the end of the day and realising you'd overextended throughout the day if I was to include that within the alerting you to overexertion...

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Would it then apply?

Participant - Mm-hmm.
Probably because I just, I think what used to happen, I used to be like, oh, let's say I was having a bad chronic fatigue day and then I'd look at, you know, like how many steps I did the day before or what was my heart rate that you know, overall in the day.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I used it more that way if that makes sense.

Interviewer - Yeah.
OK. Is there any times when, when you were using it that way that you were alerted to over exertion the day before or earlier in the day or anything like that?

Participant - Oh yeah, yeah, I could like, there's like a date scale kind of thing. So I could, you know, see, you know, for example like, I know that my body's working hard when my heart rate's high. So, you know, attract it to, you know...

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Let's say I manage your level to my heart rate that day or, you know over two day period and it would tell me that you know the-the fatigue is much higher on those days and other days if that makes sense.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.
I've lost my place here. Can you give me examples of activities you started after using the, the application please?

Participant - Umm.
I don't think there's any, to be honest. I don't....don't think. Yeah.

Interviewer - That's completely fine. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of regular activities you stopped participating in as a result of using the application?

Participant - I didn't stop participating in anything. I just made sure to pace myself a bit more like based on the context clues I already knew in the correlations that I knew. That's more how I, how I would say that, yeah.

Interviewer - OK.
So it was a confirmation and pacing tool instead of it at all, at any point, making you decide to drop an activity completely?

Participant - Yeah, but I-I think to be honest, the kind of person I am, I don't like to drop things, you know, I... But yeah, I know that's how I used it more as that kind of, you know, pacing if anything.

Interviewer - Yeah.
OK, no problem. Thank you very much. Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the the, the, the application?

Participant - Sorry can you repeat that?

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities you might have started were you not using the application? So is there anything you would have tried if you didn't have access to that data?

Participant - Umm. Not really. It's hard to say because I feel like within myself and by using the app I've become so aware of my health and you know what triggers it and you know what I can do to kind of, you know, better myself.
But like before that, I think that I tried like electrolyte tablets. I was just constantly in a bit of a rest. But now like I just try and listen to my body more and based on the context clues my body's giving me nothing. That's what I tend to do so...
Yeah. No, I don't think there's anything that anything else I'd really try, not from memory at least, I'll be honest.

Interviewer - OK, there's no problem at all. Thank you. Last question of this section. Can you give me examples of events that demonstrate your decision to choose this application?

Participant - I can't think of anything...

Interviewer - Do you want me to reword it?

Participant - Maybe.
Please. Thank you.

Interviewer - Yeah? OK. Is there anything that happened or that...didn't happen that you prevented from happening. That made you think "I'm glad I chose this app. That's why I'm using it."

Participant - I think in some ways it was kind of helpful in like medical contexts because, you know, I kind of, I always go in to like these appointments and say, hey, I did a like I did, you know, a bachelor's in psychology.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - I-I know science right? And you know, they take me on as a bit of a like I'd say that they probably see no, forgive me, but I think they do see me as a bit more conscious of other patients.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - And, in some ways...
But I think that because of, I had the ability to take guava, not literally. I didn't show them. Hey, here's my phone. Read this. But I said, you know, based on like the associations of, you know, the app and... I didn't say the app because I didn't want to be like fobbed off. I'll be honest, but I've kind of it helped me go, "OK, so you know, on days where my heart rate's higher, I think that means I'm, you know, working a lot harder. And, you know, in turn, I think my fatigue's a lot worse." So by using those correlations that I was able to communicate, OK, with medical professionals about my symptoms, it was the way I was also able to notice that my subluxations were way worse around my periods. Or, you know when my hormones were high because, like, endometriosis nightmare.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I will be honest. It was one of the...So there was like a medication that I was on, like a hormonal medication I was on and they gave me quite a high dose and I realised that was a big cause of subluxations. And I do think if it wasn't for the app, I felt like I knew it. But like, there's a difference between having a correlation tell at you.
And you're thinking it right, and I actually ended up going off that medication. It wasn't just for that. But, you know, it was. It was a part of a reason. And I'm glad that I did because I don't have them half as much now.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - But it was like the little things like that. So yeah.

Interviewer - So it's like a confirmation tool. It's something to present a more solid grounding for what you were observing, and it helps you communicating with medical professionals because of it.

Participant - I think...
I think so, yeah.

Interviewer - OK, no problem. Thank you very much. So final section, this is only three questions.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - It's a bit speculative. You can let your imagination go crazy if you want or you can stay grounded.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - What form would your ideal device or application take?

Participant - Umm.
I think obviously mobile based is that kind of what you're asking or do you mean like what kind of features or?

Interviewer - Well, what physical form do you want it in that? Do you want it to be an application, wrist worn neck worn, ear worn?

Participant - Well, I do use a smart watch a lot of the time, so you know where there's some syncing with that, it'd be great. I know that guava and I'll keep going a little bit guava, but guava does allow you to sync in with like Samsung Health, so it can use like the database. So the heart, the steps, the even like the calories burn you know.
All the crazy things it can take from that and then input it in that, which is really helpful because it kind of does a lot of the hard work for you and I think something like that is generally very helpful because it just means you've got less things going on. So obviously, I'm not manually inputting what's found on here.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - I think, yeah. I mean, I would not even, no. I know it sounds a bit bad, but like I felt like because obviously I'm quite young.
And I felt like there's that internal level of ableism in some way of like you, you know, like being a younger person, especially, you know what? This is a bit more discreet and a bit more kind of socially acceptable. I hate to use those words. I really do hate to use them.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - But you know, I do think there is probably that element. So you know things geared towards younger people would probably have to have the consideration of, "hey, what the what are the latest trends?" I know they have like them, them rings at the minute, don't they? They're expensive. They're expensive for a bloody ring.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - But like even things like that, you know. A bit less obvious and like something on the neck.

Interviewer - Something to disguise it. So if-if-if something on the neck were to be considered for you, it would have to be disguised as, say, a necklace or something. It would have to be disguised as another bit of-

Participant - Yeah.
Maybe, but like-

Interviewer - As, as a different accessory or something?

Participant - Probably, probably.

Interviewer - Yeah.
OK. Thank you very much. What functions would you remove from devices that you are aware of?

Participant - I don't really have any because guava was that open that, don't really have anything.

Interviewer - OK.
Yep, no problem. Thank you. What functions would you add?

Participant - Again, I don't really have anything for that one, because guava literally had everything. It was so great. I can't remember thinking of anything off the bat of like things that I, I think the only thing is for guava like because I've recommended it to like a lot of friends and I'd say I probably felt the same in the beginning, is that there's you have, there's so much like input at the beginning. So like, you have to like, write all your own symptoms. You have to write all your own medications. I know that like because guava is, is US based, right. And I know that the US is a lot more innovative in you know healthcare and, you know, like digital health, a lot more. Well, it seems that way anyway. From like what I see on online, but I think like they can sync things like you know with their providers of you know, like what medications you're on and you can put like it's...imported from that into the guava app, but like here I'll be honest, I haven't tried it, so I'm saying this and I might be very wrong, but I think I'm not used to like the UK being very like...
What's the opposite of innovative like, just not innovative?

Interviewer - Stagnant?

Participant - Pretty much that. I mean, I don't think I've even looked at it because I just thought automatically that it wouldn't apply. And I think because it is a lot of like US terminology like providers, I don't know, it feels a lot more American.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Where I was like, I don't know if that makes sense, but just...

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - You know, I feel like if you're to design something for the UK, it has to be co-produced for one to make sure that it fits the needs of everyone.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - But I think like making sure that you have the UK terminology, which I know sounds a bit silly on like as I'm saying it, but like just even the difference between you know what we'd say, like they say providers, we just say, you know, from your GP or, you know, they say Primary Health care and don't get me wrong, we use Primary Health care, but we tend to use like, you know, GP.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - So yeah, I think that's the only real thing I can think of why I criticise guava because I love it so much.

Interviewer - You did mention earlier that it's got no way to alert you to over exertion at the time that it was happening. Is that a feature that you would like to be added just since you've mentioned it earlier?

Participant - How do I say this without sounding? I don't want to come across as like internally ableist or anything?

Interviewer - No, that's no problem.

Participant - But I, I have this thing where for me I don't think I'd necessarily want to be alerted at the time.

Interviewer - Mm-hmm.

Participant - Because I think...
This is a really weird way to describe it. Have you got like a smart watch? Like for example just so I can put this into or, you know, like, you know, like when your phone's on like, vibrate or something and you're, like, waiting for, like, you know, an...

Interviewer - Yeah, you don't want to be constantly annoyed by it.


Participant - It's not even that but like, you're waiting for that important email, you're wondering like when it's gonna come through. Like y'know and you kind of start to Hmmmm, like, errrr, I'm waiting for it. I don't feel like amazing. When's it gonna go off? I'm expecting it to go off. That's, you know, a bit weird that it's not going off, but I think but like even just the way my disability presents, I know when something's going on, I don't, I don't know. Like, I'll be honest, I knew after London I was going to be shattered. And it sounds bad, but I paid the consequences of my own actions in that sense.

Interviewer - Hmm.
Yeah.

Participant - And I-I feel bad saying it, but I don't know how much an app would have helped me on that day because I already knew I already knew that I was going to be shattered for like days on like so...

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Hopefully that doesn't sound really rude because I feel like it kind of does.

Interviewer - No, no, not at all. So to...
So to repeat, repeat it, reword it to make sure I've understood. For you specifically, having notifications as and when isn't as useful because you already know because you know enough about your...

Participant - Pretty much.

Interviewer - ...conditions and how they present that... that's just superfluous information. That's extra. That's unneeded.

Participant - Yeah, pretty much.

Interviewer - Yeah. OK, no problem. OK. So I'll give you a quick overview of all the questions that we've had in case there's anything you want to add.

Participant - OK.

Interviewer - Like I said, I'm going to, if it's OK with you I'll add endometriosis to the chronic mobility conditions, if that's OK?

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. So section one was about your conditions and the symptoms and the onset and all of that. Section 2 was about the device or application when you used it, how you used it, etc.

Participant - Mm-hmm.

Interviewer - Section 3 was how these two things interacted, how decisions that you were making were changing, or how the device was intervening in your life and how you were acting on, on the data you were given.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - And section #4 is the speculative ideal section.

Participant - Mm-hmm, yeah.

Interviewer - So is there anything you'd like to add?

Participant - I don't think so, no.

Interviewer - OK, perfect. I'll stop the recording then.

title: P19 interview transcript
text:
Interviewer - And OK, so that's started.
So, this is the main body. This is 20 questions split into four sections. The first section is about your conditions. The second section is about any devices or applications that you use. The third section is the how those two things interact.
And the 4th section is a short little speculative section.
So again, you can say this doesn't apply to me or none or anything like that if you'd like, and feel free to go back to any previous question if something pops into your head at any time. You don't have to answer these in order or in any particular way.
OK?

Participant - Sure.

Interviewer - Lovely. So what chronic mobility conditions do you have?

Participant - I would say my primary condition is Hypermobile Ehlers Danlos and concurrent with that are postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, functional digestive disorders, and asthma and sort of hay fever, I guess. But I don't know if that, it's not really a mobility condition, but it does stop me from going out if it's so bad that like my head is really stuffy and I just can't sit up.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Can you describe to me the symptoms of these conditions that you experience?

Participant - Yeah, the Ehlers Danlos symptoms are primarily very consistent pain in joints and muscles, frequent spontaneous dislocations of joints...
Um...
Frequent organ movement, which has led to some weird emergency surgery.
And that also contributes to the digestive dysfunction and...
Yeah, I mean the others are, are POTS I guess. The main symptoms of that are racing heart rate, light-headedness, vision goes dark, can't manage my temperature or thirst and hunger signals or....blood flow. I-I quite often get like, tinnitus for example or, or vision loss and I think like oh wait, I have to put my neck in the right position because it's I've accidentally kinked a, a nerve or a vein or something and...just not getting blood where it needs to go.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - Sure.

Interviewer - Can you give me examples of activities these conditions have prevented you being able to do?

Participant - Standing up. So I can walk because the, the act of walking by the constant sort of alternation of which muscles are flexing pushes the blood around in a sort of slightly external mechanical way and I can walk fairly well. I can walk like a kilometer or two at a time, but I can't stand still because as soon as I'm not using my muscles to push the blood around, it all just falls to my feet and I get really dizzy and, and tip over sideways. So yeah, standing is...uh, tricky, which is common in daily life than you might expect, like queuing at shops or uh, waiting to cross a road or something like that. There's just a lot of standing and so I'm, I'm consistently like leaning against things in public and I look really casual and it's slightly off-putting thing. I also just sit down randomly on the floor out in public, and that really puts people off.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. When is the earliest instance you can recall of your conditions inhibiting your mobility?

Participant - Childhood. I definitely was not interested in any of the kinds of group activity, sports that involved waiting for other things to happen and then reacting very quickly. So you know, like football or basketball or whatever, you have to like to sort of pay attention to a lot of other people and then react quickly. That does not work for me. So yeah, childhood, a lot of pain, a lot of trouble standing and waiting and paying attention.
Um.
Yeah.
First time I remember thinking I, I just don't want to participate because it it's just so much work.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Um.
So because of Ehlers Danlos, because it can um...exhibit symptoms in different ways within different people, I'm going to ask all of these optional questions...

Participant - Sure.

Interviewer - ...just to see if these apply to you.

Participant - Yep.


Interviewer - Extended exhaustion, something like PEM. Does that apply to you at all? Post exertional malaise?

Participant - Um.
Not especially..

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - ...Possibly because I don't actually do anything to motivate.

Interviewer - Too much, too much pacing.

Participant - No, I wouldn't say it's a, I don't notice it being a common issue.

Interviewer - OK, um, brain fog.

Participant - Yes.

Interviewer - OK, can you give me examples of brain fog inhibiting your activities? If answering this question is being inhibited by brain fog, please say.

Participant - Brain fog definitely leads me to feel like I'm running a couple of seconds behind a conversation. So really frequently I will just not participate in conversations. I'll just sort of...be a weird little lurker. And it's only when people ask me a question directly and then I have to do a slightly deer in the headlight look while I'm thinking, like replaying it a little bit to pay attention to what was going on.
So yeah, it's definitely just leads me to be more of an observer than a participant in life.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Um, disruptions to your sleep schedule, is that applicable to you? Yeah.

Participant - Absolutely.

Interviewer - OK, can you give me examples of disruptions to your sleep schedule making your other symptoms worse?

Participant - Yes, I frequently struggle to get to sleep because of pain. The issue being when I'm trying to relax my muscles, the joints dislocate because the, the tendons don't hold them together and it's actually my muscle activity that holds them together.
And obviously having tense muscles is not great for sleep. It, it doesn't lead one to feel sleepy to be also sort of tensed. So getting to sleep is really hard because of this condition. And then the POTS will sometimes randomly misfire my adrenaline in, in the middle of the night and I'll wake up in a panic with like... or my heart rate alarm will wake me up in the middle of the night because my heart rate will have climbed suddenly to like 120 beats per minute while I'm sleeping. And that's...that really means that if I do ever get to sleep, I struggle to stay asleep for very long, yeah.
Fun stuff.

Interviewer - OK. And obviously this has a...a knock on effect with your other symptoms throughout the day then?

Participant - Yeah, if certainly if I get a dislocation...overnight that will limit my ability to use that joint during the day and then if I just don't get any sleep overnight, the-the brain fog and the vision fuzziness and the pain and irritability and all the other things will just be worse than they would if I had not had trouble getting to sleep or, or had been woken up during the night, so...

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of the first section. So we're now on to devices and applications.

Participant - Sure.

Interviewer - What device or application do you currently use for these conditions and when did you start using it or them?

Participant - Uh, I've got a Garmin smartwatch that um will set will tell me if my heart rate has climbed...
hold on...

Interviewer - Bless you.

Participant - Thank you. If my heart rate has climbed more than 30 beats per minute or has gone over 100 for 10 minutes when I'm not doing physical activity, that explains the increase. So it's a slightly sophisticated system, which is great because it will happen during the day or overnight, and it usually signals to me that my body is having trouble moving my blood around appropriately and then my and then my autonomic nervous system is panicking as a result.
So if I'm awake, the solution is pretty easy. I drink some water, I take an electrolyte supplement of some kind. I might lay on the floor with my feet elevated and do some breathing exercises. If I'm asleep, I will probably have to take a beta blocker because I'm already laying down.
But I might also drink water and take some electrolytes, or put on some compression socks or something like that to sort of force my blood to be where it ought to be.
I got another device. It's not a wearable. I don't know if you're interested. This is a breath analyzer and it tells me whether the things that I have eaten are digesting appropriately or hanging around, not being digested appropriately. So it senses hydrogen and methane in my breath, and I... it's kind of a pain to take these little breath samples all the time, so I sort of....
once every year, every other year or something will do a period of several weeks in which I check my breath constantly just to make sure that I have not developed new digestive problems.

Interviewer - Mhm. But I'm assuming you break out of that pattern if you feel like something is wrong, but otherwise it's a check every year or so.

Participant - Yeah, I'm, I'm using it right now because I have noticed a big change and I feel like, yeah, either, I've, either all of the foods that I used to think were safe foods have changed their recipes and I need to pay more attention.
Or I'm now allergic to some-, or I'm now having problems digesting something that I used to not have problems with because a, a bunch of symptoms have developed. Could be unrelated to actually the, the actual digestion is just stress or lack of sleep or something else that's... causing the, the malfunction, but yeah, it's it, I'm particularly using it at the moment because I have noticed a problem. I'm responding to that.

Interviewer - OK. Um, do you have a brand name on that at all?

Participant - That is aire AIRE by a company called Food Marble.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Do you have you used any tools previous to these?

Participant - I had a, a different style of watch earlier, but it was still a Garmin sort of fitness tracker smartwatch.
Yeah, I have a TENS machine. I don't know if you're interested in that as a device. That one is quite useful for controlling pain, but it's...

Interviewer - Mm.
Not quite, unfortunately. I think that's medically issued, isn't it?

Participant - No, I just bought that from Boots.

Interviewer - Oh, OK.

Participant -Yeah, I mean, yeah, it's quite...

Interviewer - It's not come up before, but sure, I'll make a note of that if you can just buy one from Boots.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, they're, they're, they've come down in price and you can get ones that like stick on that don't have wires now. So they're potentially more comfortable to wear like under clothes or all day or something. So yeah, the TENS technology has really come on recently.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much.

Participant - Yeah, yeah, definitely use, use that for pain management, especially for the kinds of joints that are hard to put physical supports and braces on. Like neck is quite a tricky one or shoulder or something like that. So I'll often use the TENS machine on those joints.

Interviewer - Hmm. And that's something you actively use, yeah?

Participant - Yeah, yeah.

Interviewer - OK. So if, if that's relevant to any of the answers, then by all means just make a mention of it. So how do you use these devices? If there's any details you've, you've missed out on?

Participant - Um.
I check my Garmin app... Well, I check the watch every morning. It gives me what it calls a morning report and that gives me an estimate of how many hours I, of sleep I got and what my current...it calls a body battery, but it's sort of like, like spoon theory sort of thing.

Interviewer - Mhm.

Participant - It gives me an estimate of what my spoons are at the moment based on, I think, heart rate variability and amount of sleep that I got. And so I will check that in the morning, sort of just on my watch.
And then I might check it again throughout the day if I'm feeling a lot of symptoms, I might look at the, the app to see more in detail is, is something, you know, is my heart rate behaving more erratically than normal or is it just average higher than normal? Something like that?

Interviewer - Mm.

Participant - To try and see if there's anything I can pinpoint about that. I will use the TENS machine as needed for pain. And yeah, if I'm in a testing phase with the, the breath tester, then I just try and test at least once an hour I think.
Um.
But that it's not necessarily reactive to the conditions, it's just a trying to get a, a full day tracking of, of what different kinds of digestion levels are doing.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So you mentioned with the breath tester and with when you're checking the watch, but when do you use everything else? Obviously you said that TENS machine is, is responsive to pain.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Um, do you do you check the, the application or the watch at the end of the day or?

Participant - Uh, if...

Interviewer - Or is it only if it alerts you?

Participant - I would say.
I don't necessarily check my, the, the Garmin application or watch at the end of the day, unless I've had a problematic day, or I know that tomorrow is going to be very busy. I might check, you know, what my spoon levels are to maybe go to bed early or to take some extra sort of muscle relaxants or something like that.

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - If I have had a high symptom day, I'll check it probably throughout the day, trying to keep more on top of it. And the yeah, the breath tester I do review at the end of the day, but again, that's only sort of during periods where I'm tracking it, in which I'm more actively engaged with the app and the device. Yeah, in, in a period where I'm not actively tracking, I don't think I'd check the app at all. So it's, it's really just sort of a...three or four weeks that ago, maybe 5 if, if I'm trying to iron out a really new problem that I'm having trouble pinning down.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. Just a quick question about the, the Garmin.

Participant - Sure.

Does the body battery function only show in the morning or can you keep track of it throughout the day?

Participant - No, you can look at it on the app at any point, but it doesn't, as far as I know, send you warnings about body battery throughout the day. I could...there may be a function that I haven't found that could...there's some you can get like third party apps installed that will link to the Garmin function, so I probably could get notifications if my battery goes too low, but I also don't want to be constantly getting notifications on days where it's already difficult, so um.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Yeah, bit complicated, that one.

Interviewer - OK. Thank you very much. So that's the end of the second section.

Participant - Sure.

So this third one is now about everything interacting with each other and how you're, you are reacting to that as well.

Participant - Sure.

Can you give me examples of activities that caused you a decision to use the devices?

Participant - Um.
Activities that caused me to decide to use the devices, like the first time or in a, a given day why I might check the app or check take a, a breath test or something like that?

Interviewer - If you'd like to answer both and just split up the answers, then by all means do.

Participant - Mm.
I'd say originally I started sort of using a, a constant heart rate tracker because I had a really serious vitamin B12 deficiency. This was back in 2010 or 2011, so quite a long time ago.
And I was sort of sleeping for 14 hours a day, and when I finally got treatment for that and was starting to sleep a more normal time, I would still feel really...out of sync, like I couldn't remember how much I'd been sleeping or, or how long I'd been awake or something like that. So I got an app that sort of tracked sleep really actively and it also had a heart rate monitor and I so I started getting these heart rate alerts on through that app. And I thought, oh gosh, I hadn't even noticed that my heart rate was doing this weird thing. Let me look more into that. So now I treat the heart rate alerts much more seriously. I've got a plan for how to respond to them, you know, with the, the drinking or the elevating my feet or laying down or something like that. And it's, it's getting much better. So originally an effort to know how much I was actually sleeping has led me to identifying another problem and then dealing with that much more effectively.
Um, similarly the uh...
Breath testing device. I just sort of had like lifelong bad digestion, um, and kind of didn't know what to do about it. Doctors are like, well, you've got no parasites, just, you know, consider your fiber intake or something, you know, really unhelpful advice.
And so, yeah, I found... my partner ended up going on a low FODMAP diet for digestive distress as well. And so we both did because it made sense if we're eating together all the time to eat the same things and I felt loads better.
And my partner did not feel any particular benefit from the diet, but I, I started looking into FODMAP diets and disordered functional sort of digestive issues and that led me to a bunch of products and sort of research on FODMAP diets and... how to identify which ones are problems and how to identify potential solutions or uh thresholds and different kinds of things. And this this app and device are specifically made for identifying fod maps and how the body reacts and digests them differently.

Interviewer - I've just had a quick Google and I'm seeing FODMAP is all, all, all about IBS and similar things.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Um.

Participant - Yeah, it's like fructose, oligosaccharide, something or other. They're all various kinds of sugar chains that people cannot digest. Can most people can digest most of them fairly well, but things like lactose or gluten...

Interviewer - OK.

Participant - ..But also beans. Uh, onions. It's a big one. Cauliflower. Like there's some that most people are fine with, but we, we all agree that everyone gets a bit farty after eating too many beans, and it's, it's that same effect, but for someone...who's really sensitive to them, it's catastrophic and it's much more than just beans.

Interviewer - Yeah.
It's much more a health problem than a making a problem for everyone else through wind.

Participant - Yeah.

Interviewer - Yeah.

Participant - Yeah, it looks like because of the Ehlers-danlos syndrome, so all of the different valves that close off parts of the digestive system from others are just a little bit like wishy washy. So food is moving through it at a pace that isn't appropriate.

Interviewer - Hmm.

Participant - So things are moving forward through the system before they're properly ready to get there, and that means that they're not...behaving as they should. And that's potentially why, why I or someone like me is has trouble with some of these foods is that they're just moving forward, they're charging ahead too soon.

Interviewer - Not, not getting fully processed, as it were?
